title: Syllabus
text:
Course Description and Objectives

This Honors seminar teaches you to design, analyze, verify, and defend your own empirical research, with no strong background in quantitative methods or computing required. Over one semester you will carry a single project from curiosity to a publicly defended claim. You will find a real gap in a field you care about, scope it into a researchable problem, and ask answerable questions. You will classify each question on the inquiry compass (its kind, descriptive vs causal, and its reach, the data at hand vs a population beyond it vs cases not yet seen) and match it to a design from the research library (observational or experimental, descriptive or causal, with prediction as its own objective). You will declare that design, diagnose it by simulation, redesign it, gather and verify evidence, attack your own analysis, and communicate a claim with its uncertainty and limitations in written and oral form.
Throughout, you will use AI at high intensity and under tight control. The course’s defining message is simple:

AI is your arm and your research assistant — not your brain. AI may propose. The researcher must verify. The evidence must support.

Course Website: https://davi-moreira.github.io/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/

title: Syllabus
text:
Learning Outcomes

By the conclusion of this course, you will be able to:

Find a gap and scope a problem. Locate a meaningful gap in a field you care about and turn it into a well-scoped, researchable problem.
Ask answerable questions and classify them on the inquiry compass. Turn a problem into questions evidence can address, and place each on the compass by kind (descriptive vs causal) and reach (data at hand, a population beyond it, or cases not yet seen), justifying the classification and its limits without heavy mathematics.
Declare a research design on the library pathways. Match a classified question to a design (observational or experimental, descriptive or causal, with prediction treated as its own objective) and declare it with the MIDA framework: model, inquiry, data strategy, answer strategy.
Diagnose and redesign before collecting data. Simulate a declared design, read its diagnosis, and redesign it so it can actually answer the question.
Gather and analyze evidence responsibly with AI. Direct AI tools to locate sources, operationalize concepts, and analyze evidence, and verify every result and citation against real, retrievable evidence while documenting each decision.
Attack your own analysis. Stress-test a finding with robustness checks and adversarial red-teaming, and reproduce a peer’s package in a replication and red-team review.
Communicate under uncertainty, in writing and out loud. Present a claim with its boundary, uncertainty, and limitations as a poster at the Purdue Fall Undergraduate Research Expo, a written research note, and a public evidence defense.
Manage AI as a directed research assistant. Run the Specify, Delegate, Interrogate, Inspect, Verify, Document, Defend workflow, coordinate multiple AI roles, and keep a complete AI Research Ledger, because AI is your arm and your research assistant, not your brain.

title: Syllabus
text:
How the Course Runs
The course meets Monday, Wednesday, and Friday in person, in 50-minute sessions, and each day of the week has a fixed job.

Monday and Wednesday are flipped lectures led by a rotating Student Research Lead (SRL). From Week 2 on, one of you runs each lecture as a Socratic investigation, not a slide summary: pose a puzzle, get the room to commit to an answer before opening any AI tool, direct the AI investigation, and defend a decision at the end. The instructor monitors for accuracy and formalizes the concept. You will lead about five times across the semester, and your leading is graded.
Friday is a project studio. No new topic content is introduced on Fridays. Each studio runs the same loop: a short research stand-up, a milestone sprint kicked off from that week’s Brightspace brief and worked with AI support, a peer and AI red-team review, and a submit step where you update your dossier and AI Research Ledger. The milestone you have been building all week is submitted at the close.
Two meetings are asynchronous online modules: one in mid-October (Friday, October 2) and one during Thanksgiving week (Monday, November 23, the replication and red-team module). Each async module is a self-contained unit with its own graded artifact.
There is no class the day after the Expo (Wednesday, November 18); the Expo presentation on Tuesday, November 17 stands in its place.


The Student Research Lead
Leading a lecture is a graded performance and a rehearsal of exactly what the Expo and the final defense will ask of you. Slots are assigned by rotation across five seats, so each of you leads roughly five times. You receive your lead’s SRL page five days ahead and submit a preparation script two days ahead for instructor review, then run the room live against a published rubric. The full guidance, templates, and rubric are provided in the course’s Student Research Lead materials, and your dates and formats live on the Schedule page.

title: Syllabus
text:
Assessments

There is no conventional midterm or final exam. The course assesses the research chain itself: a semester-long individual research project carried through sixteen milestones (M0–M15), presented publicly at the Purdue Fall Undergraduate Research Expo (Tuesday, November 17 — required), and closed with a public evidence defense and a reproducible final research chapter and AI-management portfolio. Grading rewards correctness, transparency, reproducibility, question-design alignment, and responsible interpretation, never coding elegance (no computing background is assumed; all code is provided).
Weights are a proposal subject to final confirmation.

Revision policy: feedback returns within 3 days of each milestone; most milestones accept a revised version within 7 days of feedback for up to half the lost points (the revising is part of the craft being graded). Terminal artifacts have no revision window: the M10 final poster (Friday, November 6, 5:00 PM), the M15 final research chapter and AI-management portfolio (Friday, December 11), and the live Expo presentation (Tuesday, November 17, a graded component of M12). For these, the deadline governs.
Late policy: −10% per day, up to 3 days, then not accepted (documented emergencies handled individually per Purdue policy).
Grading scale: A ≥ 93, A− ≥ 90, B+ ≥ 87, B ≥ 83, B− ≥ 80, C+ ≥ 77, C ≥ 73, C− ≥ 70, D ≥ 60, F < 60 (no curve).

The Milestone Chain
You carry one individual research question across the whole semester. Every milestone is a graded, submittable artifact that follows a develop in class, present, submit, revise cadence, so no deliverable is ever built alone or at the last minute. Each one updates your cumulative Research Project Dossier and appends an AI Research Ledger entry. Milestone briefs and rubrics are posted on Brightspace, one page per milestone.

Poster and Conference Policy
Presenting at the Purdue Fall Undergraduate Research Expo (Tuesday, November 17) is a required, graded component of the course. Your poster is locked for printing on Friday, November 6 at 5:00 PM (M10) and cannot be revised after that time. At the Expo you present your own poster and evaluate at least three colleagues’ posters using the course criticism instrument. The Expo presentation is a live event: only a documented emergency qualifies for a make-up arrangement, handled individually per Honors College norms.


Reproducibility Policy
Every analysis ships as a clean-run Colab package that reproduces from a fresh runtime with the course seed (SEED = 464), so the same inputs yield the same outputs on any machine. At the replication and red-team milestone (M13), you reproduce a peer’s anonymized package and attest whether its headline result runs, then report where it is fragile. Your project closes as a reproducibility capsule (M14): the notebook, data, and documentation a stranger would need to rebuild your claim.


Grade Challenges
Students who wish to challenge a grade must do so within 3 calendar days of the grade’s release. An exception applies for the last week of class, when assignments must be challenged within 1 calendar day to ensure timely computation of final course grades. Grade challenges must be grounded in legitimate disputes over research-methods principles or grading accuracy. Challenges based on post-hoc legalistic arguments or subjective dissatisfaction will not be considered.
To challenge an assignment score:

Review posted solutions / rubrics thoroughly.
If you still believe an error has been made, email the instructor with:

Course name, section, and date/time
Your name and Student ID
Assignment number / title
Specific deduction being challenged
Reason for the challenge, with reference to the solutions or grading rubric



Grades will not be discussed in class — before, during, or after class. Please use office hours for questions related to course content or assignment clarification. After the challenge period, all grades are final and cannot be revised further for purposes of calculating final course grades.

title: Syllabus
text:
Course Policies and Additional Details

AI Policy
This course explicitly teaches responsible, documented AI use. You are expected to use AI at high intensity, under tight control, and always as your arm and your research assistant, not your brain.
The discipline. Every use of AI in this course follows the full workflow Specify, Delegate, Interrogate, Inspect, Verify, Document, Defend (the everyday shorthand is Ask, Verify, Document). You specify the task precisely, delegate it to the tool, interrogate and inspect what comes back, verify it against real evidence, document what you decided, and defend the result as your own.
The AI Research Ledger. Every deliverable and every notebook session appends an entry to your AI Research Ledger, with eight fields: task delegated, tool used, prompt, output summary, decision, verification method, remaining concern, and responsible researcher. A missing ledger entry returns the submission.
Verify everything. AI tools routinely fabricate citations, sources, and facts. Every claim and every source you submit must be independently located and confirmed to exist and to say what you claim it says. Any errors or omissions from your use of an AI tool are your responsibility. Undisclosed or unverified AI output is an academic-integrity violation.
What is yours alone. Some decisions are never delegated to an AI tool. You, and only you, are responsible for:

selecting the research problem and question;
declaring the research design and target population;
choosing measurements and evaluating data quality;
ethical judgments;
deciding which claims the evidence justifies;
acknowledging uncertainty and limitations;
defending the project publicly.

Approved tools. Google Gemini is the primary in-notebook research assistant. Purdue GenAI Studio supplies reviewer roles you must consult at four milestones (M5, M7, M9, M13). Microsoft Copilot and other general-purpose tools are permitted with disclosure. You are not permitted to use AI tools during any closed assessment where its use is prohibited. Low-effort prompts produce low-quality output; refine your prompts using the course concepts.


Netiquette Guidelines

Be respectful and clear in communication; contribute constructively to in-class discussion and peer critique.
In any online session, join with your full name and mute when not speaking.



Accessibility, Accommodations, and Student Well-Being

Accessibility. Purdue University strives to make learning experiences as accessible as possible. If you anticipate or experience physical or academic barriers based on disability, please let me know so we can discuss options. You are also encouraged to contact the Disability Resource Center: drc@purdue.edu or 765-494-1247.
CAPS. Purdue University is committed to advancing student mental health and well-being. If you or someone you know is feeling overwhelmed, depressed, or in need of support, services are available. Contact Counseling and Psychological Services (CAPS) at 765-494-6995 and http://www.purdue.edu/caps/ during and after hours, on weekends and holidays, or through counselors located in the Purdue University Student Health Center (PUSH) during business hours.
Basic Needs Security. Contact ODOS if you are facing food or housing insecurity; no appointment needed.
Non-Discrimination Statement. Please refer to the Nondiscrimination Statement on Brightspace.
Emergency Situations. Please refer to the Emergency Preparedness page on Brightspace.



Additional Information
Refer to Brightspace for deadlines, academic integrity policies, accommodations, CAPS information, and non-discrimination statements. Registrar add/drop/modify deadlines: http://www.purdue.edu/Registrar/.


Subject to Change Policy
While I will try to adhere to the course schedule as much as possible, I also want to adapt to your learning pace and style. The syllabus and course plan may change during the term.

title: 36. Conflicting Agents and Human Escalation
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 36. Conflicting Agents and Human Escalation
text:
Why this decision matters

“Don’t tell me your reviewers agreed. Tell me whether they could have agreed for the same wrong reason. Agreement is the easiest thing in the world to manufacture, and the most dangerous to trust.” — a skeptical peer at your practice defense

By now you can run a small team of AI roles on your work. The seductive next thought is that a team is safer than one assistant, so three roles blessing your chapter must mean three confirmations. It does not. Three roles built the same way, reading the same draft with the same instructions, tend to make the same mistake, so their agreement is one error repeated three times. This chapter is about seeing through that, and about the moment you stop counting votes and decide for yourself.

title: 36. Conflicting Agents and Human Escalation
text:
The concept
When your roles do not match, the first move is not to pick a winner. It is reconciling conflicting outputs: deciding what to believe by first diagnosing why the roles differ. Example: before you side with the reviewer over the methodologist, you ask what would make each of them right. There are three cases, and they call for opposite responses.
Real disagreement is when the roles genuinely read the evidence differently. Example: your reviewer calls a sentence clear and strong while your methodologist flags that same sentence as reaching past your data. This is the good case. The clash points straight at the claim you need to check.
Correlated error is when the roles agree, but on the same wrong assumption, so the agreement proves nothing. Example: three reviewers built from the same base model all miss the same flaw because they share the blind spot. The question to ask is simple: could they be wrong the same way? If yes, their consensus is one signal, not three.
False consensus is when the roles agree only because your prompt framed the task so they had to. Example: you ask “confirm my chapter is ready,” and every role obliges. You led all the witnesses. Reframe the ask neutrally and run it again.
The tool that tells these apart is an independence check: a test that makes agreement worth more than a head-count, either an independent method you run yourself or a way you force two roles to be genuinely independent. Example: two roles bless a cited number, so you open the source and recompute it yourself before trusting either.
Then comes the decision only you can make. Escalation to human judgment is the moment an AI output would settle rather than inform a decision that is yours, so you stop and decide it yourself. Example: a role proposes calling your result “causal,” which would settle your claim boundary, so it escalates to you. What you do at that moment is a human override: you take the pen back and make the call in your own words, backed by your own check, even when the roles agreed on something else. A decision that just counted the roles is not a decision. It is a vote, and votes among look-alike models are meaningless.

title: 36. Conflicting Agents and Human Escalation
text:
A worked example
You measured the local acceleration of gravity, g (how fast a dropped object speeds up, about 9.81 meters per second squared at Earth’s surface), using a simple pendulum. You timed its swing, then used the small-angle formula g = 4π²L/T², where L is the length and T is the period. Your draft reports “g = 9.81 m/s², in excellent agreement with the accepted value.”
You run three AI reviewer roles. All three return the same verdict: “Looks solid. Matches the textbook value.” Is that three confirmations?
Diagnose before you celebrate. Every one of those roles already knows 9.81 is the answer, so each one pattern-matches your number to the known constant and blesses it without checking how you got there. That is correlated error wearing the mask of confirmation: they share one blind spot, the anchor to the famous value, and it hides your actual mistake. Your swing was wide, around 30 degrees, where the small-angle formula quietly breaks down, and you timed a single swing by hand, so reaction time alone is roughly a fifth of a second of noise. The tidy “9.81” was luck riding on an honest-looking method.
This is where you escalate. The reviewers’ agreement would settle your headline claim, and that claim is yours, so you take the pen back. You recompute g from your raw timings yourself and carry the stopwatch error through the arithmetic, and the honest result is g = 9.7 ± 0.4 m/s². Consistent with 9.81, but nowhere near as precise as you wrote. You override three “looks great” verdicts and rewrite the claim with its real uncertainty. The roles counted the answer as correct. You checked whether it was earned.

title: 36. Conflicting Agents and Human Escalation
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb15 — Managing multiple AI agents + the final defense. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. There you wire a worker-critic workflow on a real draft, then simulate two four-reviewer teams and watch a shared blind spot turn unanimous agreement into false confidence you can measure.

title: 36. Conflicting Agents and Human Escalation
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own diagnosis first, then delegate. Each prompt is a checkable job, not a request for a verdict.
Locate the decisions that must escalate.
Act as a research-integrity assistant. Here is my draft's headline claim and the three
never-delegate decisions I think it contains: [paste]. Using standard research-defense
criteria, list which decisions in this draft an AI output should never be allowed to
settle, and which of my three I mislabeled. Do not settle any of them for me.
After running, verify: check its list against the escalation triggers you wrote yourself; a decision it omits is the one to guard hardest. Counters illusion of completeness (a tidy list that quietly drops the call that matters most).
List the agreements to verify.
Here are three reviewer outputs on the same draft: [paste all three]. For each pair,
name one specific way they could be wrong the SAME way: a shared assumption, a shared
anchor, or a shared prompt. Rank the pairs from most to least likely to share a blind
spot.
After running, verify: for the top pair, rerun one role with a different framing and different context and see whether the agreement survives. Counters correlated errors across tools or roles (agreement that is one flaw echoed back).
Red-team the consensus.
Act as a hostile reviewer who believes my three roles only agreed because my prompt led
them. Argue that their consensus is manufactured, name the leading words in my original
ask, and give me a neutral rewrite. Do not reassure me.
After running, verify: if it cannot find a single leading word, push back and demand the worst one, then rerun the review neutrally. Counters sycophantic agreement (praise that rehearses your ego, not your evidence).






ImportantDo not delegate



Which claims your chapter can defend, where each claim must stop, whether an agreement among your roles was ever independent, and the moment to escalate: those stay yours. No role decides its own trustworthiness, and no head-count of roles decides your claim boundary or your ethics. When an AI output would settle one of those rather than inform it, you stop and you decide.

title: 36. Conflicting Agents and Human Escalation
text:
An AI failure case
You send your chapter to four AI roles and all four reply “no major problems.” It feels like overwhelming confirmation, and it is confidently wrong. The four share a base model and read the same draft with the same instructions, so they share a blind spot. Whenever your one real flaw falls inside that blind spot, all four miss it together. Their unanimous “fine” is close to a single voice, not four.
You catch it by refusing to treat agreement as evidence. You run an independence check: re-read the flagged claim against your own inquiry declaration, or hand the draft to a role with a different framing and different context. In the lab you will see the size of this trap measured. Four correlated reviewers miss a real flaw about seven times as often as four independent ones, and piling on more correlated roles cannot close the gap.

title: 36. Conflicting Agents and Human Escalation
text:
Verification laboratory
Verify the chapter’s key result, the correlated team’s miss rate, with direct calculation from the verification guide: recompute the same quantity a second way and compare. Four independent reviewers, each catching a flaw half the time on its own, all miss only when every one misses at once: 0.5 × 0.5 × 0.5 × 0.5 = 0.0625, about the 6% the lab prints. The correlated team misses on the entire shared blind spot, 40% of chapters, plus the other 60% where each still misses on its own: 0.40 + 0.60 × 0.0625 = 0.4375, about the 43% the lab prints. Two roads, one answer. The gap between 6% and 44% is the whole argument, and now you hold it by hand, not on faith.

title: 36. Conflicting Agents and Human Escalation
text:
Project transfer
This chapter builds two of the four parts of M15, your final research chapter and AI-management portfolio. The first is your conflict-and-override log: one real clash between your roles, your diagnosis (real disagreement, correlated error, or false consensus), and the override you made with the non-AI check that backed it. The second is your independence check: one place your roles agreed on something that mattered, and the independent test that made the agreement worth more than a head-count. Write both from your own project this week, so your portfolio shows not just that your roles agreed, but that you knew when their agreement was worth trusting.

title: 36. Conflicting Agents and Human Escalation
text:
Defend your decision
In four sentences, defend one moment you escalated. Name the AI agreement or conflict you faced and the diagnosis you made (real disagreement, correlated error, or false consensus). State the call you took back and the independent check that decided it, not the roles’ vote. Then name the boundary and the uncertainty: what your override established, what it did not, and the one blind spot your roles might still share that you have not yet ruled out.

title: 34. Open and Reusable Research Packages
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 34. Open and Reusable Research Packages
text:
Why this decision matters

“I don’t want to hear that it runs on your laptop. Hand me the folder, let me clear everything, and let me press run. If your headline number does not come back on my machine, you do not have a result yet. You have a memory of one.” — a replication editor, opening the folder attached to your submission

A research note argues your claim in prose. The capsule proves the claim survives without you in the room. If the only place your analysis runs is the laptop where you built it, in the click-order you happen to remember, then no one can check you, and a result no one can check is a rumor with a chart. This chapter is how you package the work so the checking is easy, and how you stay honest about what “reproducible” really means.

title: 34. Open and Reusable Research Packages
text:
The concept
A reproducibility capsule is everything a stranger needs to rebuild your numbers and nothing they would have to guess. Example: a folder holding your notebook, your data, and a short record of every by-hand choice. It has five parts, each defined once.

A runnable notebook that passes restart-and-run-all, meaning you clear the kernel and run every cell top to bottom with no memory of earlier clicks. Example: Runtime, Restart and run all, and your headline number reappears.
A data-provenance note: where each dataset came from, its version, and how it may be used. Example: the download URL, the date, and the licence.
A fixed seed, a starting number that makes every random step return the same values on every run. Example: SEED = 464 feeding each resample.
A decision log: the by-hand choices that shaped the result, each with its reason. Example: every row you dropped, and why.
An AI-use ledger: every tool, its task, and how you verified its output.

Capsules break in boringly predictable ways, which is good news, because a predictable failure is a catchable one. Named, they are the five package sins: a hard-coded path that exists only on your machine, a missing seed that moves every run, a by-hand edit no clean run reproduces, an undocumented exclusion with no logged reason, and stale data a reader cannot reobtain. The lab’s auditor scans for all five. But hold onto the one line that carries the whole chapter: a capsule with zero flags is runnable, never proven correct. The scan gets you to the starting line. A person who reruns you cold is the race.

title: 34. Open and Reusable Research Packages
text:
A worked example
You measured nitrate, a nutrient that washes off farm fields into streams, at twelve monitoring sites in one watershed, and your headline is the share of samples above the U.S. EPA drinking-water limit of 10 mg/L. Watch the same analysis shipped two ways.
The sinful capsule loads ~/Desktop/nitrate_clean.csv, a file only your laptop has. It bootstraps a confidence interval for that share with no seed, so the interval shifts on every run. It patches one mislabeled site by hand in a cell no clean run repeats. It silently drops the below-detection-limit samples with no note on why. And its data was downloaded “sometime last spring” with no version. Five sins, and every one breaks the rerun.
The clean capsule loads the same data through a shared URL, with the source, date, and licence in the provenance note. It fixes SEED = 464 before the bootstrap. It records the one relabel in the decision log with its reason. It keeps the below-detection samples, or drops them by a logged, pre-declared rule. Now a stranger clears the kernel, runs top to bottom, and your share above 10 mg/L comes back. Same number, no guessing.
Here is the honesty check the auditor cannot do for you. The clean capsule can still be wrong: your seed might be fixed on the wrong subset, or your detection-limit rule might be a bad rule cleanly logged. Runnable is not correct. That gap is exactly why your capsule is exercised by a partner, not only by a script.

title: 34. Open and Reusable Research Packages
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb14 — From poster to research note. Open it in Colab with the badge above. There you build audit_capsule, watch it flag all five sins in a deliberately broken package and none in a clean one, then paste your own capsule’s key lines in and fix what it catches before Friday’s studio.

title: 34. Open and Reusable Research Packages
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt is a checkable job, not a verdict.
Locate the standard tool.
Act as a reproducibility assistant. Name the standard file and format for recording
the exact package versions a notebook needs in order to rerun, and cite the official
documentation. Only name tools you are confident exist.
After running, verify: open the official docs and confirm the file and its syntax exist. Counters confident fabrication (an invented tool name arrives as confidently as a real one).
List so you can verify (the cold replicator).
Here are the key lines of my capsule: [paste]. Playing a replicator who has only
these lines and none of my memory, list every input, file, or by-hand step you would
need to rerun my headline number and might not find here.
After running, verify: match each named gap against what your own audit_capsule run actually flagged, and drop any gap that maps to no real missing line. Counters illusion of completeness (a tidy capsule that looks whole while one input is missing).
Red-team the claim.
My claim is: "a stranger can rerun this capsule and get my number." Act as a hostile
replication reviewer and name every way it could fail on a machine that is not mine.
Do not fix it for me.
After running, verify: if it only reassures you, push back and demand the single worst failure. Counters sycophantic agreement (praise that reviews your ego, not your package).






ImportantDo not delegate



You decide what goes in the capsule and whether the sentence “this reproduces” is honest. The tool can list gaps and pin versions, but it cannot record your true data provenance, judge whether an exclusion’s logged reason is a good reason, or certify that your analysis is correct rather than merely runnable. The name on the folder, and the claim that a stranger can trust it, are yours.

title: 34. Open and Reusable Research Packages
text:
An AI failure case
You paste your notebook into Gemini and ask, “will this run cold and produce my nitrate share?” It walks through every cell and answers, with total confidence, “Yes, this runs top to bottom cleanly and returns your headline number.” It sounds like a green check. It is not. Gemini never executed anything. It read the code and narrated it. Cell 3 loads ~/Desktop/nitrate_clean.csv, a path only your laptop has, and the fluent walkthrough described reading that file as if it were sitting there.
You catch it the only way that counts: you actually run restart-and-run-all on a fresh kernel, ideally in Colab on a machine that is not yours. The FileNotFoundError appears on cell 3 in seconds. A narrated run is not a run. You verify the notebook by executing it, not by reading a paragraph about executing it.

title: 34. Open and Reusable Research Packages
text:
Verification laboratory
Verify this chapter’s key result, that your capsule reproduces, with a benchmark result from the verification guide: compare a fresh run against a previously verified value. Record your headline number from your own machine. Then clear the kernel and run all in a second environment, a classmate’s laptop or a clean Colab session, and check that the number returns within rounding. A match means the capsule travels. A mismatch means something you carried by hand, a path, a seed, an unlogged edit, was doing quiet work you never packaged. Two machines, one number, is a reproduced result.

title: 34. Open and Reusable Research Packages
text:
Project transfer
This chapter builds the capsule half of your M14 research note and reproducibility capsule. Assemble the five parts for your own project, run audit_capsule on your key lines, and fix the first sin it flags. Then do the thing the script cannot: hand the folder to a partner at Friday’s studio and have them clear the kernel and rerun it cold. The number they get, set next to the number you reported, is your reproducibility evidence, and their rerun is what turns “it runs for me” into a claim you can defend.

title: 34. Open and Reusable Research Packages
text:
Defend your decision
In four sentences, defend your capsule. Name what you put in it and the one package sin you were most tempted to leave unfixed. State the bounded claim you can now make, “a stranger reran this and got my number,” and its boundary: runnable is not correct, and reproduced on the machines you tried is not proof it runs everywhere. Close with your uncertainty: the one input or step a cold replicator might still trip on, and the check you would run next to close it.

title: 32. Replication and Reproduction
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 32. Replication and Reproduction
text:
Why this decision matters

“I do not care that your figure is pretty or that the code ran on your laptop. Hand me your files and let me get the same number back out. If I cannot, you do not have a result yet. You have a story.” — a principal investigator reading a first-year researcher’s lab report

In a working lab, a number nobody else can regenerate is a liability, not a finding. A poster can round, a caption can overstate, and a clean-looking notebook can still hide the one choice that holds the whole claim up. The reviewer above is applying the standard every result eventually meets. This chapter puts you on the reviewing side of that standard first, so you learn to see the cracks before someone finds them in your own work.

title: 32. Replication and Reproduction
text:
The concept
Two words sound alike and mean different things. Reproduction is getting the same number from the same data and code a study shipped. Example: you open a classmate’s notebook, run it top to bottom, and check that the same growth figure comes back. Replication is getting a similar result from a new study or new data. Example: a second lab runs its own experiment and sees the same effect. This chapter is about reproduction. You test the package, not the world.
What you test is a reproducibility package: the full bundle a study ships so someone else can rebuild its numbers, meaning the data, the code, the run order, any random seed, and the write-up. The one figure the main claim rests on is the headline number. Example: “the supplement raised larval growth by 0.5 mm.” You rebuild that first, using restart-and-run-all, which means clearing the notebook’s memory and running every cell from the top with no manual fixes. If a number only appears when cells run out of order, it is not reproducible.
A clean run proves the code executes. It never proves the write-up is true, so you run three audits on top of it. Claims-vs-computation agreement checks that every sentence the write-up asserts is backed by a number the code actually prints. An alternative specification is a different but equally defensible way to compute the same headline, to see whether the answer depends on an undisclosed choice. A hidden assumption is a claim the analysis quietly relies on and never states, which the result would collapse without. The most dangerous one in biology has a name: pseudoreplication, treating repeated measurements from the same animal, plate, or tank as independent data points when the real experimental unit is the animal, plate, or tank (Hurlbert, 1984).

title: 32. Replication and Reproduction
text:
A worked example
A classmate’s package tests whether a probiotic added to the water raises the body length of zebrafish larvae. Six tanks: three get the probiotic, three get plain water. Each tank holds about 40 larvae, and body length is recorded for every larva. The poster reads: “The probiotic increased larval body length by 0.5 mm.”
Reproduce it. You restart-and-run-all. The code loads the file, splits larvae by tank type, and differences the two group means. The number that comes back is 0.43 mm, not 0.5. The package reproduces, which is a real success. But the write-up says 0.5 and the code says 0.43. That silent rounding is your first finding, and it earns you the right to push harder.
Change one defensible choice. The package dropped larvae that died before the final measurement and never said so. Keep each dead larva’s last recorded length instead, an equally defensible rule, and the gap slides to 0.31 mm. Neither number is a lie. The finding is that the headline moves on a choice the poster never disclosed, so the honest report is the range.
Test the hidden assumption. The package reports its gap as if all 240 larvae were independent, producing a tight interval well above zero. They are not independent. The probiotic was assigned by tank, so larvae sharing a tank share water, food, and crowding. The real experimental unit is the tank, and there are only six. Recompute at the tank level, averaging each tank first and comparing three treated tanks against three controls, and the interval widens until its lower edge touches zero. The point estimate barely moved. The honest uncertainty around it exploded. That is pseudoreplication, and it is the weakness with teeth.
Rank by threat. Pseudoreplication comes first, because it can dissolve the “clear effect” claim. The undisclosed survivor rule comes second, because it moves the number. The 0.43-reported-as-0.5 gap comes third: real, but the smallest threat to whether an effect exists.

title: 32. Replication and Reproduction
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb13 — Async module: replication and red-team of a peer’s package. Open it in Colab with the badge above. There you cold-reproduce a real field experiment’s headline number from its files alone, then run the same three audits you just saw: you line each written claim against the output behind it, move the number with an undisclosed specification, and widen its interval by respecting the clustering the write-up ignored.

title: 32. Replication and Reproduction
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt is a checkable job, not a verdict you hand off.
Locate every missing input.
Act as a cold reproducibility auditor. Here is everything a peer's package
shipped: [paste the README, the data description, the code, and the headline
claim]. List every input or step a stranger needs and might not find: the data
file and its version, the exact run order, any random seed, any hard-coded path.
Put it in a table with the item, whether the package supplies it, and the cell
where I should check.
After running, verify: map each item to a real cell, then ask “what is the single most important thing you left out?” Counters illusion of completeness (a tidy gap list can still omit the one missing pointer that blocks the whole run).
Walk the headline cell, then confirm it independently.
Here is the cell that computes the package's headline number: [paste the cell].
Explain line by line what it computes, and name which printed value is the
headline a reader should compare against the write-up. Then give me one
independent way to confirm that value without rerunning this exact code.
After running, verify: confirm the value it names matches the number your run actually printed, then run its independent check yourself. Counters confident fabrication (a fluent walkthrough can describe a number the code never produced).
Red-team the hidden assumption.
This analysis treats every measured larva as an independent observation, though
the probiotic was assigned by tank. Act as a hostile methods reviewer. Name the
one assumption most likely to inflate the reported certainty, say what the honest
experimental unit is, and do not reassure me the design is fine.
After running, verify: recompute the interval at the tank level yourself and see whether it reaches zero. Counters plausible-but-wrong-method (an independent-observations assumption on clustered data silently shrinks every interval).






ImportantDo not delegate



An AI tool cannot run the code, so it can never settle whether the number actually reproduced. Only your own restart-and-run-all does that. You also keep three judgments: which weakness most threatens the claim, how you rank the recommendations, and the final verdict on what the package can and cannot support. A tool proposes suspects. You decide which ones the evidence convicts.

title: 32. Replication and Reproduction
text:
An AI failure case
You paste the whole package into a general AI tool and ask, “Does this study reproduce, and are its limitations complete?” It answers with total confidence: yes, the code runs, and the limitations paragraph looks thorough, covering sample size, measurement noise, and a call for future work. Every sentence is fluent. The tool never once flags that 240 larvae came from only six tanks. It has quietly mistaken a long list for a complete one.
You catch it by refusing to grade the paragraph and grading the design instead. You ask what the experimental unit actually was, then recompute the interval at the tank level. When the lower bound drops to zero, the “clear effect” the tool endorsed turns out to rest on a certainty the data never earned. A green check and a polished paragraph are not a reproduced result. You verify the number and its match to the claim, never the confident story about them.

title: 32. Replication and Reproduction
text:
Verification laboratory
Verify this chapter’s key result, the honest tank-level interval, with alternative code from the verification guide: recompute the same quantity a different way and compare. Collapse each tank to its own mean length, giving six numbers, then difference the three treated tank means against the three control tank means. Confirm the point estimate matches your larva-level number and that the spread across six tanks is far wider than the spread the naive 240-point calculation implied. Back it with a direct calculation: average two tanks by hand from the raw rows and check your code returns the same two means. Two roads to one answer is a verified result. Two roads to two answers means you measured the pipeline, not the fish.

title: 32. Replication and Reproduction
text:
Project transfer
This chapter becomes your M13 Replication and Red-Team Report on an assigned peer package. Draft its spine from your own run: a reproduction log (did restart-and-run-all succeed, what headline number did you get, did it match the write-up), a claim-to-output trace with every mismatch flagged, one alternative specification and one hidden assumption you tested, and prioritized recommendations ranked by threat to the claim. Post your single most-threatening weakness to the board with the one check that would settle it. The audit you run on a classmate here is the exact audit your own dossier has to survive when class resumes.

title: 32. Replication and Reproduction
text:
Defend your decision
In four sentences, defend your verdict on one package you audited. Name the headline number you reproduced and whether it matched the write-up. State the single weakness you ranked most threatening and the number or output that exposed it. Give the boundary: reproduction confirms the number regenerates, not that the science is right, and a red-team names risks it does not by itself resolve. Close with your uncertainty: the one check you could not finish, and the run you still owe before you would call the verdict final.

title: 30. Difficult Questions and Uncertainty
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 30. Difficult Questions and Uncertainty
text:
Why this decision matters

“When a presenter tells me, unprompted, exactly what their study can’t show, I trust everything else they said more. When they insist it’s airtight, I start looking for the crack myself.” — a policy stakeholder who has heard a thousand poster pitches

Your poster is finished and honest. The danger now is your own mouth. Under pressure, a nervous presenter does one of two things, and both misstate the evidence. One buries a real finding under apology. The other, caught in the moment, upgrades a careful “went together” into a confident “caused.” The stakeholder above is not testing your confidence. She is testing whether you know where your claim stops. This chapter gives you the answer that survives her hardest question.

title: 30. Difficult Questions and Uncertainty
text:
The concept
The move that turns a limit into a strength has a name: the ULN move, a three-beat way to speak a boundary. It runs uncertainty, then limitation, then next step, and each beat is a term worth defining once.
An uncertainty statement is a sentence saying how much your number could wobble, and why. Example: “with 812 people, my estimate could sit a few points higher or lower.” A limitation is a true sentence about what your design cannot show. Example: “I measured that two things went together, so I cannot say one caused the other.” A next step is the study that would resolve that limitation. Delivered together, ULN sounds like expertise, because you are naming the edge of your evidence on purpose.
The failure ULN prevents is the apology spiral: burying a real, defensible finding under self-erasure like “honestly this is probably all wrong, my sample’s tiny.” The fix is to choose precision, naming the exact boundary, over hedging, vague self-protection that blurs where the claim stops. “This is one campus sample” is precision. “Sort of, take it with a grain of salt” is hedging. Precision informs the listener; hedging only protects you.
The second instrument is the question bank: a short written list of the hardest fair questions a visitor could ask, each with a prepared answer. Five covers the space, one per recurring type (method, alternative explanation, so-what, generalization, AI-use). When you cannot fully answer one, you deliver an honest don’t-know: an admission with content, naming what you cannot say, the thing you can stand on, and the method that would settle it.

title: 30. Difficult Questions and Uncertainty
text:
A worked example
You study human-computer interaction. From your app’s usage logs you compared task-completion times for people who chose the compact toolbar against those who kept the expanded one. Compact users found the target page a median of six seconds faster. At the poster, a visitor asks the question you feared: “so the compact toolbar causes faster navigation?”
Your study cannot say that. Users picked their own toolbar, so power users may have chosen compact and would have been fast with any layout. That self-selection is a confound, a third factor that shapes both the choice and the outcome. So you deliver the two-beat reply. First the truth: “I can’t say it causes the speed, because compact users may already be power users.” Then the interest: “the study that would answer you is randomly assigning the toolbar to new users, and that’s what I’d run next.” You refused the word “causes,” and handed the visitor a real research question rather than a wall.

title: 30. Difficult Questions and Uncertainty
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb11 — Poster delivery: pitches, uncertainty, and the questions you fear. Open it in Colab with the badge above. There you run an apology-spiral detector that counts self-erasing words in three sample deliveries, then paste in your own uncertainty-and-limitations sentence and drive its flag count to zero. You also build your five-question bank and rehearse the causal reply against your own project.

title: 30. Difficult Questions and Uncertainty
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt below is a checkable job, not a request for approval.
List your hardest questions, to verify against your own fear.
Play a skeptical but fair conference visitor. My project: "[one-paragraph summary
of question, design, and headline finding]". Write the single hardest FAIR question
for each type: method, alternative explanation, why-it-matters, generalization,
AI-use. For each, flag whether an honest two-sentence answer exists.
After running, verify: compare the five against the one question you wrote down that you most dread. If it is missing, the set is not complete. Counters illusion of completeness (a tidy five that omits the exact question you fear).
Force the uncertainty into the open.
Here is my headline number and how I got it: "[paste]". Do not restate it.
List what is missing before I can state its uncertainty honestly: the sample
size, the source of wobble, and the one limitation a reviewer would raise first.
After running, verify: confirm each item against your own data before you speak it. Counters missing uncertainty (a clean number reported with no bound).
Red-team your reply.
Here is my two-sentence reply to "does it cause it?": "[paste]". Act as a hostile
reviewer. Name every word that still claims more than an association supports.
Do not rewrite it for me.
After running, verify: if it only praises the reply, push back and demand the single worst word. Counters sycophantic agreement (praise that reviews your ego, not your evidence).






ImportantDo not delegate



Three decisions never leave your hands: which claim your evidence actually supports, where that claim must stop, and how you acknowledge its uncertainty and limitations. The tool can draft a question or flag a word, but only you decide the compass position your design can bear and the one word you must refuse aloud. Your honest don’t-know is yours to mean, not to recite.

title: 30. Difficult Questions and Uncertainty
text:
An AI failure case
You paste your limitation into an AI tool and ask it to “make this sound more confident for the poster.” It returns a fluent, polished sentence: “The compact toolbar improves navigation speed, with strong results across users.” It runs clean, it reads well, and it is wrong. The tool quietly swapped “was associated with faster times” for “improves,” and “improves” is a causal verb your observational logs cannot support. This is a silent scope change dressed as helpful editing.
You catch it by reading the AI’s verb against your poster’s headline, word for word. Your headline says “associated with.” The rewrite says “improves.” The kind of claim moved while the sentence got prettier. You keep your own verb, and log that the polish inflated the claim.

title: 30. Difficult Questions and Uncertainty
text:
Verification laboratory
The chapter’s key spoken result is your uncertainty-and-limitations delivery, and you verify it two independent ways from the verification guide. First, direct calculation: recompute the detector’s flag count by hand. Read your sentence aloud and tally every self-erasing word (“sorry,” “just,” “probably,” “I guess”). If your hand count matches the printed count, the number is trustworthy; if it disagrees, the harness measured something other than your words. Second, peer reasoning: say your boundary sentence to a classmate and ask, “does anything I said claim more than my design shows?” A zero flag count proves your spine was straight, but only a second human can confirm the boundary itself was the right one.

title: 30. Difficult Questions and Uncertainty
text:
Project transfer
This chapter completes the second half of your M11 presentation package, due at Friday’s studio. You leave with three artifacts you drafted yourself and then stress-tested: a ULN paragraph pairing your most threatening limitation with its next step, a five-question bank marked “defend” or “concede,” and one honest don’t-know written in full form for the question you fear most. At the mock symposium you run all three under live fire, so the first stranger who asks “does it cause it?” meets a prepared answer, not a flinch.

title: 30. Difficult Questions and Uncertainty
text:
Defend your decision
In four sentences, defend the answer you prepared. State your headline claim and its exact boundary, worded to stop where your evidence stops. Name the compass position your design supports and the one word you must refuse aloud to keep the claim honest. Give your uncertainty and limitation in ULN form, ending on the next step rather than the flaw. Close with the one question you still cannot fully answer, and the study you would run to close it.

title: 28. Poster Criticism
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 28. Poster Criticism
text:
Why this decision matters
Picture the last hour before a poster prints. A research-integrity judge, the person at a conference who probes whether a poster’s claims are actually backed, walks your row and stops at one number on your board. “Where did this come from?” If you can walk that judge to the exact line of code that produced it, the whole poster earns trust. If you answer “I think that came from the analysis somewhere,” the judge stops believing every other number too. Once the file goes to the printer you cannot patch it, so the honesty has to be built in before it leaves your hands. This chapter is the last audit between a draft poster and a permanent one.

title: 28. Poster Criticism
text:
The concept
Three passes turn a fragile poster into one that survives a stranger, and each ends in a decision only you can make.
The first pass is the gallery-walk audit, a structured walk where you stop at a poster and run the same fixed checklist instead of glancing and moving on. Four questions form its core. The claim-boundary audit asks whether the headline stays inside its evidence, where a claim boundary is the line between what your evidence licenses and what it does not, sitting in the headline verb: an observational field trial may say “is associated with,” never “causes.” The figure-honesty audit asks whether the picture matches the numbers, where figure honesty means a chart cannot say more than its data, so an axis starting just below the data draws a small gap as a canyon. The read-path audit asks whether a stranger finds the headline in ninety seconds. The uncertainty audit asks whether the interval sits beside the claim, not buried in a footnote. A fifth check, accessibility, asks whether every reader can receive the claim, and its fix has a name: redundant encoding means carrying a distinction in more than one channel, such as a value label plus a hatch pattern, so color is never the only signal a color-blind reader can lose.
The second pass is the oral defense, the live conversation at your board where you answer questions you cannot edit afterward. Three lenses cover almost every hard question: an outsider testing whether your claim survives translation, a specialist probing a design choice, and a skeptic offering a rival explanation for the same evidence. The judgment underneath is defend-versus-concede: you defend when the objection asks for something your evidence already licenses, and you concede, narrowing the claim on the spot, when it asks for a crossing your design never paid for. Your compass position, the kind and reach of the question your project answered, tells you which call to make.
The third pass is the lock. Claim-evidence traceability means every number on the poster traces to the exact cell that produced it, so you can show that line when a judge points at it. The terminal lock is the moment the poster becomes final and unchangeable, which is why the audit before it is the most consequential one you run all week.

title: 28. Poster Criticism
text:
A worked example
An honors agronomy poster carries the headline “Compost RAISES corn yield.” Its center figure is a bar chart of mean grain yield for plots that received a compost amendment against plots that did not, drawn as a red bar and a green bar. The means are 185 and 191 bushels per acre, a gap of 6. The y-axis starts at 180, and no interval is shown. The plots were not randomized; the farmer chose which fields got compost.
Walk the gallery audit across it. The claim-boundary audit catches the verb first: “raises” is causal, but nothing was randomized, so a naturally richer field could have received both the compost and the higher yield. The honest headline is “compost is associated with about 6 more bushels per acre.” The figure-honesty audit catches the axis, floored at 180, which draws a 6-bushel gap several times taller than it truly is, so run it from 0. The uncertainty audit catches the missing interval: a 95% interval of roughly ±9 bushels per acre swamps a 6-bushel gap, so it has to ride beside the headline. The accessibility audit catches the red-green pair, which a reviewer who is red-green color-blind, roughly one man in twelve, cannot tell apart, so you add value labels and a hatch pattern.
You cannot fix everything before printing, so you decide. The claim-boundary hit travels farthest, because a reviewer who catches one causal overreach distrusts the whole board, so you lock the associational verb first. That single decision, made by you and defended out loud, is the work of this chapter.

text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own audit first, then delegate. Each prompt hands the tool a job you can check, with a verify note naming the failure it defends against.
Generate the hostile questions (you map each to an audit).
Act as a hostile poster reviewer. My headline figure is a bar chart comparing
mean corn yield for compost-amended versus control plots on a y-axis that starts
at 180, titled "Compost RAISES corn yield," with no interval shown and the two
bars told apart by red versus green only. Generate the six hardest skeptic
questions you would ask, then name the single flaw this figure cannot answer.
After running, verify (counters illusion of completeness): map each question to one audit (claim boundary, figure honesty, read path, uncertainty, accessibility). An entire audit the tidy list skips is the gap. Then check the survivors against the real gap and exaggeration factor your own notebook cell printed.
List the color encodings to verify (you check against the rendered figure).
Here is the code that draws my poster's headline figure: [paste the cell]. List
every place it encodes a meaningful distinction with color, and for each, say what
a red-green color-blind reader and a screen-reader user would fail to receive.
Then propose one redundant encoding that carries the same distinction.
After running, verify (counters plausible-but-wrong-method): read each encoding it names against your rendered figure. Generic accessibility advice that does not match how your figure is actually drawn is wrong for your case, so trust the picture you can see, and re-render after applying one proposed fix.
Red-team your spoken answer (you keep the words).
Here is my written headline: "[paste it]". Here is what I plan to say when a
skeptic asks whether something else could explain it: "[paste it]". Find every
place my spoken answer claims more than my written headline. Do not rewrite it;
list the exact words.
After running, verify (counters silent scope change): read each flagged phrase next to your compass position. Keep every catch of a spoken “causes” your written poster never said, and reject any push to concede a point your design does license.






ImportantDo not delegate



These stay yours, however fluent the tool sounds. The words of your headline claim, and the verb that keeps it inside your compass position. The call, made out loud, to defend a point or concede it. And the decision that the poster is honest enough to lock. The tool can generate hard questions and scan a figure for color traps; deciding what your evidence licenses, and answering for it at the board, is yours.

title: 28. Poster Criticism
text:
An AI failure case
You describe your compost figure to Gemini and ask for the hardest skeptic questions. Back comes a crisp six-item list: the sample size, the single growing season, the soil-test method, generalizability to other regions, cost, and the definition of “yield.” It reads as thorough, and the temptation is to treat the poster as fully audited.
It is not. The list never mentions the truncated axis, and never mentions the red-green bars. Those are the two most concrete, most fixable flaws on the board, and the polished list quietly skipped both. This is the illusion of completeness: a long, organized answer that omits the thing that matters most.
Here is how you catch it. You map each question to one audit. Four land on claim boundary and uncertainty, none on figure honesty, none on accessibility. Two whole audits are missing, which tells you the list is incomplete no matter how confident it reads. Then you check the survivors against the exaggeration factor your own notebook cell printed, and cut any question that assumes a gap other than the 6 bushels you computed. The completeness check is yours, not the tool’s.

title: 28. Poster Criticism
text:
Verification laboratory
The chapter’s key result is that the same numbers drawn honestly tell a smaller story than the truncated chart does. Confirm it with direct calculation (from the verification guide): recompute the yield gap by hand, 191 minus 185 is 6 bushels per acre, then recompute the truncated axis’s exaggeration factor, the taller bar’s height above the 180 floor divided by the shorter bar’s, and confirm the honest 0-to-full-scale chart shows a difference that is real but small. For the citation row of the lock, add primary-source reading: click each source your poster names, and confirm the work opens and says what your poster claims.

title: 28. Poster Criticism
text:
Defend your decision
In a short paragraph, state your lock call for your own poster. Is it ready to be terminal, yes or no? Name your locked headline and the single verb that keeps it inside its compass position, and the uncertainty that rides in the same eye-span as the claim. Then name the boundary: which hit did you fix, which did you leave for later, and what does the locked poster still not establish? A lock call with no stated boundary and no uncertainty is not yet defended.

title: 26. Claim–Evidence Tables
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 26. Claim–Evidence Tables
text:
Why this decision matters

“I point at one number on your board and ask where it came from. Walk me to the line that made it and I trust the whole poster. Say ‘it’s in the analysis somewhere’ and I stop believing every other number up there.” — a research-integrity judge working a poster hall

A poster is a set of promises made in public. Each headline number, each figure, each cited fact is a claim a stranger can test in ninety seconds. The dangerous gap is between a claim you can say and a claim you can back. A claim–evidence table closes that gap on purpose, before anyone points a finger, so the honesty is built into the board and not patched under pressure.

title: 26. Claim–Evidence Tables
text:
The concept
A claim–evidence table is a table with one row per claim, pairing each claim your poster makes with the specific evidence behind it. Example: a row that reads “Material B conducts heat about 1.4x faster than Material A” in the claim column, with the two measured conductivities in the evidence column.
Four columns carry each row, and each is a term worth knowing.

Claim. The statement your poster asserts as true. Example: “our measured value of g is 9.79 m/s².”
Evidence. The exact result or source that backs the claim, with its uncertainty. Example: 30 timed swings giving a period of 2.007 s, and the interval ±0.06 m/s² around the estimate.
Trace. Where a number was actually produced, named precisely enough to open. A traceable number is one you can walk back to the notebook cell that computed it. Example: “cell 16, where g = 4*pi**2*L / T**2 runs.” A number you cannot walk back, one typed from memory or rounded by hand, is an untraceable number, and it does not belong on the board.
Boundary. The claim boundary is the line between what the evidence licenses and what it does not. Example: “measured at one location, one pendulum length, timed by hand, so this is not the standard sea-level value.”

Building the table is the graded skill. Once every claim has a filled row, an unbacked claim has nowhere to hide, because a blank cell is a visible hole.

title: 26. Claim–Evidence Tables
text:
A worked example
You built a physics poster reporting a measurement of g, the acceleration due to gravity, from a simple pendulum. Your headline reads “we measured g = 9.79 m/s².” Before the file goes to the printer, you build the table.
Row 1 (the headline). Claim: “g = 9.79 ± 0.06 m/s².” Evidence: 30 timings of a 1.00 m pendulum, mean period 2.007 s, the spread of the 30 periods giving the interval. Trace: cell 16, the line g = 4*pi**2*L / T**2. Boundary: one location, one length, hand timing, so a reaction-time bias of order a tenth of a second is possible and the value is not claimed to be the accepted 9.81 m/s².
Filling Row 1 forces one honest catch. You reach for the trace and find the number on your draft board reads 9.81, not 9.79. You had quietly written the textbook value instead of the one your cell printed. The table caught an untraceable number before a judge did.
Row 2 (a secondary claim). Claim: “the period barely changes with amplitude for small swings.” Evidence: periods measured at 5° and 15° differ by less than your timing spread. Trace: cell 18, the amplitude comparison. Boundary: only tested below 15°, so nothing is claimed about large swings.
Two claims, two rows, every number walked back to a cell, every boundary written down. That table is the thing that lets your poster survive a pointed finger.

title: 26. Claim–Evidence Tables
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb10 — Poster criticism + final poster lock. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. There you run the final-lock audit on a real poster: you trace each number to the cell that produced it, confirm each citation resolves to a source you opened yourself, and turn loose claims into locked, backed rows.

title: 26. Claim–Evidence Tables
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own draft table first, then delegate. Each prompt is a checkable job, not a request for a verdict.
Locate the source for a cited fact.
Act as a physics reference assistant. I want to cite the accepted sea-level value
of g. Name the value and one real, retrievable source that reports it, and give me
enough detail to find it myself. If you are not confident a source exists, say so
instead of guessing.
After running, verify: open the named source yourself and confirm it reports that value before it touches your poster. Counters confident fabrication (an invented citation arrives as confidently as a real one).
List every claim, so none is unbacked.
Here is my poster text: [paste it]. List every distinct claim it makes, one per
row. For each, name what evidence a reader would need to see to believe it. Do not
judge whether my evidence is good; just surface every claim, including implicit
ones.
After running, verify: check the list against your own table and add any claim you had not written a row for. Counters illusion of completeness (a tidy list that quietly skips the one claim with no evidence behind it).
Red-team the claim–evidence match.
Here is one row of my claim-evidence table: claim "[paste]", evidence "[paste]".
Act as a hostile reviewer. Tell me every way this evidence fails to license this
claim, or claims more than it shows. Do not soften the claim for me; name the words
that overreach.
After running, verify: read each objection against your boundary column and reject any that asks you to concede something your evidence does license. Counters silent scope change (evidence for an easier claim, dressed up as backing the hard one).






ImportantDo not delegate



The wording of each claim, the judgment that a piece of evidence actually backs it, and the boundary each claim carries stay yours. An AI can list claims and draft rows, but it cannot open your notebook cell to confirm a number is real, and it cannot decide the evidence is honest enough to print. The call that the table is complete and the poster is locked is yours to declare and defend.

title: 26. Claim–Evidence Tables
text:
An AI failure case
You paste your poster text and ask an AI tool to build the whole claim–evidence table for you. It returns a clean, complete grid, every cell filled, and the trace column reads confidently: “g value from cell 14.” You almost accept it. Then you open cell 14, and it computes the period, not g. The value you report is produced two cells later, in cell 16. The tool guessed a plausible cell number and filled it with full confidence, and its tidy finished table hid the wrong trace behind a polished look.
You catch it by refusing to trust a trace you have not opened. Reading the actual cell, number in hand, is the whole discipline. A filled table is not a verified table.

title: 26. Claim–Evidence Tables
text:
Verification laboratory
Verify this chapter’s key result, the table’s traceability, with two named methods from the verification guide. For every traced number, use direct calculation: recompute the value a second, simpler way and confirm it matches the figure on the board, digit for digit. For every cited fact, use primary-source reading: open the source, find the line, and confirm it says what your poster claims. A number that survives an independent recomputation and a citation that resolves to a real page are backed. A mismatch on either is a finding, and the claim comes off the board until you fix it.

title: 26. Claim–Evidence Tables
text:
Project transfer
This chapter is a graded piece of your M10 final poster lock. Build a claim–evidence table for your own poster: one row for every headline number and cited fact, each with its trace to a cell or source and its boundary written down. That table is what you carry into the gallery-walk audit and the oral defense, and it is the proof that your poster’s honesty was verified, not merely asserted.

title: 26. Claim–Evidence Tables
text:
Defend your decision
In four sentences, defend one row of your table. State the claim in the exact words that will appear on your board, and name the specific evidence that backs it. State its boundary: what this claim does not establish, and the compass position it speaks from. Close with its uncertainty: the interval or caveat that rides in the same eye-span as the claim, and the single check you ran to confirm the traced number is real.

title: 24. Recognizing False Confidence
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 24. Recognizing False Confidence
text:
Why this decision matters
Picture the officer who reviews grant reports for a watershed restoration program. Every year she reads summaries that announce success in polished, sure prose. Her standard is blunt: “I don’t fund the sentence. I fund the number behind it. Show me the measurement, how you computed the change, and why the project gets the credit.” A confident paragraph moves nobody who has read a hundred confident paragraphs. What moves her is a number you recomputed and can defend. An AI partner will write you a beautiful, sure-sounding finding in seconds. Your job is to treat that confidence as worth nothing until you have checked what sits under it.

title: 24. Recognizing False Confidence
text:
The concept
The trap has a name. False confidence is when fluent, detailed output makes you feel sure of something you never actually verified. Example: an AI summary reports that a stream cleanup “cut nitrate by roughly 40 percent,” and the crisp figure and smooth wording make the claim feel settled before you have looked at a single measurement.
Two habits make the trap dangerous. Automation bias is the tendency to over-trust an automated system and stop checking, precisely because a machine produced the answer. Example: you would double-check a lab partner’s arithmetic, but you paste the AI’s percentage straight into your report because “the tool computed it.” Illusion of understanding is mistaking a smooth explanation for real comprehension. Example: you nod along as the AI walks you line by line through its calculation, then realize you could not defend the number to the watershed reviewer without rerunning it yourself.
The rule that beats all three is short: verify the number, not the paragraph. Verification is an independent check, run by a method outside the model, that a result is actually true. Example: instead of trusting the AI’s “40 percent,” you recompute the average nitrate before and after the cleanup from the raw readings and see what the data really show. Code that runs without errors is not the same as code that is correct, and a summary that reads beautifully is not the same as a summary that is right.

title: 24. Recognizing False Confidence
text:
A worked example
You are studying a small stream where a riparian buffer (a strip of trees and grasses planted along a bank to filter runoff) was installed last year. You have weekly nitrate readings (nitrate is a nutrient from fertilizer that, in excess, fuels algae blooms and starves the water of oxygen), measured in milligrams per liter, for the year before planting and the year after.
You ask an AI to summarize the dataset. It returns: “The riparian buffer reduced average nitrate by about 40 percent, a clear environmental success.” The sentence is fluent and sure. Here is where false confidence would win: you feel the relief of a clean result and reach to quote it.
Instead you verify the number. You compute the mean nitrate for the “before” weeks and for the “after” weeks, then subtract. Suppose the before-mean is 8.1 mg/L and the after-mean is 6.9 mg/L. The real drop is 1.2 mg/L, about 15 percent, not 40. The confident summary overstated the change by more than double. A second problem also hid behind the polish: the readings come from one site with no comparison stream, so even the true 15 percent cannot be pinned on the buffer alone. A wet spring or a new practice upstream could move nitrate just as much. You keep the finding you can defend, “nitrate fell about 15 percent at this site over one year,” and you drop both the inflated figure and the word “success.”

title: 24. Recognizing False Confidence
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb00, Research in the Age of AI: Your Arm, Your RA, Not Your Brain. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. In the lab you run a small randomized study, meet a confident one-line summary that claims a large effect, and recompute the actual gap in the data to watch the summary overstate it. You then red-team a finding you wrote and catch an AI that praises your sentence instead of testing it.

title: 24. Recognizing False Confidence
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own expectation first, then delegate. Each prompt hands out a task you can check, with a verify note naming the failure it defends against.
Point to the real number (you recompute it).
Here is a cell that computes average nitrate before and after a buffer was
installed and prints both means: [paste it]. Tell me which single printed number
is the actual change, and state plainly whether a "40 percent reduction" summary
is or is not that number. Do not rerun it; I will read my own output and compare.
After running, verify (counters confident fabrication): a fluent summary can state a number the data never produced. Read the two means your cell printed and compute the change yourself. If it does not match the AI’s figure, trust your printout.
Force the uncertainty out (you check the bound).
For the before/after nitrate change in my data, rate how firm the estimate is.
Give a plausible range, and name what drives the uncertainty: the sample size, the
week-to-week scatter, and the lack of a comparison stream.
After running, verify (counters missing uncertainty): if a result arrives as a clean fact with no bound, assume the uncertainty was dropped, not that there was none. Confirm the range against the spread you can see in your own readings.
Red-team your finding (you keep the claim).
Here is my finding: "[paste your sentence]." Act as a hostile watershed reviewer.
Name every place it reaches past one site and one year of data. Do not rewrite it
for me; list the weaknesses.
After running, verify (counters sycophantic agreement): if every objection is mild, or it calls your sentence “well-balanced,” push back and ask for the single worst flaw.






ImportantDo not delegate



These stay yours, no matter how sure the tool sounds. Deciding what claim the verified number actually supports, and how far that claim reaches. Judging whether your design lets you credit the buffer for the change, or only lets you report that nitrate fell. Stating the uncertainty and the limits in your own words. The tool can draft a summary; deciding whether that summary is true, and answering for it, is the researcher’s job.

title: 24. Recognizing False Confidence
text:
An AI failure case
You ask the AI to “summarize what this water-quality dataset shows,” and it hands back a confident paragraph ending in “a 40 percent reduction, a clear success.” Every part reads like a finding you could quote: a round figure, a firm verdict, no hedging.
Here is exactly how you catch it. You do not repeat the paragraph. You compute the two group means from the raw readings and subtract, and the real change is about 15 percent, not 40. Then you ask what the design licenses: one site, no control stream, a single year, so even the honest 15 percent could come from the weather rather than the buffer. The confident summary failed twice, once on the number and once on the causal reach, and both failures were invisible until you checked outside the model.

title: 24. Recognizing False Confidence
text:
Verification laboratory
The chapter’s key result is that a confident summary’s number can be flatly wrong. Confirm it yourself with direct calculation (from the verification guide): recompute the before-mean and the after-mean by hand, or with a second simple expression, and subtract. If the change you get differs from the AI’s figure, the figure was never evidence. For extra assurance, use alternative code: compute the same difference a second way, such as filtering each period separately and subtracting, and confirm both roads give one answer.

title: 24. Recognizing False Confidence
text:
Project transfer
This chapter feeds milestone M0, your research-and-AI baseline, presented at Friday’s studio. Two moves carry over. First, adopt a standing rule for your own project: no AI-reported number reaches your poster or brief until you have recomputed it by a second method. Second, log the catch in your AI Research Ledger, recording the task, the tool, your decision (kept the verified 15 percent, dropped the claimed 40), and the verification method (direct calculation). Then name, in your baseline, the one place you are most likely to let a confident answer past unchecked. That admission is where automation bias gets managed.

title: 24. Recognizing False Confidence
text:
Defend your decision
In a short paragraph, take one number an AI reported to you and state the claim you will defend after recomputing it: the bounded finding, in your own words. Then give its boundary and your uncertainty. What does the verified number not establish (does your design let you credit a cause, or only describe a change?), and where were you most tempted to trust the confident version without checking? A finding stated with no boundary and no uncertainty is the overclaiming-certainty defect, not a defense.

title: 22. Diagnostics and Negative Tests
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 22. Diagnostics and Negative Tests
text:
Why this decision matters
