“Before you tell me the extract works, show me the plate with no extract on it. If the blank disk also cleared the bacteria, you have not discovered a drug. You have discovered that your solvent is toxic.” — a microbiology lab advisor, on the first plate she asks to see

A striking result is the easiest thing in the world to produce by accident. Your procedure has many moving parts: how you handled the samples, how you measured, how you coded the groups. Any one of them can manufacture a signal that has nothing to do with the thing you set out to study. A negative test is how you find out whether the world produced your result or your own pipeline did. Skip it, and you may spend a semester defending an artifact.

title: 22. Diagnostics and Negative Tests
text:
The concept
A negative test is a check that runs your exact analysis on a situation where the true answer has to be zero, so any signal it returns is coming from your procedure, not the phenomenon. Example: measuring your “effect” in a group that received no treatment at all. Because the honest answer there is zero, a non-zero reading exposes a flaw in your machinery. Three members of this family show up in almost every audit.

Placebo test: you replace the real cause with a fake one that cannot act, and confirm the effect disappears. Example: swap the real treatment and control labels for a coin flip, and check that the estimate collapses toward zero.
Falsification test: you check a consequence that must be false if your explanation is right, and confirm it is false. Example: your compound can only act after you add it, so a reading taken before you added it should show nothing.
Negative control: you point the same machinery at an outcome your cause could not possibly touch. Example: an antibiotic should not change the diameter of the agar dish, so a “shrinking dish” reading would mean the measurement, not the drug, is talking.

One term earns its own line. A vehicle control is the negative control matched to how you delivered the cause: the carrier with the active ingredient left out. Example: if you dissolved your extract in ethanol, the vehicle control is a disk soaked in ethanol alone. What every one of these tests hunts for is an artifact, a signal produced by your procedure rather than by the thing you study. Example: a clear ring caused by the solvent, not the extract.
They share one move: aim the machinery where the answer must be zero, and make sure it says zero. Here is the boundary you must never blur. A passed negative test rules out one specific artifact. It does not prove your effect is real, and it never turns a correlation into a cause.

title: 22. Diagnostics and Negative Tests
text:
A worked example
You are testing whether an extract from a medicinal plant kills a common bacterium. You soak a small paper disk in the extract, lay it on a lawn of bacteria growing on an agar plate, incubate overnight, and next morning measure the zone of inhibition, the clear ring where bacteria failed to grow around the disk. A wide ring looks like a real antibacterial effect, and it is tempting to write that down.
Before you do, you run the negative test. You dissolved the extract in 70% ethanol, so your vehicle control is a disk soaked in 70% ethanol alone, placed on an identical plate and treated exactly the same way. If that disk leaves a bare, uninhibited lawn, the test passed: ethanol on its own did nothing, so the ring around your extract disk is not a solvent artifact, and your effect survives one specific worry. If the vehicle disk grows its own clear ring, the test failed. The ethanol is killing the bacteria, and you can no longer tell how much of the extract’s ring is the extract and how much is the solvent. The finding is an artifact until you redo it at a lower concentration.
Notice what a clean pass still does not buy you. It does not tell you the extract works inside a living animal, or which molecule is responsible. It ruled out one artifact and nothing more, and that modesty is the point.

title: 22. Diagnostics and Negative Tests
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb09 — Share the research + attack the analysis. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. There you run a placebo test on a real survey estimate: you throw away the true grouping, replace it with a coin-flip label 500 times, and watch the fake gaps cluster tightly at zero while the real result sits far outside that cloud. Then a leave-one-out check shows you whether a single data point is quietly carrying the whole finding.

title: 22. Diagnostics and Negative Tests
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own expectation first, then delegate a checkable job. Never let the tool decide whether your control is valid.
Locate the standard control.
Act as a lab-methods assistant. I ran an antibiotic disk-diffusion assay with a
plant extract dissolved in 70% ethanol. Before any advice, name the standard
negative (vehicle) control for this assay and cite a protocol or textbook where it
appears. Only name controls you are confident are standard practice.
After running, verify: open the cited protocol and confirm the control is real and standard before you use it. Counters confident fabrication (an invented “standard” control arrives as confidently as a real one).
List candidate negative tests, then verify each.
Here is my analysis: [one-sentence design, your cause, your outcome]. Propose three
negative tests: a placebo label, a falsification outcome measured where the effect
cannot yet exist, and a negative-control outcome my cause could not plausibly affect.
For each, state the exact zero I should expect and what a non-zero result would mean.
After running, verify: for each test, confirm your cause genuinely cannot touch the negative control. A control your cause can affect is not null, and it fails silently. Counters plausible-but-wrong-method (a “negative control” that is not actually zero).
Red-team a clean pass.
Here is a negative test I ran and its result: [describe the control and its
near-zero outcome]. Act as a hostile reviewer. Name every reason this test could
read as zero for the WRONG reason: too few samples to detect anything, a control too
weak to carry the artifact, a zero I would have gotten no matter what. Do not reassure me.
After running, verify: if it only praises the clean result, push back and demand the single way the test is uninformative. Counters sycophantic agreement (praise that reviews your relief, not your evidence).






ImportantDo not delegate



You decide which negative test your design actually needs, whether the control is genuinely null (a place your cause truly cannot reach), and what a clean pass licenses you to claim. A tool can list candidate controls, but only you know the mechanism well enough to certify that your cause cannot touch the one you chose. The judgment that a passed negative test rules out an artifact without proving your effect is real stays yours.

title: 22. Diagnostics and Negative Tests
text:
An AI failure case
You ask an AI to design a negative control for your ethanol-based assay. It answers, with complete confidence, “use a disk soaked in sterile water.” You run it, the water disk comes back perfectly clean, and the tidy conclusion writes itself: control passed, effect confirmed. Here is the trap. Water is not the vehicle you used. You delivered the extract in ethanol, so the artifact you needed to rule out is ethanol toxicity, and a water disk can never show it. The clean result ruled out nothing at all.
You catch it with one question the tool never asked itself: what artifact does this control actually rule out? A valid vehicle control matches how you delivered the cause, solvent for solvent. The moment you see the mismatch you swap water for 70% ethanol and run the test that can genuinely fail. This is a plausible-but-wrong-method: the advice sounded like textbook practice and was wrong for your case.

title: 22. Diagnostics and Negative Tests
text:
Verification laboratory
Verify this chapter’s key result, that a genuine negative test returns zero, with simulation from the verification guide. Build a dataset by hand where no effect exists: assign a fake label by coin flip and generate an outcome that ignores it entirely. Run your negative-test machinery on it. If the procedure is sound, the estimate comes back near zero, again and again across many random labels. If it returns a signal on data you built to hold none, the test itself is broken, not the world. A negative test you have confirmed returns zero on known-null data is one you can trust when it returns zero on your real data.

title: 22. Diagnostics and Negative Tests
text:
Project transfer
This chapter adds one pre-listed check to your M9 research audit, the graded record of how hard you tried to break your own claim. Before you look at your result, write down one negative test your design calls for, name the exact zero you expect, and commit to reporting it whichever way it comes out. Run it, record what you got against what you predicted, and if the estimate moved, say by how much. That row, the negative test you chose plus the zero you expected plus the result, is one of the strongest lines you can walk a skeptic through at Friday’s studio.

title: 22. Diagnostics and Negative Tests
text:
Defend your decision
In four sentences, defend the negative test you chose for your own result. Name the test and the exact zero you expected before you ran it. State what a clean pass licenses you to claim, and its boundary: the one artifact it rules out, and the causal or general claim it does not establish. Close with your uncertainty: whether the test was powerful enough to have caught the artifact if it were really there, and the one check you would run next.

title: 20. AI as Analytical Assistant
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 20. AI as Analytical Assistant
text:
Why this decision matters

“I do not care that a model ran your robustness checks. I care which ones you chose, which numbers you re-derived yourself, and which flags you confirmed against the data. Show me that trail and I trust your judgment. Show me the model’s transcript and I trust neither.” — a reviewer for a human-computer interaction venue, reading a usability study’s results section

An AI tool can now write your analysis code, run a dozen robustness checks, and hand you a clean table in seconds. That speed is exactly why a loose habit is dangerous. Paste the table into your poster and you have signed your name to numbers you never checked. The reviewer above wants the short list of decisions that stayed yours. This chapter gives you the habit that survives her question.

title: 20. AI as Analytical Assistant
text:
The concept
An AI analytical assistant is an AI tool you hand a well-specified analytical task, never your judgment. Example: you tell it “recompute this time gap after dropping the three most experienced users” and it writes and runs that one check. The task is checkable. The verdict is not its job.
The unit you attack is your headline estimate, the single number that stands in for your whole finding. Example: “users of the new interface finished about 0.7 standard deviations faster.” Before you defend that number, you try to break it, and this is the labor you delegate.
Two attacks do most of the work. A robustness check re-runs the same finding under a different but equally defensible choice and asks whether the answer holds. Example: you reported a mean completion time, so you also compute the median and check the story survives. A placebo test runs your exact analysis where the effect cannot exist and confirms it returns near zero. Example: you shuffle the “new interface / old interface” labels at random and check the fake gap collapses to nothing.
Here is the division of labor. The assistant proposes checks, writes the code, and prints the numbers. You decide which checks count, and you verify every number before it reaches your claim. A tool that ran without an error has not proven its result is correct. You verify the number, not the paragraph about the number.

title: 20. AI as Analytical Assistant
text:
A worked example
You are studying whether a command palette (a searchable menu you open with one keystroke) helps users format a document faster than the old nested menus. Your headline estimate: palette users finished about 0.7 standard deviations faster. You use an analytical assistant to attack it.
Delegate. You ask the assistant to recompute the gap across a small grid: all participants versus dropping the three who had used a command palette before (the sample), mean versus median completion time (the measurement), and the raw gap versus one adjusted for each user’s baseline typing speed (the specification).
Inspect and verify. The assistant returns eight numbers. You do not trust them yet. You recompute two rows by hand with a second, simpler expression, and they match. The direction holds across all eight, and the magnitude ranges from about 0.5 to 0.9 standard deviations. Your honest headline is a direction plus a range, not one flattering number.
Placebo and red-team. You ask the assistant to shuffle the palette labels and re-run. The fake gap comes back near zero, so the machinery is not manufacturing your effect. Then you ask it, as a hostile reviewer, for the single worst flaw. It says with total confidence that experienced users drive the result. You already dropped them in the sample check and the gap held, so the data refute that flag. The flaw it missed is the one that matters: completion time is not user satisfaction, so your claim must stay about speed, not preference.
The assistant did the arithmetic. You decided which checks counted and which flags the data confirmed.

title: 20. AI as Analytical Assistant
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb09 — Share the research and attack the analysis. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. There you delegate a real robustness grid, a placebo test, and an influence check to an assistant, then adjudicate three confident AI reviewers against the data and watch two of their three flags dissolve the moment a check touches them.

title: 20. AI as Analytical Assistant
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own expectation first, then delegate. Each prompt is a checkable job, not a request for a verdict.
Propose the checks you might have missed.
Here is my robustness grid for one estimate: I vary the sample (all users /
drop prior palette users), the measurement (mean / median time), and the
specification (raw gap / typing-speed-adjusted). Act as a hostile reviewer.
Name up to three additional, equally defensible checks I did NOT list, and
for each say which direction you expect it to move the estimate.
After running, verify: check each proposal against your four handles and confirm you can actually compute it in your data. Counters illusion of completeness (a tidy grid that still omits the one check that would break the claim).
Delegate the computation, keep the numbers checkable.
Recompute my time gap under each row of this grid and return a table:
row label, the gap, and the exact columns and filter used for that row.
Do not summarize; show one number and its code path per row.
After running, verify: recompute two rows by hand with a second expression and confirm they match the table. Counters plausible-but-wrong-method (a row whose label and code quietly disagree).
Red-team the surviving claim.
Here is my claim: "the palette is about 0.7 SD faster." Act as a hostile
reviewer of HCI studies. Name the single worst way this result could
mislead, and the exact data check that would confirm or kill it. Do not
rewrite my claim.
After running, verify: run the check it names, and treat the flaw as real only if your own output confirms it. Counters sycophantic agreement (praise that reviews your ego, not your evidence).






ImportantDo not delegate



Three calls never leave your hands. You decide which checks count for the claim you want to make, which flagged problems the data actually confirm, and the final claim you defend, with its boundary and its range. The assistant proposes and computes. The evidence decides, and you are the one who reads the evidence.

title: 20. AI as Analytical Assistant
text:
An AI failure case
You hand an assistant your grid and it returns a polished eight-row table, every row near 0.7, and declares the result “fully robust.” The run threw no error. Here is the trap: its “median” rows silently call the same mean function as the “mean” rows, so four of the eight numbers are duplicates wearing different labels. The table agrees with itself because it never varied the handle it claims to vary. This is the illusion of completeness, a thorough-looking output missing the one thing that matters, shading into confident fabrication, a number stated with certainty that never came from the code path it names.
You catch it by recomputing one “median” row by hand. The true median gap is 0.55, not 0.70. The grid overstated how much your choices agreed. A green check is not a correct result.

title: 20. AI as Analytical Assistant
text:
Verification laboratory
Verify this chapter’s key result, the surviving robustness of your headline gap, with alternative code from the verification guide: recompute the same gap a second way and compare. Filter each group by hand, subtract the two means yourself, and confirm you land on the assistant’s number. Then add a second, independent road with a falsification test, the placebo: shuffle the group labels, re-run, and confirm the fake gap sits at zero. Two roads to one answer is a verified result. Two roads to two answers means you measured the assistant’s code, not your effect.

title: 20. AI as Analytical Assistant
text:
Project transfer
This chapter feeds your M9 poster draft and research audit, the graded record of how hard you tried to break your own claim and what survived. Take your own M8 estimate, delegate its robustness grid and a placebo to an assistant, and verify every number before it enters the audit. For each AI use, record the task you delegated, the number the tool returned, the named check that earned your trust, and the one flag you caught the tool getting wrong. That verified trail, plus your surviving headline stated with its range, is what you walk a skeptic through at Friday’s studio.

title: 20. AI as Analytical Assistant
text:
Defend your decision
In four sentences, defend one analytical task you handed an assistant. Name the check you delegated and the one number you re-derived yourself before you believed it. State the bounded claim you can now make, with its boundary (what the check does not establish) and its range (how far the estimate moved across your defensible choices). Close with your uncertainty: the one flaw no check could settle, and the verification you would run next to close it.

title: 18. Measurement and Operationalization
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 18. Measurement and Operationalization
text:
Why this decision matters

“Before you tell me the cover crop made the soil healthier, tell me exactly what you put a number on. ‘Healthier’ is a word. I need to know which measurement stands behind it, and what that measurement quietly ignores.” — a field agronomist reading the first draft of a soil study

Every result you report answers a question about some idea: soil health, drought stress, yield quality. But you never measure the idea. You measure a number that stands in for it, and your finding is only as trustworthy as that swap. If you cannot say which number you recorded and what it misses, the agronomist above has no reason to believe your headline. This chapter gives you the move that makes the swap honest.

title: 18. Measurement and Operationalization
text:
The concept
Operationalization is the act of turning an abstract idea into a specific, repeatable measurement procedure. Example: turning “soil health” into “the percent organic matter you get by drying and burning a soil sample.” You do it by climbing down a three-rung ladder, and each rung has a name.

Concept: the abstract idea your question is really about. Example: soil health. A concept is rich and fuzzy, and you cannot measure it directly.
Construct: the one specific, named facet of the concept you decide to measure. Example: soil organic matter content, the fraction of the soil made of decomposed plant and animal material. You pick a construct because it is concrete enough to attach a procedure to.
Indicator: the exact number a procedure produces. Example: percent organic matter by loss-on-ignition, a standard method where you dry a sample, weigh it, burn off the organic material in a furnace, weigh it again, and report the mass lost as a percentage.

Two questions decide whether the swap holds. Measurement validity asks whether your indicator actually captures the construct it claims to. Example: does percent organic matter really stand for soil health, or does it miss compaction and pH? Measurement reliability asks whether measuring the same thing twice gives you the same reading. Example: you split one soil sample into two, run both, and check that the numbers agree. An indicator can be reliable and still invalid: a scale reads your weight the same way five times in a row, yet weight is a poor indicator of the construct “fitness.”

title: 18. Measurement and Operationalization
text:
A worked example
You want to answer a real farm question: is the cover-cropped field healthier than the bare field next to it? Watch the ladder do its work.
You start at the concept, soil health, and admit right away that no instrument reads “health.” So you drop to a construct. Of the several honest facets of soil health, you choose soil organic matter content, because a higher organic fraction holds water and feeds soil life. Now you name the indicator: percent organic matter by loss-on-ignition, measured on ten samples from each field.
Before you trust a single reading, you check reliability. You split three samples in two and run each half. The paired readings land within a few tenths of a percent, so the procedure is repeatable. Then you check validity by naming what the indicator ignores. Organic matter says nothing about compaction, pH, or the microbes in the soil. It is one true facet, not the whole of soil health.
That honesty sets your claim boundary. You can defend this: “the cover-cropped plots averaged higher soil organic matter than the bare plots, one facet of soil health, measured reliably by loss-on-ignition.” You cannot defend “the cover crop made the soil healthier,” because you never measured the facets your indicator skips. The number is real, and its meaning stops exactly where the construct stops.

title: 18. Measurement and Operationalization
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb04 — Observational descriptive research. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. There you build a concept → construct → indicator ladder for your own key measure, and you watch a single indicator flatten reality firsthand: a survey’s mean of 4.43 sits in a dip between two peaks, so the average describes almost no real respondent. An index is an operationalization, and every operationalization hides what it averages over.

title: 18. Measurement and Operationalization
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own draft first, then delegate. Each prompt is a checkable job, not a verdict to trust.
Locate the standard indicators.
Act as a soil-science assistant. I want to measure the construct "soil organic
matter content" for a field study. Name the standard, published indicators
agronomists use for this construct, with the method name and a real source I can
open. Only list methods you are confident exist; mark anything uncertain.
After running, verify: open one named source and confirm the method exists and measures organic matter. Counters confident fabrication (an invented method name arrives as fluently as a real one).
List the limits, so you can verify them.
Here is my operationalization: concept = soil health, construct = organic matter,
indicator = percent by loss-on-ignition. Return a table of every facet of soil
health this indicator does NOT capture, one row each, with why it matters.
After running, verify: check the table against a real soil-health framework you retrieve yourself. Counters illusion of completeness (a tidy list that still omits the facet that most threatens your claim).
Red-team the swap.
Act as a hostile thesis reviewer. Here is my claim: "the cover-cropped field is
healthier, because it has more organic matter." Name every place my indicator
fails to support the word "healthier." Do not rewrite the claim for me.
After running, verify: if it only praises the design, push back and demand the single worst gap. Counters sycophantic agreement (praise that reviews your ego, not your measurement).






ImportantDo not delegate



Which concept your question is about, which construct honestly stands for it, and where your indicator’s meaning must stop are yours alone. The tool can list methods and name limitations, but only you decide that organic matter is a fair stand-in for the health facet you actually care about, and only you write the sentence that refuses to call one facet the whole concept. Naming what your number leaves out is the researcher’s job, not the model’s.

title: 18. Measurement and Operationalization
text:
An AI failure case
You ask, “operationalize soil health for my field study,” and the tool answers with total confidence: “Measure soil organic matter by loss-on-ignition. That is soil health.” The recipe is clean, correct-sounding, and runs without complaint. Here is the trap. Soil health is a broad concept with several independent facets, and the tool silently narrowed it to one construct, then handed you the indicator as if it were the whole idea. This is a scope change wearing the costume of a complete answer.
You catch it by reading the answer’s claim next to your question, word for word. Your question was about “soil health”; the answer is about “organic matter,” and those are not the same box. Then you confirm it with a real source: open a recognized soil-health framework and find the several facets the recipe never mentioned. A confident recipe is not a valid operationalization. You verify the construct, not the fluency of the paragraph that proposed it.

title: 18. Measurement and Operationalization
text:
Verification laboratory
Verify this chapter’s key result, that your indicator faithfully stands for its construct, with primary-source reading from the verification guide: find the recognized method for your construct, open it, and confirm it measures what you claim before you cite it. Then add a second, independent check for reliability: measure the same thing twice and compare, the way you split one soil sample and confirm the two halves agree. Two roads to one number is a reliable indicator. In the lab, do the same by direct calculation: recompute the distribution behind that mean of 4.43 and see how much shape the single number hid.

title: 18. Measurement and Operationalization
text:
Project transfer
This chapter becomes the measurement rung of your M4 observational descriptive design audit, which asks for a measurement ladder and a coverage note. Draft it here: write your own concept → construct → indicator for your project’s key measure, one line each, then name the one facet your indicator most worries you by leaving out. Bring that ladder, plus a one-sentence reliability and validity note, to Friday’s studio. It is the piece that lets your later population claim mean something, because a reach claim about a badly-measured construct reaches nowhere.

title: 18. Measurement and Operationalization
text:
Defend your decision
In four sentences, defend one operationalization from your own project. Name the concept, the construct you chose to stand for it, and the exact indicator you will record. State the bounded claim your indicator licenses, worded to stop where the construct stops. Name what the indicator leaves out and why it is still the honest choice. Close with your uncertainty: the reliability or validity limit you have not yet checked, and the one measurement you would run next to close it.

title: 16. Hybrid and Complex Designs
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 16. Hybrid and Complex Designs
text:
Why this decision matters

“Do not hand me the headline number. Hand me the error budget: every quantity you measured, how you measured it, and how each one’s uncertainty flowed into the final answer. A precise result built on one uncharacterized step is a precise wrong answer.” — an experimental physicist reviewing a lab report that claims to have measured a constant

A complex design fails in a quiet way. Each piece looks careful on its own, so the whole looks careful too. But an answer assembled from several measurements is only as trustworthy as its worst-characterized piece, and the assembly can amplify a small mistake into a large one. This chapter gives you the discipline to take such a design apart and diagnose the whole before you trust its number.

title: 16. Hybrid and Complex Designs
text:
The concept
Every research design has four parts, and the course names them MIDA: the Model (your written picture of how the world could work), the Inquiry (the one exact quantity you want from it), the Data strategy (how the data come to exist), and the Answer strategy (how you turn that data into an estimate). The Colab lab builds all four from scratch. This chapter takes them somewhere harder.
A complex, or hybrid, design is a research design whose four MIDA parts have more than one moving piece: several measurements, stages, or sub-questions stitched into a single answer. Example: computing a physical constant from two separate measurements, each with its own instrument and its own error.
Two ideas decide whether such a design holds together. The first is alignment, meaning all four parts point at the same single quantity. Complexity breaks it in a fresh way: two carefully built sub-measurements can each be fine yet quietly target slightly different quantities that never combine into the one you named. The second is error propagation, meaning each measured input’s uncertainty flows into the final answer, sometimes amplified. Example: if your answer depends on the square of a measured time, a 1% error in that time becomes roughly a 2% error in the result.
Keep the lab’s two failure words close. Bias is a systematic tilt that more data does not shrink; variance is run-to-run wobble that more data does. Complexity lets a small systematic error hide behind an impressively small random one.

title: 16. Hybrid and Complex Designs
text:
A worked example
You want the local acceleration due to gravity, call it g, from a swinging pendulum. Your model is the small-angle pendulum, whose period obeys T = 2π√(L/g) and rearranges to the estimand you will report: g = 4π²L / T². That single formula is a complex design in miniature. It stitches two sub-measurements into one answer: the length L you read off a ruler, and the period T you time with a stopwatch.
Watch the anatomy. Timing one swing is noisy, because your reaction time adds a random error to each start and stop. So you make a smart data-strategy move: time 50 swings and divide by 50. That averaging crushes the variance, and your period looks beautifully repeatable.
Here is the trap the lab trains you to see. Repetition does nothing for bias. Let the pendulum swing wide and the small-angle model behind T = 2π√(L/g) no longer holds, so the period you measure runs systematically longer than the formula assumes. Your data strategy and your model have drifted apart, and because T is squared, that drift is amplified straight into g. A value of g that reads 9.72 and never wobbles is not a correct g, only a confidently wrong one. The fix is a redesign of the data strategy, swinging at a small amplitude so the model holds again, not one more round of timing.

title: 16. Hybrid and Complex Designs
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb03 — The anatomy of a research design: MIDA + declare → diagnose → redesign. Open it in Colab with the badge above. There you build a small simulated world, declare a design as something a computer can run, diagnose it by simulating it thousands of times to read its bias, variance, and power, then redesign the one part that most improves it. The lab runs that loop on a causal social question; this chapter shows the same anatomy holds when the design is complex.

title: 16. Hybrid and Complex Designs
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt is a checkable job, not a request for a verdict.
Locate the real rule.
Act as a physics-lab assistant. For a simple pendulum timed to estimate g via
g = 4π²L/T², name two things and cite a real, retrievable source for each: (1) the
amplitude below which the small-angle approximation holds to about 1%, and (2) the
standard rule for propagating the uncertainty in L and T into g. Only state values
you are confident exist.
After running, verify: open the cited source and confirm the amplitude threshold and the propagation rule actually appear there. Counters confident fabrication (an invented threshold arrives as confidently as a real one).
Decompose the design into pieces you can check.
Here is my combined design: [paste your model, inquiry, and how you measure L and T].
Break it into sub-designs. For each sub-measurement, name which MIDA part it lives in,
the one assumption it depends on, and how its error enters the final g. Return a table.
After running, verify: set the table beside your own decomposition. Any sub-design or assumption it skipped is exactly the gap to chase. Counters illusion of completeness (a tidy table that omits the one uncharacterized step).
Red-team the combined result.
Act as a hostile metrologist. Here is my measured g and its stated uncertainty:
[paste]. Name the single systematic error that more repetitions can never fix and the
place my four MIDA parts fail to point at the same quantity. Do not fix it for me.
After running, verify: if it only praises your precision, push back and demand the one worst systematic error. Counters sycophantic agreement (praise that reviews your ego, not your error budget).






ImportantDo not delegate



Three calls stay yours. You decide what single quantity your inquiry names (which g, under what conditions), whether each sub-design actually targets that same quantity so the parts align, and whether to trust a combined number your diagnosis says a systematic error dominates. A tool can propagate the arithmetic, but it cannot decide that a precise number is a correct one. That judgment is the whole point of the chapter, and it is yours.

title: 16. Hybrid and Complex Designs
text:
An AI failure case
You paste your L and T into an AI tool and ask for g. It returns, with total confidence, g = 9.79 ± 0.02 m/s². The number sits right next to the textbook 9.81, the interval is tiny, and the code ran without an error. It is wrong. The tool propagated only the random timing variance, the part you already crushed by timing 50 swings, and never modeled the systematic bias from your wide swing amplitude. That tight ± 0.02 describes wobble it could see and stays silent about the tilt it could not.
You catch it in two moves. First, check the assumption the number rests on: was the amplitude actually small enough for T = 2π√(L/g) to hold? Second, run a falsification move: redo the timing at a deliberately larger amplitude and watch the “precise” g drift systematically downward. A quantity that shifts well outside its own stated interval had an uncertainty that was never honest. Verify the number, not the tidy interval beside it.

title: 16. Hybrid and Complex Designs
text:
Verification laboratory
Verify the chapter’s key result, that a complex design’s precision does not prove it is unbiased, with simulation from the verification guide. Declare the combined design in code with a known g and a planted large-angle bias, run it many times, and read the two numbers apart: variance shrinks as you add swings, while bias stays exactly where the amplitude put it. Then add a second, independent check, direct calculation: recompute g by hand from your L and T. Two roads to one answer is a verified result; two roads to two answers means the pipeline, not the pendulum, is what you measured.

title: 16. Hybrid and Complex Designs
text:
Project transfer
This chapter deepens your M3 research charter and MIDA declaration, the three-minute declaration you present at Friday’s studio. If your own project’s design is complex, meaning it draws on more than one data source, runs a two-stage answer strategy, or blends a descriptive sub-question with a causal one, do not declare it as a single black box. Decompose it into its sub-designs, confirm each aligns to your single inquiry, and diagnose the whole rather than each piece alone. Bring that decomposition and one named redesign to the studio as the diagnosis-and-redesign part of your declaration.

title: 16. Hybrid and Complex Designs
text:
Defend your decision
In four sentences, defend one complex design. Name the sub-designs it stitches together and the single inquiry all of them must align to. State the bounded claim the whole design licenses, and say whether one sub-measurement’s systematic error dominates the result. Close with your uncertainty: the sub-measurement whose bias you have not yet ruled out, and the one check you would run next to close it.

title: 14. Prediction and Generalization
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 14. Prediction and Generalization
text:
Why this decision matters
Picture a county public-health officer who must decide, each Friday, whether to post a swimming advisory at a lake. A vendor arrives with a model that flags harmful algal blooms “with 94% accuracy.”

“Ninety-four percent compared to WHAT? Most weeks this lake is fine, so if I just say ‘no bloom’ every single week I am already right about eighty-five percent of the time. If your model cannot clearly beat that, and prove it did so on weeks it never studied, I am not closing a public beach on its say-so.” — a public-health officer who has been burned by an impressive-sounding number

That officer asks the two questions this chapter trains you to answer before you trust any forecast. Is it better than the dumbest honest rule, and was it earned on cases the model never saw? Get those wrong and you cry wolf or miss a real hazard.

title: 14. Prediction and Generalization
text:
The concept
A prediction is a best guess about a case whose outcome you cannot see yet. Example: forecasting whether a lake blooms next week, before next week exists. Prediction is descriptive, not causal. It forecasts what will happen, never why.
What makes prediction its own question is its reach, the cases a claim is meant to cover. Prediction’s reach is unseen cases, units not in your data whose outcome is still unknown, such as next month’s samples. Its cousin is generalization, reach from your sample to a broader population, such as from the twelve lakes you measured to every lake in the watershed. Both go beyond the data in hand, and both are honest only when you can name the crossing that licenses them.
A forecast earns trust under a four-part contract, in fixed order. The target is the one column you predict, for example bloom_next_week. The baseline is the dumbest honest rule you must beat, usually “always guess the most common answer”; if 85% of weeks have no bloom, that rule scores 85% for free, so 85% is the bar. The split divides rows into a training set the model learns from and a held-out set, rows kept back during training and used only as its exam, say a random quarter of the weeks. The metric is how you keep score, matched to the target; accuracy misleads when blooms are rare, so recall on the bloom class, the share of real blooms the model catches, often matters more.
One trap sits above all others. Data leakage is a feature carrying information you would not have at the forecast moment, because its value is settled only at or after the outcome. Example: the toxin concentration measured during the bloom. A leaked feature inflates your score on today’s data and collapses on tomorrow’s, because it will not exist when you truly need the forecast.

title: 14. Prediction and Generalization
text:
A worked example
Suppose you want to warn swimmers a week ahead. Your target is bloom_next_week, one if the lake exceeds the advisory threshold and zero otherwise. Across three summers of weekly samples, 85% of weeks are clear, so your baseline, “always say no bloom,” scores 0.85 with no model at all.
You keep features genuinely known a week early: water temperature, recent rainfall, upstream phosphorus load. You fit a logistic model (a standard yes-or-no classifier), split off a held-out quarter of the weeks, and score both only on those held-out weeks. The model lands around 0.88. That four-point edge feels small, and its smallness is the honest result: a modest true win beats a dramatic fake one.
Then a well-meaning collaborator adds chlorophyll_a, the pigment reading. Held-out accuracy leaps to 0.97. Exciting, until you ask when chlorophyll is measured: during the very bloom you are forecasting. It is the outcome wearing a different label. Drop it, and the score falls back to 0.88. The timing test, not the accuracy, decides. And because you trained on one shallow lake, you name the boundary out loud: on a deep, cold reservoir the pattern may not hold, so the forecast does not yet generalize there.

title: 14. Prediction and Generalization
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb07 — Prediction: generalizing to unseen cases. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. In the lab you sign the four-part contract on a real dataset, score an honest baseline first, then build a leak on purpose so you feel a fake score climb and learn to hunt it by correlation and timing before you retrain clean.

title: 14. Prediction and Generalization
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Paste real output, never a number you remember.
Act as a Python tutor. Here is a cell that holds out 25% of my weekly
lake samples, scores a majority-class baseline on them, then scores a
logistic model on the SAME held-out weeks: [paste cell]. Explain, line
by line, what the split does and why it must happen before any fitting.
Then give me one independent way to confirm the held-out weeks were
never in training.
After running, verify: read the baseline, model, and margin off your own printout and confirm the tool’s numbers match, not ones it guessed. Counters confident fabrication, a fluent walkthrough quoting a margin your code never produced.
Here is the outcome I want to predict and the moment I need the forecast:
a bloom next week, decided by next week's samples. Here is my candidate
feature list with when each value is recorded: [list]. For each feature,
say whether its value is settled before, at, or after the outcome, and
flag any that could not exist at prediction time.
After running, verify: re-derive the timing of the one feature it clears most confidently. If any feature is settled at or after the bloom, reject it whatever the tool says. Counters illusion of completeness, a tidy list omitting the one late-settled feature that dooms the forecast.
Act as a hostile reviewer. Attack this headline: "Our model forecasts
blooms with 88% held-out accuracy." Name every reason a skeptic would
distrust it, including the baseline it beat, the metric, and any hidden
leak.
After running, verify: check each objection against your printout and keep the ones your numbers support. Counters sycophantic agreement, where an assistant primed to help praises a headline you wrote.






ImportantDo not delegate



Four calls stay yours. The target: what you forecast and why it matters. The baseline: the honest rule the model must beat. The timing of every feature: whether a value would truly exist at the forecast moment, which only you, who knows your data’s timeline, can settle. And the verdict on whether your project should predict at all. A tool that has never seen your timeline cannot make these calls for you.

title: 14. Prediction and Generalization
text:
An AI failure case
You paste your feature list and ask an AI partner which features are safe to use. It answers with confidence: “chlorophyll_a is your strongest predictor, keep it.” The reasoning sounds airtight, because chlorophyll correlates almost perfectly with blooms. That near-perfect correlation is exactly the tell. This is the plausible-but-wrong-method failure: the tool ranked the feature on how well it fits the past, not on whether it could exist in time for a forecast. You catch it with one question statistics alone cannot answer. When is chlorophyll measured? During the bloom, so no forecast made a week earlier could have it. Drop it, watch the score fall back to 0.88, and keep the honest model.

title: 14. Prediction and Generalization
text:
Verification laboratory
Verify the chapter’s key result with a holdout sample, the verification-guide method for any fitted model: never report a score computed on rows the model trained on. Confirm the split is real by checking that no held-out week appears in the training set, a one-line direct calculation on the row indices. Then falsify the leak: refit with the suspect feature removed and confirm the score drops back to the honest held-out number. If pulling one feature collapses your accuracy, that feature was doing work a real forecast cannot.

title: 14. Prediction and Generalization
text:
Project transfer
This chapter builds the spine of your M7 declared analysis protocol, the plan for how you will analyze your data, fixed before you touch it. Draft four pieces for your project: the prediction verdict (target, baseline, split, metric, in that order, or an honest “prediction is the wrong question, and here is why”), the feature most likely to leak with the timing check that rules it out, the honesty checks you will run before reporting, and a one-sentence claim boundary. The prediction-and-leakage auditor tests exactly these at Friday’s studio.

title: 14. Prediction and Generalization
text:
Defend your decision
State and defend the decision this chapter equips. Name your target and the baseline it must beat. State the compass position out loud: prediction, descriptive kind, unseen-cases reach, with no causal reading of any model weight. Then name the edge of what you can claim: your uncertainty (the spread you see when you repeat the held-out split several times, a check called cross-validation), your leakage risk, and the population where your forecast may stop generalizing. A forecast reported without its boundary and its uncertainty is not yet defensible.

title: 12. Observational Causal Research
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 12. Observational Causal Research
text:
Why this decision matters
Picture a thesis advisor reading your first draft. You wrote, “the treated group did better, so the treatment worked.” Her reply is the sentence you will hear for the rest of your research life: “After is not because. Show me the world where they were not treated, or admit you are guessing.” Almost every question worth asking forbids the clean experiment. You cannot assign a person a disease, a country a war, or a wild animal a gut microbe. So the honest researcher’s real craft is deciding, out loud and in writing, when an observed comparison is trustworthy enough to carry a cause. Get this wrong and a paper claims more than its data support. Get it right and a modest, bounded claim becomes credible.

title: 12. Observational Causal Research
text:
The concept
Start with the missing world.

Counterfactual: the outcome that would have happened under the choice that was not made. Example: how long a coffee drinker would have lived if she had not drunk coffee. You never see it, because she did drink coffee.

A causal claim is a claim about a counterfactual. The trouble is a villain that hides inside almost every observational comparison.

Confounder: a third factor that pushes on both who gets the treatment and the outcome. Example: a health-conscious lifestyle raises both the chance of a daily coffee habit and lifespan, so coffee drinkers look longer-lived even if coffee did nothing.

You can draw the trouble. A causal diagram is a picture of arrows where each arrow means “this directly influences that.” In it, the confounder creates a back door: a sneaky path linking treatment and outcome that runs through the confounder instead of through any real effect. A raw comparison sees both paths at once and blames the treatment for the confounder’s work.
Two moves close the back door without an experiment.

Selection on observables: identifying an effect by adjusting for every confounder you can measure, so the treated and untreated match on all of them. Example: compare coffee drinkers and non-drinkers within the same lifestyle group. It works only for confounders you can actually see.
Natural experiment: a situation where a chance-like force outside anyone’s control decided who got treated, making assignment as-if random (credibly like a coin flip). Example: a visa lottery decides who makes a trip, so winners and losers differ only by luck.

Whichever move you use, you owe a reader an identification argument: your written reason that the comparison recovers a real effect, resting on one untestable assumption the data can never confirm, only make plausible. When you cannot make that argument honestly, you have hit the causal-language boundary, the point past which your evidence stops earning because and must switch to associated with. The most common failure here has a name too. Design mimicry is borrowing the vocabulary (“natural experiment,” “difference-in-differences”) to decorate a comparison whose assumption was never argued. The word is not the argument.

title: 12. Observational Causal Research
text:
A worked example
Bring this into a biology lab. You study wild-derived mice and notice a striking pattern: individuals whose gut carries a particular fiber-digesting bacterium are, on average, leaner than mice without it. The headline writes itself: “the microbe causes leanness.” Would you stake a thesis on it?
Draw three boxes first: microbe, leanness, and a third box for anything that could drive both. That third box fills in fast. Diet is a confounder. Mice that forage on high-fiber plants both harbor more of the fiber-digesting bacterium and stay leaner for reasons unrelated to the microbe. Diet points into both boxes, opening a back door, so a naive microbe-versus-no-microbe comparison mostly measures the diet gap and credits it to the bacterium. A colony of ten thousand mice would make that wrong number beautifully precise and no less wrong, because a larger sample shrinks noise, not confounding.
Now the fix. If you recorded each mouse’s diet, use selection on observables: compare carriers to non-carriers within the high-fiber group, then within the low-fiber group, and average the two honest within-diet differences. If the gap collapses toward zero, the microbe was mostly a passenger of diet. The catch is honest: this works only for the confounder you measured. If mice also differ in an unrecorded trait, say a temperament that drives both foraging and metabolism, that trait keeps the back door open and “we controlled for diet” is not identification. Then your defensible claim is that the microbe is associated with leanness, and the causal question waits for a real intervention, such as colonizing genetically identical, identically fed mice. That boundary is what this chapter teaches you to see and to say.

title: 12. Observational Causal Research
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb05 — Observational causal research. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. In the lab you build a small world where you set coffee’s true effect, plant a hidden confounder, and watch a naive comparison inflate a true +1.0 to about +3.1, then recover the truth by adjusting for the confounder you can see. You then analyze a real natural experiment, the Hajj visa lottery from Clingingsmith, Khwaja and Kremer (RDSS ch. 16), as a difference in means and state exactly what its as-if-random assignment lets you claim, and over whom.

title: 12. Observational Causal Research
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first every time, then delegate.
Locate a real design (commit your own guess at the leverage first).
Act as a research librarian in biology. Find peer-reviewed observational studies
that used a natural experiment or a discontinuity to estimate the causal effect of
a gut microbe on a host trait. For each: title, authors, year, venue, and the
as-if-random source. Only include work you are confident exists; mark anything
uncertain.
After running, verify (counters confident fabrication): open each source and read the line yourself before citing it — primary-source reading. Cut any you cannot find.
List the confounders to verify (commit your own list first).
I claim a gut microbe causes leanness in wild mice. List traits that plausibly raise
both microbe presence and leanness, in a table with the trait and the direction it
would bias a naive comparison. Then, as a hostile reviewer, name the one trait you
are most likely wrong to include and the important one you left out.
After running, verify (counters illusion of completeness): compare the list to the one you committed first, and map each trait to a back-door path (trait → microbe, trait → leanness). A trait with only one arrow is not a confounder.
Red-team the causal sentence you wrote (write the sentence yourself, do not let AI draft it).
Here is a causal sentence I wrote: "[paste your sentence]." Act as a hostile peer
reviewer. Name every place it over-reaches or claims more than my observational
design identifies. Do not rewrite it for me; list the weaknesses so I fix them.
After running, verify (counters sycophantic agreement): if the objections are all mild, push back — “assume the sentence is wrong; name the single worst flaw.” Praise without an objection is a red flag, not a grade.






ImportantDo not delegate



Three decisions stay yours alone. Whether your design identifies a causal effect is a judgment about how treatment was really assigned, and only you know that. Which confounder most threatens your comparison turns on the mechanism, which a model cannot see in your data. And whether your finding earns because or must stop at associated with is the whole skill of the week. A tool will happily call your comparison a “natural experiment” because you typed the words. Earning because is your signature, not the model’s.

title: 12. Observational Causal Research
text:
An AI failure case
You paste your mouse comparison into a chatbot and ask, “Is this a natural experiment, and can I say the microbe causes leanness?” It answers with a confident, well-formatted yes: your setup “resembles a natural experiment,” the difference “can be read causally,” the write-up “looks rigorous.” This is two named failures at once: plausible-but-wrong-method, attaching a design label to a comparison whose key assumption plainly fails, and silent scope change, upgrading associated with to because while sounding like it settled the question. Fluency is not evidence. You catch it by refusing the label and asking the one question the model cannot answer for you: how was treatment actually assigned? No lottery, no cutoff, no outside force decided which mice carried the microbe. They sorted themselves by diet and behavior, so there is no as-if-random assignment, the back door stays open, and because is not earned. Say associated with, and log why.

title: 12. Observational Causal Research
text:
Verification laboratory
Verify the chapter’s key result with simulation, the method from the verification guide for checking how a procedure behaves rather than a fixed number. Build a world where you set the true effect yourself, so you know the right answer and can grade every method against it:
rng = np.random.default_rng(464)
n = 4000
lifestyle = rng.integers(0, 2, n)                       # the confounder you can see
p = np.where(lifestyle == 1, 0.70, 0.30)                # health-conscious drink more
coffee = rng.binomial(1, p)
score = 2.0 * lifestyle + 1.0 * coffee + rng.normal(0, 1, n)  # TRUE effect = +1.0

naive = score[coffee == 1].mean() - score[coffee == 0].mean()
adj = np.mean([score[(coffee==1)&(lifestyle==g)].mean()
             - score[(coffee==0)&(lifestyle==g)].mean() for g in (0, 1)])
The naive gap lands well above the +1.0 you planted; the within-lifestyle adjustment pulls it back near +1.0. That is confounding and its fix, in numbers you own. For the lab’s real lottery result, add a second, independent method: direct calculation, hand-filtering winners and losers and subtracting their mean views yourself, rather than trusting one cell.

title: 12. Observational Causal Research
text:
Project transfer
This chapter is the spine of milestone M5, your causal identification strategy (or honest causal-language boundary), due at Friday’s studio. Draft four pieces for your project: the causal question as a treatment and an outcome; a causal diagram naming the confounder you most fear; your leverage (selection on observables, a natural experiment, difference-in-differences, regression discontinuity, or instrumental variables) and the one untestable assumption it rests on, or the causal-language boundary if you have none; and one number you would recompute by a second route to check any collaborator’s estimate. Write them so the required “Causal Identification Skeptic” review at the studio can find the exact assumption your claim depends on, because that is what it will attack first.

title: 12. Observational Causal Research
text:
Defend your decision
In one short paragraph, state your project’s treatment and outcome, then declare the decision this chapter equips: does your design identify the causal effect, or does your claim stop at associated with? If you claim identification, name the argument and the one untestable assumption it rests on, and report the effect with its uncertainty. If you stop at association, name the unmeasured confounder you cannot rule out. Either way, state the boundary plainly: the one thing a skeptic could say that would move your claim from because back to associated with.

title: 10. Declaring and Diagnosing a Research Design
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 10. Declaring and Diagnosing a Research Design
text:
Why this decision matters
Picture a methodologist on your committee reading your plan. She asks one question: “How often would this exact design catch the real effect if you ran it a thousand times?” If your answer is “I never checked,” she stops there. A design can read beautifully, with a sharp question and a clean comparison, and still come back “nothing here” nine times out of ten, or land confidently on the wrong number every time. This chapter lets you find that out cheaply, in simulation, so a semester you would have spent collecting doomed data goes instead to a design that can actually answer your question.

title: 10. Declaring and Diagnosing a Research Design
text:
The concept
In chapter 9 you built the four parts of a design. This chapter puts them to the test with a three-step loop the course borrows from its RDSS text (Research Design in the Social Sciences: Declare, Diagnose, Redesign).
Declare means writing your four design parts as something a computer can run: a model that generates fake data, an inquiry it can compute on that fake data, a plan for sampling and assigning conditions, and a rule for turning data into an estimate. Example: code that invents 40 users, flips a coin to give each a new interface, and subtracts the two group averages.
Diagnose means running that declared design many times on fresh simulated worlds and watching how its estimate behaves. Example: run the coin-flip study 2,000 times and collect all 2,000 estimates. Three numbers summarize the pile.
Bias is the average distance between your estimate and the truth across the runs; a design is unbiased when its estimates center on the true answer. Example: if the truth is 2 and your estimates average 2.0, bias is about zero. Bias is a systematic tilt, and more data never shrinks it.
Variance is how much the estimate wobbles from one run to the next. Example: estimates swinging between -5 and +9 around a true value of 2 have high variance. More data does shrink this.
Power is how often the design detects a real effect. Example: a design that finds the effect in 8 of 100 runs has 8% power, which is almost useless.
Redesign means changing exactly one part in response to the diagnosis, then diagnosing again. Low power from high variance calls for more data. Bias calls for a different design, because size buys steadiness, not truth.

title: 10. Declaring and Diagnosing a Research Design
text:
A worked example
You are a human-computer interaction researcher asking whether an inline validation hint, a small message that appears as you type (“password needs a number”) instead of only after you submit, helps people finish a signup form faster. Your outcome is seconds-to-complete. Your true effect, in the world you simulate, is that the hint shaves 8 seconds off.
You declare it: build users whose baseline times vary a lot, because some read slowly and some race through; flip a coin to assign the hint version; subtract the two group averages. You diagnose a pilot of 12 users per version. The result is sobering. Bias is near zero, so the design aims true, but the spread is huge because per-person times vary so much, and power comes back under 10%. The design is honest and useless at once: it points at the 8-second gain on average and almost never gets close enough on any single run to prove it.
Now redesign. Variance is the problem, so you raise the sample to 400 per version and diagnose again. Power jumps past 90% and the spread collapses toward the truth. That is variance cured by size. But run one more design as a warning: drop the coin flip and let tech-savvy users self-select into the new version. Savvy users finish faster anyway, so the hint gets credit for speed that savviness delivered. This confounded design is just as tight at 400 per version, yet it centers on a 14-second gain instead of 8, and no amount of extra data moves it. That gap names the last idea. A randomized design can identify the hint’s effect, meaning pin the time gap on the hint and rule out other causes, while the confounded one shows only an association, where the two move together but a common cause could explain it.

title: 10. Declaring and Diagnosing a Research Design
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb03 — The anatomy of a research design: MIDA + declare → diagnose → redesign. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. In the lab you declare the mentoring design in a few lines of Python, run it thousands of times to read its bias, variance, and power with your own eyes, then watch one redesign rescue a fair-but-powerless study while a confounded twin stays wrong at any sample size.

title: 10. Declaring and Diagnosing a Research Design
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own expectation first, then delegate. Each prompt below leaves you a draft you still have to check.
Act as a simulation assistant. Here is my declared design in words:
[model, inquiry, data strategy, answer strategy]. Write the smallest Python
that builds a world where I set the true effect, runs the design many times,
and reports bias, variance, and power. List every assumption your code makes
about my design that I did not state.
After running, verify (counters plausible-but-wrong-method): re-run it with the true effect set to zero and confirm it “detects” an effect only about as often as your significance threshold allows. A simulation that fires on a zero effect is declaring a different design than yours.
Here is my diagnosed design and its numbers: [bias, variance, power]. List the
threats to any conclusion from one run as a table: threat, whether it is a
bias / variance / power problem, and the single redesign that reduces it most.
After running, verify (counters illusion of completeness): check the table against your own printed diagnosis. The killer your numbers already proved must be the top row; a tidy six-threat list that never names it missed the point.
Argue against my claim that this design is strong enough to run. As a hostile
methodologist, name the one diagnosand I am most likely fooling myself about,
and the observation that would expose it.
After running, verify (counters sycophantic agreement): if it praises the design without a single objection, discard the answer and diagnose again yourself.






ImportantDo not delegate



Three calls stay yours. Which world your question assumes, the model you declare, is a claim about reality no tool can make for you. Which single quantity you name as your inquiry decides what “success” even means. And the honest call of whether to run a design your own diagnosis says is too weak is a judgment you sign your name to. An AI can propose threats and draft simulation code, but it cannot decide that your under-powered study is worth a semester anyway.

title: 10. Declaring and Diagnosing a Research Design
text:
An AI failure case
You paste your confounded observational design and ask, “My estimate is noisy. What should I do?” The tool answers with confidence: “Recruit more participants to tighten your estimate.” That sounds obviously right and is exactly wrong for your case. Your problem is bias from self-selection, not variance, and more data only makes a biased estimate a more precise wrong number. You catch it the way this chapter teaches: run the confounded design in simulation at a large sample and watch the estimates cluster tightly around 14 seconds when the truth is 8. The spread shrank; the error did not. That is the plausible-but-wrong-method failure, and your own diagnosis unmasks it.

title: 10. Declaring and Diagnosing a Research Design
text:
Verification laboratory
The method here is simulation (verification guide, method 2: reach for it when a claim is about how a procedure behaves, not a fixed number). Never trust an AI’s “your design has about 80% power.” Declare the design yourself, set a true effect you choose, run it a few thousand times, and read the power off your own pile of estimates. For any design that claims a cause, add an independent second check: causal diagram (method 8). Draw your model as a DAG and look for a common cause with an arrow into both your treatment and your outcome. If that backdoor is open, no power number rescues you, and your honest claim stops at “associated with.”

title: 10. Declaring and Diagnosing a Research Design
text:
Project transfer
This chapter carries you through the back half of M3, your research charter and MIDA declaration, the three-minute declaration you present at Friday’s studio. Chapter 9 gave you the four parts; here you declare them as a runnable design, diagnose it for bias, variance, and power, and bring one named redesign. Your M3 is graded on drawing the identify-versus-associate line for your own design, so the diagnosis you run this week is the evidence behind the boundary you will defend.

title: 10. Declaring and Diagnosing a Research Design
text:
Defend your decision
In five sentences, declare and defend one design for your project. State the single quantity you are after (your inquiry). Report the worst diagnosis number you can estimate, bias, variance, or power, and say what it does to your answer. Then make the honest call: run it, redesign first, or narrow the claim, and name the one change that would most improve it. Close with the boundary: does your design identify an effect or only show an association, and what remains uncertain even if everything goes right?

title: 8. Finding and Verifying Prior Evidence
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 8. Finding and Verifying Prior Evidence
text:
Why this decision matters
Picture a thesis advisor handing your first draft back across the desk. “Show me the conversation your work joins,” she says, “not a pile of summaries. Which studies agree, which fight, and where exactly is the hole you say you are filling?” That question decides whether your project has a place to stand. Get it wrong in one of two ways and the whole project tilts. Cite a source that does not exist, or does not say what you claim, and every result resting on it is compromised. Announce a gap that a paper already filled, and you have promised a contribution you cannot deliver. This chapter equips you to make both calls and defend them.

title: 8. Finding and Verifying Prior Evidence
text:
The concept
A field is a cumulative conversation: a running argument where each study answers, extends, or challenges the ones before it. For example, one lab reports that a soil treatment raises crop yield, and the next lab tests whether the effect holds in a different climate. Your job is not to invent an island. It is to find the conversation your question belongs to and add one honest sentence to it.
Every source you keep passes through one habit. The retrieval-verification loop is four steps: ask any tool to surface leads, retrieve the actual source yourself, verify it exists and says what was claimed, and document where you found it. A lead is a candidate source you have not confirmed yet, such as a citation an AI hands you. A lead stays a lead until retrieval and verification promote it to a source.
Two named failures are what the loop exists to catch. A citation hallucination is an AI inventing a source that does not exist, dressed in a real-sounding author pair, a recent year, and a plausible journal. A mischaracterized source is a real paper cited for a claim it never makes, such as a study that reports a correlation being cited as proof of a cause. A source can be real and still be mischaracterized, so “does it exist” and “does it say this” are two separate checks.
Once you hold one verified source, you grow the rest by snowballing: walking its links in two directions. A backward citation search reads the source’s own reference list to find the older work it built on. A forward citation search finds the newer works that cite it, through a “Cited by” trail. Two solid sources, snowballed both ways, become ten.
That is also how you earn the word gap. A genuine gap is a question your verified sources actually leave unanswered, confirmed by looking. An unfinished search is a question you simply stopped chasing. They feel identical from your desk, and only one is a real contribution. The test is retrieval: you have earned the word gap only after real sources, followed forward and backward, still stay silent on your question.

title: 8. Finding and Verifying Prior Evidence
text:
A worked example
You are an honors student in agronomy asking whether adding biochar (charcoal made from crop waste and mixed into soil) raises maize yield in sandy fields. You ask an AI tool for evidence. It returns a confident citation: a named author pair, a 2021 date, a real-sounding soil-science journal, and a tidy finding that biochar lifted yield by 18 percent. Perfect. So you go to read it, and it is nowhere. Not in the library portal, not on Google Scholar, not on the publisher’s site.
Here is the decision on the table. Is the missing study proof that “no one has tested biochar in sandy soil, so my gap is real”? No. Three things could be true. The AI fabricated the study whole, and there is nothing to find. It garbled a real source, right idea but wrong author or year, so you search the claim itself and the likely real authors. Or the paper is real but paywalled or obscure, reachable through the library or by emailing the author. Your next move is the same in all three: the lead stays out of your verified column until retrieval succeeds.
So you do the work. You retrieve four real soil-science studies through the library database, read each one, and snowball. Backward from one 2019 field trial you find the older greenhouse study it built on. Forward through “Cited by” you find two newer trials that tested the same idea in loam and clay. Now you can see the conversation: biochar helps yield in some soils, the size varies, and none of the four you retrieved ran the trial in the sandy soil your county farms. That quiet spot, ringed by sources you opened yourself, is a gap you can defend.

title: 8. Finding and Verifying Prior Evidence
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb02 — Research builds on research: verified evidence + real gaps. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. In the lab you run the retrieval-verification loop on your own claim and watch a simulation collapse a tidy pile of fifteen AI citations down to the handful that survive retrieval, then build an evidence map whose one empty square is your gap.

title: 8. Finding and Verifying Prior Evidence
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each block below is something you would actually type.
My research question is: [paste it]. The claim I need sources for is:
[paste the claim]. List the five published studies most relevant to this
claim, each with authors, year, exact title, and venue, so I can retrieve
and verify each one myself.
After running, verify: search each exact title in quotes before believing it exists. This counters confident fabrication (a real-sounding citation for a paper that was never written).
Here are the five sources I retrieved and read: [paste them]. For each, state
the single claim its evidence actually supports, and flag any where my
one-line summary reaches further than the study does.
After running, verify: read each flagged claim against the paper’s own results section. This counters the mischaracterized source and silent scope change (a correlation quietly reported as a cause).
Here is my research question and the gap I placed: [paste them]. Argue the gap
is NOT real: name up to three published studies that may already fill it, with
authors, year, title, and venue. Do not reassure me.
After running, verify: retrieve every study it names; a source you cannot open closes nothing. This counters sycophantic agreement (a partner that praises your gap has flattered you, not tested it).






ImportantDo not delegate



Two calls stay yours alone, because your name goes on the map. First, whether a source counts as verified: you retrieved it, opened it, and read the claim yourself, so no AI summary substitutes for the paper. Second, whether your gap is genuine: you walked one backward and one forward link from each verified source and the question still stands unanswered. An AI can point; only you can vouch.

title: 8. Finding and Verifying Prior Evidence
text:
An AI failure case
The failure to expect here is confident fabrication. Ask a general-purpose tool for “the most-cited study on biochar and maize yield in sandy soil” and it will often produce one, complete with a DOI-shaped string and a real journal name, because producing a fluent citation is exactly what it is built to do. The detail is not evidence of existence. You catch it the moment you try to retrieve it: paste the exact title in quotation marks into the library database and Google Scholar. If nothing resolves anywhere it should live, the citation is fabricated until proven otherwise, and it never enters your ledger. The fluency was the trap; the empty search result is the tell.

title: 8. Finding and Verifying Prior Evidence
text:
Verification laboratory
The chapter’s key result is your verified-source list, and the method that confirms it is primary-source reading from the verification guide: for any citation, find the actual source and read the relevant line. Reach for it whenever an output cites a study, a statistic, or a fact from the literature. Concretely, for each source you plan to keep, open the paper itself, confirm the author, year, and venue match, and confirm the sentence you attribute to it is a sentence it actually contains. A source that survives that read is verified; one that cannot be opened, or that does not say what you claimed, is cut. For the gap itself, add a second, independent method: peer reasoning, walking a classmate through why the map stays quiet where you placed your gap and letting their one question expose what your retrieval still owes.

title: 8. Finding and Verifying Prior Evidence
text:
Project transfer
This chapter is the engine of Milestone 2, your Verified Evidence and Contribution Map, presented as a gallery walk at the Friday studio. M2 asks for a ledger of at least six sources you personally retrieved, an evidence map with your contradictions drawn rather than hidden, your gap placed where the map is demonstrably open, and a bounded contribution line of the form “based on the sources I retrieved and verified, ___ has not been established for ___.” The retrieval-verification loop you practice here is exactly what fills that ledger, and the verified map becomes the ground the M3 research charter stands on.

title: 8. Finding and Verifying Prior Evidence
text:
Defend your decision
In a short paragraph, state the decision this chapter equips and defend it for your own project. Name one source you have verified and the exact claim it makes. State your provisional gap in one bounded sentence. Then give its boundary and uncertainty: which compass position it sits in (description or generalization), how many sources you have actually retrieved so far, and whether your gap is confirmed by retrieval or is still an unfinished search you must keep chasing before Friday.

title: 6. Descriptive, Predictive, and Causal Questions
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 6. Descriptive, Predictive, and Causal Questions
text:
Why this decision matters
A thesis advisor at your first committee meeting puts it plainly: “Before I approve a single method, I make you answer two questions about your question. What kind of answer does it want, and for whom must that answer hold?” Everything downstream sits inside the box those two answers draw. Misclassify your question and every later method aims at the wrong target, because a method that describes what is there cannot tell you what an intervention would change. The compass fixes what your evidence may claim before you spend a term collecting it.

title: 6. Descriptive, Predictive, and Causal Questions
text:
The concept
Every project narrows to a research question: the single sentence evidence can answer and be wrong about, such as “does a lid slow a mug’s cooling?” Before you pick a method, you classify that question on the inquiry compass, the course map that sorts any question by exactly two things. The compass is adapted from Research Design in the Social Sciences (Blair, Coppock, and Humphreys, 2023, ch. 7).
The first axis is kind.
