A descriptive question asks what the world is or was, with no intervention needed; you look and record, as in “what fraction of first-years skip breakfast?” A causal question asks what would change if someone intervened, which forces a comparison to a world that did not happen, as in “does a free breakfast raise attendance?”
The second axis is reach: for which units the answer must hold. Data at hand means only the units you actually observed. A population means a larger group your sampling reaches beyond the data. Unseen cases means units you have not observed yet, usually future ones.
Crossing the two axes gives four named positions. Description is descriptive about the data at hand. Generalization is descriptive about a population, and it must pay for its reach with a sampling design that lets the units you saw stand in for the ones you did not. Prediction is descriptive about unseen cases; it forecasts a new case from a pattern. Prediction is still descriptive, not causal: guessing who will drop out is not the same as knowing what makes them drop out. Causal reasoning is the causal kind, the only position that earns the word “because.”
One last hazard. A double-barreled question is one sentence that secretly asks two questions of different kinds, such as “how common is burnout, and does night work cause it?” It cannot be answered as one, and the fix is to split it into two clean questions, each with its own position.

title: 6. Descriptive, Predictive, and Causal Questions
text:
A worked example
Start with a physics curiosity: “why does my coffee go cold so fast?” Give it edges and it becomes a topic, heat loss from travel mugs. That one topic hides four different questions, one at each compass position. Newton’s law of cooling, a standard result in introductory physics, says a warm object loses heat faster the hotter it is than the room. Against that backdrop you time twelve mugs as each cools from 90 °C to 60 °C.

“In these twelve mugs, what was the average time to cool from 90 to 60 °C?” This asks only what your recorded numbers say. It is descriptive with reach data at hand. Position: Description.
“Across every mug of this model ever made, what is the mean cooling time?” Same descriptive kind, but the answer must hold for a population you did not measure, paid for with a sampling design that makes your twelve stand in for the rest. Position: Generalization.
“A new mug arrives tomorrow; how long until it cools to 60 °C?” Still descriptive, because you only need a pattern that travels to a case you have not seen. Position: Prediction. You forecast the cooling time without knowing why any particular mug runs fast or slow.
“Does adding a lid slow the cooling?” Now the kind flips. To answer it you must compare the same mug in two worlds, one with a lid and one without, and only one of those worlds actually happens. That imagined comparison is the signature of a causal question. Position: Causal reasoning.

One move finishes the skill. A lab partner offers what they call one question: “how fast do our mugs cool, and does a lid slow them down?” It is double-barreled, fusing the descriptive headcount of question 1 with the causal comparison of question 4, and no single design answers both. You split it, and each half keeps its own position.

title: 6. Descriptive, Predictive, and Causal Questions
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb01 — From curiosity to a research problem: descriptive, predictive, causal. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. There you walk your own curiosity down to a question, watch a naive keyword sorter get fooled on a deck of eight questions, and place your own question on the compass in the shape milestone M1 will grade.

title: 6. Descriptive, Predictive, and Causal Questions
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first in every case. Write your classification down before you open the tool, so its reply has something of yours to disagree with.
Classify, after you have classified it yourself:
Act as a research-methods tutor. I have already classified my question:
[paste your question and your own kind + reach]. For my question, give its
KIND (descriptive or causal) and REACH (data at hand, a population, or
unseen cases), name the single word that most influenced you, and say
whether answering it needs an intervention or only a pattern. Use a table.
After running, verify (counters silent scope change): reread the position it returns against the two definitions. If it calls your question causal, confirm your design ever intervenes; if it only observes what already is, the honest kind is descriptive.
Split a question you suspect is double-barreled:
Here is a question I think secretly asks two things: [paste it]. Split it
into exactly two clean questions. For each half name its KIND and REACH. Do
not add any unit, outcome, or population that was not in my sentence.
After running, verify (counters illusion of completeness): a tidy split can quietly drop a half or smuggle in a new variable. Confirm both halves together cover your whole sentence and that neither added anything you did not write.
Red-team your own placement:
Act as a hostile methodologist. Here is my question and the compass position
I assigned it: [paste both]. Argue the strongest case that I classified it
wrong. If you claim it is causal, name the method you would use and the one
assumption that method needs, in a table.
After running, verify (counters sycophantic agreement and plausible-but-wrong method): praise with no objection is a red flag, so force the objection. For any method it names, check whether your data can actually meet the assumption it lists. An assumption you cannot meet turns a confident method into a wrong one.






ImportantDo not delegate



Three decisions stay yours: which problem you will study, how you word your question, and which compass position it belongs to. A tool can offer a label, but only you can decide what answer your question truly wants, and only you defend the placement at the studio. If you cannot classify your question cleanly yet, that is a finding, not a failure; a question that will not sit in one box is usually still double-barreled, and naming that is progress.

title: 6. Descriptive, Predictive, and Causal Questions
text:
An AI failure case
Suppose your project only records the cooling curves of bottles that are already vacuum-insulated. Nothing is ever assigned; you measure bottles as they are. You ask a chatbot to classify “is a vacuum-insulated bottle an effective heat barrier?” It sees the word “effective,” reads the question as causal, and confidently recommends a randomized experiment that assigns bottles to insulation on or off. That is fluent and well-formatted, and wrong for your design, because no un-insulated comparison world exists anywhere in your data. The word “effective” is a giveaway word, a surface hint the tool trusted instead of asking what answer the question wants. You catch it the same way every time: reread the question against the two definitions and ask whether your design ever intervenes. It does not, so the question is descriptive (Description for your bottles, Generalization if it reaches to all bottles of the model), and you reject the causal label as a silent scope change.

title: 6. Descriptive, Predictive, and Causal Questions
text:
Verification laboratory
The chapter’s key result is a classification, which is a judgment call, so the fitting check from the verification guide is peer reasoning. Explain your placement to a classmate or the instructor, walking them through the two definitions, and see whether the reasoning survives their hardest question. Do it after you have committed your own placement, so their challenge has something to push against. When your placement is causal, add a second, independent check: draw a causal diagram of what causes what and confirm your design isolates the effect, meaning a real comparison to a world that did not happen exists. Two checks that agree beat one, and guard against the trap where a tool and its own re-check share the same flaw.

title: 6. Descriptive, Predictive, and Causal Questions
text:
Project transfer
This chapter builds the spine of your milestone M1, the research opportunity landscape you pitch at the Friday studio. Take the strongest curiosity from your nb00 curiosity map, walk it down to a single question, and place that question on the compass in the exact shape M1 grades: “my question is a [descriptive or causal] inquiry reaching [the data at hand / a population / unseen cases], because the answer it wants is [what].” Then name the one claim that position permits and the one it forbids. Every design declaration you make for the rest of the term lives inside the box you draw here.

title: 6. Descriptive, Predictive, and Causal Questions
text:
Defend your decision
In a short paragraph, state your project’s question, naming its units of analysis, its outcome, and its target population, then give its compass position in the M1 shape above. Defend it: name the one claim the position permits and the boundary it draws (the reach it does not reach, and whether it earns the word “because”), and close with one sentence of honest uncertainty about the classification or the problem. A placement with no stated boundary and no stated uncertainty is incomplete, however confident it sounds.

title: 4. Research Responsibility and Intellectual Ownership
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 4. Research Responsibility and Intellectual Ownership
text:
Why this decision matters

“When something in this paper turns out to be wrong, I do not ask which software wrote it. I ask whose name is on it. That person answers for every line.” — a journal editor, to a first-time author

You will finish this course with work that carries your name: a poster, a research note, a public defense. An AI tool may have helped you find sources, draft a paragraph, or compute a number. None of that moves the responsibility off you. The reviewer, the reader, and your future self all hold you, the named author, accountable. This chapter draws that line on purpose, before someone else draws it for you.

title: 4. Research Responsibility and Intellectual Ownership
text:
The concept
Intellectual ownership is being the author of, and answerable for, every claim that appears under your name, whatever tool helped produce it. Example: if an AI drafts your methods paragraph and it states that creek chloride exceeded a safety threshold, you own that sentence, and if it is wrong, the mistake is yours.
Ownership rests on accountability, being the person who answers for the work’s correctness and integrity when it is questioned. Example: at your defense, when someone asks how you know a number, “the AI computed it” is not an answer. “I recomputed it from my own measurements, and here is the check” is.
The habit that makes accountability visible is AI-use disclosure, a short, honest record of which AI tool did which task and how you verified its output. Example: “Gemini located candidate sources; I retrieved and confirmed each one in the library catalog.” Disclosure is not a confession. It protects you, because it shows exactly where your own judgment entered the work.
Some calls never move to the tool at all. A never-delegate decision is a judgment that stays yours no matter how capable the tool becomes: which question you pursue, what your evidence licenses, the ethics of the study, and the decision to put your name on the work. Example: an AI can list candidate measures of water quality, but choosing the one you will defend is yours.
Put together, the rule is simple. Use AI freely for reach and speed. Keep ownership, accountability, and honest disclosure for yourself.

title: 4. Research Responsibility and Intellectual Ownership
text:
A worked example
Picture a first-year honors project in environmental science. You are testing whether winter road salt raises chloride, a dissolved salt ion that harms freshwater life above certain levels, in a creek behind campus. You collect ten water samples and ask an AI tool to help write up the result.
The tool returns a fluent paragraph: “Mean chloride was 310 mg/L, well above the U.S. EPA chronic aquatic-life criterion of 230 mg/L, so the creek is unsafe for aquatic life.” It reads like a finished result. Here is the ownership decision: which parts of that sentence will you personally stand behind?
You split it into pieces you can own. The mean of your ten samples is a number you can recompute in a minute, so you do, and you get 297, not 310. The EPA criterion is a published value, so you open the EPA’s National Recommended Water Quality Criteria and confirm the real figure yourself rather than trust the tool’s number. The word “unsafe” claims something about the whole creek all winter, from ten samples on one day, so you narrow it to what your evidence licenses: elevated chloride in these samples, at this site, on this date.
Now your disclosure writes itself: the tool drafted the paragraph, and you did the checking, retrieving, and rewriting that made the claim yours. You own the corrected sentence. You would not have owned the original.

title: 4. Research Responsibility and Intellectual Ownership
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb00, “Research in the age of AI: your arm, your RA, not your brain.” Open it in Colab with the badge above. There you sort real research tasks by how safely they delegate, catch a live citation by retrieving it yourself, and fill your first AI Research Ledger row: the record that turns “I checked it” into something you can show.

title: 4. Research Responsibility and Intellectual Ownership
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt below hands the tool reach, not judgment.
Locate the real source.
Act as a research assistant in environmental science. I need the current U.S. EPA
aquatic-life criterion for chloride. Give the exact value, the document it comes
from, and a link. Only state a figure you are confident is published; if you are
unsure, say so and tell me where to look.
After running, verify: open the EPA document and read the figure yourself (primary-source reading). Counters confident fabrication, a number quoted with a made-up source.
List every claim to verify.
Here is my draft results paragraph: [paste]. List every empirical claim in it as a
table: the claim, the number or source it rests on, and whether that number came from
my data or from you. Do not add any new claims.
After running, verify: check the table against your own list of what you actually measured. Counters illusion of completeness, a claim the draft slipped in that never shows up in the tool’s own accounting.
Red-team your ownership.
Act as a hostile reviewer. Read my draft and name every sentence I could NOT defend
myself if someone asked how I know it is true. Do not rewrite anything. List the weak
spots.
After running, verify: for each flagged sentence, either produce your own check or cut the claim. Counters sycophantic agreement, praise that reviews your ego instead of your evidence.






ImportantDo not delegate



The tool may fetch, draft, and compute. It may never decide which claims you put your name on, whether your evidence supports a word like “unsafe,” the ethics of your sampling, how you state your uncertainty, or what your AI-use disclosure says. Authorship is a responsibility, not a task, and it does not transfer.

title: 4. Research Responsibility and Intellectual Ownership
text:
An AI failure case
You ask the tool to write your AI-use disclosure for you. It produces a tidy, confident sentence: “AI was used only for minor language editing.” It sounds professional, and it is false. The tool actually drafted your entire results paragraph, including the 310 mg/L figure and the “unsafe” claim. Left unchecked, that disclosure understates the tool’s role and overstates your ownership, which is exactly the integrity problem disclosure exists to prevent.
You catch it with a record the tool cannot see: your own AI Research Ledger. Lay the one-line disclosure next to your ledger rows and the mismatch is plain. The ledger says the tool drafted the analysis; the disclosure says it only edited language. You rewrite the disclosure to match what happened. The lesson is blunt: never let a tool describe its own role. You describe it, from your record.

title: 4. Research Responsibility and Intellectual Ownership
text:
Verification laboratory
Verify this chapter’s key result, that a claim under your name is one you can defend, with primary-source reading, the method of finding the actual source and reading the relevant line yourself (see ai_resources/verification_guide.md). Take the EPA chloride figure the tool quoted, open the EPA’s criteria document, and confirm the number and its meaning with your own eyes. Pair it with direct calculation: recompute the mean of your samples and check it matches the draft. Two independent checks beat one, and each leaves a line for your ledger.

title: 4. Research Responsibility and Intellectual Ownership
text:
Project transfer
This chapter launches milestone M0, your research-and-AI baseline, presented at Friday’s studio. Bring three things it asks for: two decisions you refuse to delegate on your own project, with one sentence each on why; your first AI Research Ledger row, honestly disclosing a real AI use and how you checked it; and one claim you can already defend, stated at the reach your evidence supports. Ownership is not a late-semester worry. It is the baseline you build everything else on.

title: 4. Research Responsibility and Intellectual Ownership
text:
Defend your decision
In four or five sentences, state the ownership decision for one piece of your own work. Name a claim you will put your name on. Give its boundary: what your evidence does not establish, and if the claim rests on a sample, say it is about that sample, not the world. Name one thing you do not yet know. Then disclose the AI’s role in one honest line, and name the check that lets you own the claim anyway.

title: 2. The Student as Research Director
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 2. The Student as Research Director
text:
Why this decision matters
Picture a principal investigator, the researcher who runs a lab and owns its scientific decisions, briefing a new lab member on the first day:

“A research assistant here can run the assay, plot the readings, and format the table. What the assistant does not do is choose the question, decide what the readings mean, or sign the claim. That part is mine. Learn where that line sits, because it does not move just because the assistant got faster.”

AI is the fastest research assistant you will ever direct. The risk is not that it does the routine work. The risk is letting it drift into the calls that make the work yours. Deciding, task by task, what you delegate is the first act of being a research director instead of a passenger.

title: 2. The Student as Research Director
text:
The concept
A research director is the person who assigns each piece of work and stays accountable for the finished result. Example: you may ask a tool to draft your methods paragraph, but you are the one who signs it and answers for it. Delegation is handing a task to someone or something else to carry out, the way you might hand off formatting a messy table while keeping the reading of what the numbers mean.
The core move of this chapter is a delegation triage, sorting each task into one of three buckets before you start.

Safe to delegate: low stakes and easy to check, so a wrong answer costs little. Example: rewording one survey item three ways.
Delegate then verify: fine to hand out, but you must confirm the result with a real check before you trust it. Example: asking the tool to locate a lab protocol, then opening the actual protocol to confirm it exists and says what the tool claimed.
Never delegate: a judgment that stays yours no matter how capable the tool becomes. Example: deciding whether your evidence actually supports the claim you want to make.

A never-delegate decision is any call that defines the science itself: the question you pursue, the design and the people or samples it covers, what your measures mean, the ethics, the boundary of your claim, and the uncertainty you report. Verification is the step that earns trust: confirming a result yourself, by a named method, before you put your name on it.

title: 2. The Student as Research Director
text:
A worked example
An honors biology student wonders whether a common food preservative, sodium benzoate, slows the growth of a harmless lab strain of E. coli. She measures growth by optical density, how cloudy a liquid culture gets as cells multiply, read on a plate reader as OD600. She is the director. The AI is her assistant. Here is her triage.
Safe to delegate. Reformatting three plates of raw OD600 numbers into one tidy table, and drafting a plain-language version of her methods paragraph. If a value lands in the wrong column, one glance catches it, so the stakes are low.
Delegate then verify. Locating a standard protocol for reading bacterial growth curves, and drafting the formula for doubling time, the hours a population takes to double in number. She hands these out, then confirms each: she opens the actual protocol, and she recomputes one doubling time by hand from two printed OD values.
Never delegate. Deciding that the concentrations she tests are biologically meaningful and safe, deciding whether a gap between the treated and untreated curves licenses the word “inhibits,” and stating how uncertain that conclusion is given three plates and one strain. No tool makes those calls. The moment she lets it, she becomes a reader of someone else’s guess.

title: 2. The Student as Research Director
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb00 — Research in the age of AI: your arm, your RA — not your brain. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. In the “safe to delegate, verify first, or never delegate” practice you sort five real research tasks into the three buckets and defend each call, and at the Human-Only Checkpoint you close the AI and name, for your own project, two decisions you will never hand over.

title: 2. The Student as Research Director
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Each prompt below is commit-first: write your own answer before you open the tool, so its reply has something of yours to disagree with.
Locate a bench resource (delegable).
Act as a lab-methods assistant in microbiology. I am measuring bacterial growth
by optical density (OD600). List 3 published, retrievable protocols or textbook
methods for reading a growth curve. For each, give authors or source, year, and
where it appears. Only include work you are confident exists; mark anything
uncertain. Then tell me which one to verify first.
After running, verify (counters confident fabrication): open each protocol yourself before you cite it; a source you cannot retrieve is invented until it opens.
Turn your triage into a checkable list.
Here are eight tasks in my project: [paste your list]. For each, label it "safe to
delegate," "delegate then verify," or "never delegate," and give a one-line reason.
Return it as a table so I can audit each row on its own.
After running, verify (counters illusion of completeness): compare the table against the triage you wrote first and against the never-delegate checklist; a tidy table can quietly mis-file the one decision that mattered.
Red-team your own boundary.
I marked this task "never delegate": [paste one task]. Act as a hostile reviewer
and argue that it is actually safe to hand to an AI. Give your strongest case. Do
not agree with me.
After running, verify (counters sycophantic agreement): if the reviewer folds and praises your call, push back and take its strongest objection to a classmate or your instructor rather than trusting the tool’s approval.






ImportantDo not delegate



The buckets themselves are yours to fill, and three calls never leave the “never delegate” bucket: which question your project pursues, whether your evidence supports the claim you want to make, and how much uncertainty you report. You may ask a tool to lay out options, but choosing among them, and answering for the choice, is the job that makes you the director.

title: 2. The Student as Research Director
text:
An AI failure case
You paste your eight tasks in and ask the tool to sort them. Back comes a clean, confident table. Seven rows look right. But one reads: “Decide whether the growth difference is biologically meaningful: delegate then verify, just apply the standard effect-size cutoff.” It sounds authoritative, and it hides a scope change. A judgment about what counts as meaningful in your specific system has been swapped for a mechanical lookup, and the “standard cutoff” it names is one no source will confirm. You catch it two ways. You wrote your own triage before you asked, and your version had that row as never delegate, so the mismatch jumps out. Then you try to retrieve the “standard cutoff,” and nothing real supports it. The row goes back where it belongs.

title: 2. The Student as Research Director
text:
Verification laboratory
The key result of this chapter is a triage, which is a judgment, so verify it with peer reasoning: walk your three buckets past a classmate or the instructor and ask them to challenge any placement. A judgment that survives a skeptical second reader is far stronger than one you sorted alone. For any “delegate then verify” row that names a source, add primary-source reading: open the actual protocol or paper and confirm it says what the tool claimed.

title: 2. The Student as Research Director
text:
Project transfer
This chapter builds the spine of your M0 research-and-AI baseline, the milestone you present at Friday’s studio. M0 asks you to name two decisions in your own project that you will not delegate, and to say in one sentence each why each is yours to own. The triage you practice here is exactly that list, written for your topic instead of the E. coli example.

title: 2. The Student as Research Director
text:
Defend your decision
In a short paragraph, state your rule for a task you are unsure about, then defend it. Name the task, name the bucket you put it in, and give the check you would run if you delegated it. Then state the boundary: what would move it to a different bucket, and how confident you are that you drew the line in the right place. If you cannot yet name where you might over-trust the tool, say so plainly. That admission is itself a finding about the habit you most need to watch.

title: Preface
text:
Preface
Evidence-Driven Research is the course book for HONR 46400 at Purdue’s Honors College. Its one message: AI is your arm and your research assistant, not your brain. You will direct AI tools through the Specify → Delegate → Interrogate → Inspect → Verify → Document → Defend workflow while keeping every research decision your own.
Blair, Coppock and Humphreys’ Research Design in the Social Sciences (free at book.declaredesign.org) remains the theory text. This book is the course’s own AI-era manual: each chapter names a research decision, works a STEM example, links a Colab lab, shows an AI failure and how to catch it, and asks you to defend a claim. The chapters track the sixteen course notebooks; open each chapter’s lab and work them together.
The book has six parts: research when AI can produce almost everything; from curiosity to research design; the research pathways (observational and experimental, descriptive and causal, and prediction); producing credible evidence with AI; communicating and defending research; and research after the conference.

title: 1. AI Is Your Arm, Not Your Brain
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 1. AI Is Your Arm, Not Your Brain
text:
Why this decision matters
Picture a design review. A licensed professional engineer (PE) is an engineer legally authorized to approve a design, whose signature (their “stamp”) makes them personally answerable for it. Example: the PE who stamps a pedestrian bridge is who the public trusts if it fails.
When a PE stamps your drawing, they are not saying the software ran without errors. They are saying, “I checked this, and I will answer for it.” That is the posture this course asks you to adopt on day one. An AI tool can fetch a property, draft a paragraph, or write working code in seconds, but it cannot stamp your work. You can. So the question that matters is not “what can the tool produce?” It is “what did I hand it, and how did I check what came back?”

title: 1. AI Is Your Arm, Not Your Brain
text:
The concept
The rule that names the chapter is AI as your arm, not your brain: the tool can reach, fetch, draft, and compute for you, but it does not get to decide what is true or what you claim. Example: you let it draft a summary of a datasheet, then confirm every number in that summary yourself.
Two moves put the rule into practice. Delegation is handing a well-specified task to the tool, such as “find three candidate datasheets for this alloy.” Verification is an independent check, run by a method outside the model, that the result is actually true. Example: you open the manufacturer’s real datasheet and read the number yourself instead of trusting what the tool typed.
Verification cannot be skipped because a wrong AI answer looks exactly like a right one. A hallucination is confident, polished output that is simply made up, such as a citation to a standard that does not exist. Example: a tool reports a fatigue limit and attributes it to “ASTM E-4471,” a number never published. Tone is identical for real and invented, so tone cannot be your filter. Retrievability is. A source is retrievable when you can open the actual document and confirm it, such as finding the real standard in the Purdue Libraries and reading the clause.
Some judgments never get delegated. A never-delegate decision is a call that stays yours no matter how good the tool becomes: which question you pursue, what your evidence licenses, the ethics of the work, and how you state your uncertainty. Example: a tool may list candidate safety factors, but deciding which one your design requires is yours to own. The habit that ties this together is short: Ask, then Verify, then Document. Use the tool ambitiously, confirm every fact and number yourself, then log the tool, the task, and the check in a record that travels with your work.

title: 1. AI Is Your Arm, Not Your Brain
text:
A worked example
You are an honors student sizing the arms of a small quadcopter drone printed from carbon-fiber-reinforced PLA, a common 3D-printing filament. Each arm is a thin beam, and if it flexes too much in flight the motors misalign. To size it you need the material’s flexural modulus, the number that says how stiff the material is in bending, where a higher value means the arm bends less under the same load.
Sort the work. You can delegate locating candidate datasheets and listing the properties a beam-bending calculation needs. You keep three things: deciding that flexural modulus (not tensile strength) governs a stiffness problem, choosing the safety margin, and judging whether a source is authoritative. You ask Gemini for datasheets and it returns a filament with a flexural modulus of “roughly 4 gigapascals,” citing a manufacturer.
Now verify. You open the manufacturer’s actual datasheet and read the flexural modulus line yourself. If it confirms the number, you log a verified input. If it shows a different value, or cannot be found at all, the number the tool handed you was never evidence. Either way, the arm rests on a number you retrieved, not one a model typed.

title: 1. AI Is Your Arm, Not Your Brain
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb00, Research in the Age of AI: Your Arm, Your RA, Not Your Brain. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. In the lab you run a small seeded simulation showing why how confident a source sounds tells you nothing about whether it is real, then take one topic you care about, ask an AI for scholarly sources, and verify each by retrieving it yourself.

title: 1. AI Is Your Arm, Not Your Brain
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt hands out a task you can check, with a verify note naming the failure it defends against.
Locate candidate sources (you verify each one).
Act as a materials-research assistant. I need the flexural modulus of
carbon-fiber-reinforced PLA filament. List 3 candidate manufacturer datasheets
or standards, each with the exact document title and where it is published. Only
include documents you are confident exist; mark anything uncertain.
After running, verify (counters confident fabrication): open each document yourself and read the number. A datasheet you cannot retrieve is invented until you find it.
Turn a decision into a checklist (you audit the list).
For sizing a printed drone arm as a beam in bending, list every material and
geometric property my deflection calculation needs. For each, say why it belongs
and which single property most governs the result.
After running, verify (counters illusion of completeness): a long, tidy list can still omit the property that governs your design. Compare it against a checklist you wrote first, and confirm the governing property is really the one bending demands.
Red-team your own choice (you keep the decision).
Here is my material and safety-factor choice for the drone arm: [paste it]. Act as
a hostile design reviewer. Name every place the choice reaches past my evidence.
Do not rewrite it for me; list the weaknesses.
After running, verify (counters sycophantic agreement): if every objection is mild or it calls your choice “well-balanced,” push back and ask for the single worst flaw.






ImportantDo not delegate



These stay yours, no matter how fluent the tool sounds. Deciding which property governs your design and what safety margin the part requires. Judging whether a source is authoritative enough to build on. Setting the claim you will defend and stating its uncertainty. The tool gathers options and drafts prose; choosing among them, and answering for the choice, is the researcher’s job.

title: 1. AI Is Your Arm, Not Your Brain
text:
An AI failure case
You ask for the fatigue limit of your filament and a source. The tool returns a crisp value and attributes it to “ASTM D-7791, Table 3.” It reads like every real citation you have seen: a standards body, a document number, a table. It is fabricated, or the number is not on that page.
Here is exactly how you catch it. You do not trust the citation as delivered. You search the standard in the Purdue Libraries and, if it opens, turn to the named table and read the line. If the standard does not exist, or has no such value, the confident citation collapses. Fabricated sources sound just as authoritative as real ones, so the only reliable filter is opening the document.

title: 1. AI Is Your Arm, Not Your Brain
text:
Verification laboratory
The chapter’s key result is that polish does not predict truth. Confirm it yourself with primary-source reading (from the verification guide): take a real list of citations an AI returns for a topic you know, search each exact title, and count how many actually open. The confident-sounding ones are not more likely to be real than the plain ones. For a number, add direct calculation: recompute the claimed value by hand from the datasheet and check it matches.

title: 1. AI Is Your Arm, Not Your Brain
text:
Project transfer
This chapter launches milestone M0, your research-and-AI baseline, presented at Friday’s studio. Three moves carry over directly. Sort the tasks in your own project into safe-to-delegate, delegate-then-verify, and never-delegate, and say why for each. Run one live citation catch on your topic and record the verdict. Fill your first row in the AI Research Ledger, logging the task, the tool, your decision, and the verification method. That ledger row is the habit M0 installs.

title: 1. AI Is Your Arm, Not Your Brain
text:
Defend your decision
In a short paragraph, state the delegation line you drew for one task in your own project: what you handed the tool, what you kept, and the exact check you ran on what came back. Then name its boundary and your uncertainty. What does your verified result not establish, and where are you most likely to over-trust the tool and stop checking? A decision with no stated boundary is not yet defended.

title: 3. Specify, Delegate, Interrogate, Inspect, Verify, Document, Defend
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 3. Specify, Delegate, Interrogate, Inspect, Verify, Document, Defend
text:
Why this decision matters

“Show me the benchmark, not the number. I want to see what you timed, how many times you ran it, and what you compared it against. A speedup I cannot reproduce is a rumor.” — a research-software reviewer, reading a claim that a new function is faster

A tool can now write the code, run it, and hand you a tidy result in one breath. That is exactly why a loose habit is dangerous. If you paste the result straight into your work, you have imported a guess and signed your name to it. The reviewer above does not care how confident the output sounded. She cares what you set up, what you handed off, and how you checked it before you believed it. This chapter gives you the habit that survives that question.

title: 3. Specify, Delegate, Interrogate, Inspect, Verify, Document, Defend
text:
The concept
The habit has a name you can spell: SDIIVDD, a seven-step protocol for handing work to an AI tool and getting back something you can defend. A protocol is just a fixed sequence of steps you run every time, so you never skip the check that matters. You met its three-word shorthand in Chapter 1, Ask then Verify then Document. SDIIVDD is the full version.
The seven steps, each defined once with an example:

Specify. Write down the exact task and your own expected answer before you ask. Example: “time two dedupe functions on 10,000 records; I expect the hash version to win by maybe 5x.”
Delegate. Hand the well-specified piece to the tool. To delegate is to assign a checkable chunk of work, not your judgment. Example: “write the timing harness for these two functions.”
Interrogate. Ask the draft what it assumes. Example: “does your timer include the setup cost, or only the lookup?”
Inspect. Read what the tool actually produced, not its summary of itself. Example: open the code and see which lines sit inside the timed block.
Verify. Confirm the result with a real, independent check before you trust it. Example: re-run the timing a different way and compare.
Document. Log the tool, the task, and how you checked, in a record that travels with your work.
Defend. State the result in your own words, with the tool out of the room.

Two of these stay human no matter how good the tool gets. Never-delegate decisions are the calls you always own: what you are really asking, what counts as a fair test, and what your evidence licenses you to claim. Example: the tool can time your code, but only you decide the comparison was fair enough to report. Specify and Defend are the human bookends. Everything you hand off sits safely between them.

title: 3. Specify, Delegate, Interrogate, Inspect, Verify, Document, Defend
text:
A worked example
You are studying whether a hash-based deduplication function you asked an AI to write is genuinely faster than the simple version you trust. A benchmark is a controlled timing test that compares two implementations on the same input. Watch the protocol run.
Specify. You write your expectation first: “on 10,000 records, the hash version should be a few times faster, and it must return the exact same de-duplicated list.”
Delegate. You ask the tool to write dedupe_fast() and a harness that times both functions.
Interrogate. You ask, “what does your timer assume?” It admits it runs each function once.
Inspect. You read the harness. The slow function runs first, on a cold start, before the interpreter has warmed up. Warmup is the first run’s extra cost from loading and caching that a steady run does not pay. The harness charges that cost to the slow function alone.
Verify. You run each function five times, drop the first, and take the median. You also check that both return identical output.
Document. You log the prompt, the fix, and the verified numbers.
Defend. You write: “on this synthetic 10,000-record input, the hash version was about 6x faster and returned a byte-identical list. I have not tested real data or inputs full of near-duplicates.”
The tool did the typing. You did the research.

title: 3. Specify, Delegate, Interrogate, Inspect, Verify, Document, Defend
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb00 — Research in the age of AI: your arm, your RA, not your brain. Open it in Colab with the badge above. There you run the protocol live: you catch a fabricated citation by retrieving it yourself, then catch a confident summary that overstates a number the data never held. Both are the Inspect and Verify steps under real pressure.

title: 3. Specify, Delegate, Interrogate, Inspect, Verify, Document, Defend
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt below is a checkable job, not a request for a verdict.
Locate the standard tool.
Act as a Python performance assistant. I want to time two functions fairly.
Before any code, name the standard-library tool for timing repeated runs and its
key arguments, citing the official docs section. Only name functions you are
confident exist.
After running, verify: open the official docs and confirm the function and its defaults exist. Counters confident fabrication (an invented function name arrives as confidently as a real one).
Delegate, with a checklist to verify.
Write a harness that benchmarks dedupe_slow and dedupe_fast on the same input.
Then return a table of every assumption your timing makes: warmup, number of
repeats, what sits inside the timed block, and how realistic the input is.
After running, verify: run both functions on one input and confirm identical output with a second, simpler check. Counters plausible-but-wrong-method (a timer that quietly compares unfair conditions).
Red-team your speedup.
Here is my claim: "the hash version is about 6x faster on 10,000 records."
Act as a hostile reviewer of research software. Name every way this benchmark
could be unfair or this number could mislead. Do not rewrite the claim for me.
After running, verify: if it only praises the claim, push back and demand the single worst flaw. Counters sycophantic agreement (praise that reviews your ego, not your evidence).






ImportantDo not delegate



Specify and Defend never leave your hands. You decide what question the benchmark answers, what counts as a fair comparison, and whether the synthetic input is honest for the claim you want to make. You own the final sentence, its boundary, and its uncertainty. The tool can time the code, but it cannot decide the test was fair or the claim was earned. Those are yours.

title: 3. Specify, Delegate, Interrogate, Inspect, Verify, Document, Defend
text:
An AI failure case
You ask for a benchmark and the tool reports, with total confidence, “the hash version is 50x faster.” The number is wrong, and the code that produced it runs without a single error. Here is the trap: the harness timed each function exactly once, and the slow function ran first, on a cold start, so it paid the entire warmup cost. The comparison charged one function for a setup both of them share.
You catch it at Inspect and Verify. Reading the harness shows the single-run timing and the unlucky ordering. Re-running each function five times, dropping the first, and taking the median collapses the “50x” to about 6x. A green check is not a correct result. You verify the number, not the paragraph about the number.

title: 3. Specify, Delegate, Interrogate, Inspect, Verify, Document, Defend
text:
Verification laboratory
Verify this chapter’s key result, the speedup, with alternative code from the verification guide: recompute the same quantity a different way and compare. Time both functions again with a second mechanism, average several runs instead of taking one, and confirm the speedup holds. Then confirm correctness the same way: check that dedupe_fast and dedupe_slow return identical output on the same input, element by element. Two roads to one answer is a verified result. Two roads to two answers means the harness, not the function, is what you measured.

title: 3. Specify, Delegate, Interrogate, Inspect, Verify, Document, Defend
text:
Project transfer
This chapter turns into your M0 research-and-AI baseline, the course’s first milestone. The next time you delegate anything real on your own project, whether locating a source, drafting a rewrite, or running a small analysis, route it through all seven steps and record it as your first row in the AI Research Ledger: the task, the tool, your decision, and the named check that earned your trust. That row, plus the two decisions you refuse to delegate, is the spine of what you present at Friday’s studio.

title: 3. Specify, Delegate, Interrogate, Inspect, Verify, Document, Defend
text:
Defend your decision
In four sentences, defend one task you ran through the protocol. Name the task you delegated and the one step where you caught something the tool got wrong or left out. State the bounded claim you can now make, with its boundary (what your check does not establish). Close with your uncertainty: the one thing you still do not know, and the verification you would run next to close it.

title: 5. From Curiosity to a Research Problem
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 5. From Curiosity to a Research Problem
text:
Why this decision matters
Picture a research mentor reading your one-line pitch before your first meeting. She does not ask about your data or your software. She asks one thing: “What, exactly, is the question?” Her reason: “I can tell in a paragraph whether someone knows what they are asking. If they do not, nothing downstream saves the project.”
Everything you do later sits below this decision. A vague question makes every later choice arbitrary, because any data and any answer will seem to fit. A sharp question tells you what evidence you need and what would count as being wrong. Pick the problem well and the term has a spine. Pick it badly and no analysis will straighten it.

title: 5. From Curiosity to a Research Problem
text:
The concept
Turning curiosity into a problem is a funnel with four stops. Each stop throws away vagueness the stop above it still allowed.

Curiosity: a felt interest with no edges yet. Example: “why do some people seem to get sick more during exam weeks?” You cannot collect evidence on it, because it never says who or what to look at.
Topic: a curiosity with edges, a territory someone could gather evidence inside. Example: “sleep and illness among residence-hall first-years.”
Research problem: one specific unknown inside that territory whose absence matters. Example: “we do not know whether short sleep during finals week goes with more reported illness.”
Answerable question: the single sentence evidence can answer, and be wrong about. Example: “do first-years who sleep under six hours during finals report more sick days than those who sleep seven or more?”

A good question also carries three things it must name, or you will not know what to record.

Units of analysis: the who or what each row of your evidence stands for. Example: one student. If you cannot say what one unit is, you cannot count anything.
Target population: the full group the question is really about, beyond the handful you reach. Example: all residence-hall first-years, not only the twenty you surveyed.
Outcome: the observable thing that varies, the quantity you actually record. Example: the number of self-reported sick days. It must be measurable, even if imperfectly.

Finally, three tests decide whether a candidate problem is worth a term. Importance asks whether anyone beyond you would care about the answer. Feasibility asks whether you can get the evidence this term, with the access and time you have. Contribution asks whether the answer adds something not already settled, since a ten-minute search that finds it already known kills the contribution.
Once you have a question, you will also classify what kind of answer it wants. That is the inquiry compass, and it is the whole job of the next chapter. For now, the work is the funnel.

title: 5. From Curiosity to a Research Problem
text:
A worked example
Start with a health curiosity: “does moving more actually help you sleep?” It is a real feeling, but you cannot study it, because it names no one and measures nothing. Give it edges and it becomes a topic: physical activity and sleep quality in undergraduates. Push further to a research problem: we do not know whether more daily walking goes with better-rated sleep among first-years living in residence halls. That points at one missing piece, not a whole subject.
Now sharpen it into an answerable question: “among residence-hall first-years, do those who average more than 8,000 steps a day report better sleep quality than those who average fewer than 4,000?” The three carried terms snap into place. The unit is one first-year. The outcome is a sleep-quality rating, say a 1-to-10 self-report you collect on a phone. The population is residence-hall first-years, not the whole campus.
Now run the three tests. It clears importance (anyone advising sleep-deprived first-years wants the answer), feasibility (step counts and a nightly rating are both collectable in one term), and contribution (the activity-and-sleep link is studied broadly in adults but far less in this dorm-living, first-year group). The curiosity is now a problem you could defend.

title: 5. From Curiosity to a Research Problem
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb01 — From curiosity to a research problem: descriptive, predictive, causal. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. You will watch one curiosity sharpen the instant it touches real survey columns, then walk your own curiosity from your nb00 map down the funnel, draft two candidate problems, and defend the one you keep.

title: 5. From Curiosity to a Research Problem
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first. Only then open the tool, so its output has something of yours to disagree with.
Locate what is already known (before you claim your problem is new):
Act as a research-scoping assistant. My topic is [your topic]. List what is
already reasonably well established about it and where a genuine open question
might remain. For every established fact, give one real, retrievable source I
can open myself. Mark anything you are unsure exists.
After running, verify: open each source before you trust it. This counters confident fabrication, where a fluent answer cites a real-sounding study that does not exist.
Widen a curiosity into candidates you check:
Here is my curiosity: [one sentence]. Produce five candidate research problems
inside it. For each, name its units of analysis, its outcome, and its target
population, in a table. Do not add any topic I did not mention.
After running, verify: check each row against the curiosity you actually wrote. This counters silent scope change, where the tool quietly answers a nearby question and hands it back as yours.
Red-team the problem you chose:
Act as a hostile grant reviewer. Here is the research problem I want to commit a
term to: [paste it]. Argue the strongest case that it is not worth doing. Attack
it on importance, on feasibility, and on whether it adds anything new.
After running, verify: keep only the objections that survive your own reasoning. This counters sycophantic agreement, where a tool praises your idea because you proposed it.






ImportantDo not delegate



Three calls stay yours alone. Which problem you commit a term to. How you word the question, including its units, outcome, and population. And the judgment on whether it is important and genuinely new. A tool can widen your options and attack your choice. It cannot make the choice, because only you carry the reasons you will have to defend at the studio.

title: 5. From Curiosity to a Research Problem
text:
An AI failure case
You ask a tool to “turn my curiosity about sleep and health into a research question.” It replies, with total confidence: “Does blue-light exposure before bed reduce REM sleep in college undergraduates? Studies show blue light suppresses melatonin.” It sounds authoritative, and it is wrong for you.
It fails in two ways. First, it silently changed your scope. You never chose blue light or REM sleep, yet the question now hangs on both. That is a silent scope change, and you catch it by reading the tool’s units, outcome, and population against the ones you committed. Yours were steps and a sleep rating, not screens and REM. Second, the melatonin line is stated as settled fact with no source. Before repeating it, you find and open a real study yourself, and if you cannot, you treat it as confident fabrication and drop it.

title: 5. From Curiosity to a Research Problem
text:
Verification laboratory
The key result of this chapter is a claim about your own question: that it is answerable and carries its three terms. Verify it with peer reasoning, method 11 in the verification guide. Hand your one-sentence question to a classmate who has never seen your project, and ask them to name, from that sentence alone, what one unit is, what you would record, and who the answer is about. If they name all three, your question holds. If they stall, the question is not yet answerable, and their confusion shows which term is still missing.

title: 5. From Curiosity to a Research Problem
text:
Project transfer
This chapter builds the spine of milestone M1, your research opportunity landscape. Take the strongest curiosity from your nb00 map and walk it down the funnel: topic, research problem, then answerable question, naming its units, outcome, and population, and scoring it on importance, feasibility, and contribution. What you defend at the Friday studio is a problem you funneled and chose yourself, not a tool’s suggestion.

title: 5. From Curiosity to a Research Problem
text:
Defend your decision
In three or four sentences, state the one problem you are committing to as an answerable question, and defend it. Name the boundary: one thing this question will not let you claim, even if you answer it well. Name your uncertainty: the part of importance, feasibility, or contribution you are least sure of, and what evidence would settle it. A choice you can defend with its edge and its doubt named is a research problem. A choice without them is still a curiosity.

title: 7. Research Builds on Research
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 7. Research Builds on Research
text:
Why this decision matters
Picture the moment a thesis advisor hands your first draft back with one line circled: “You cite six papers. Which did you actually read, and where is the hole you claim to fill?” That question is the whole job. A literature built from confident-sounding summaries collapses the instant a reviewer opens one citation and finds it says something else, or finds it does not exist. This chapter’s decision turns “I looked it up” into “I retrieved it, I read it, and I can show you the empty spot on the map.”

title: 7. Research Builds on Research
text:
The concept
A field is a cumulative conversation: a running argument where each study answers, extends, or challenges the ones before it. One lab measures a reaction rate, a second repeats it with a new catalyst, a third pools both. Your job is not to invent an island. It is to find the conversation your question belongs to and add one honest sentence.
You run one habit on every source. The retrieval-verification loop has four steps: ask a tool for leads, retrieve the source yourself, verify it exists and says what was claimed, and document where you found it. A lead is a candidate you have not checked yet, such as a citation an AI hands you. It stays a lead until you retrieve and read it.
From one verified source you grow the rest by snowballing: walking its links two ways. Backward citation search reads the source’s own reference list for older work it built on. Forward citation search finds the newer papers that cite it. Two solid sources, snowballed both ways, quickly become ten.
Snowballing lets you tell a real gap from a dead end. A genuine gap is a question your verified sources actually leave unanswered, confirmed by looking both ways. An unfinished search is one you simply stopped chasing. They feel identical from your desk, and only one is a contribution.
You then arrange what you found as an evidence map: each verified source becomes one claim node (what it asserts, in one sentence), lines connect claims that agree or contradict, and one marked spot shows where no source reaches. That spot is your gap, honest only because every node around it is a source you opened.

title: 7. Research Builds on Research
text:
A worked example
Say your curiosity is green chemistry. You have read that silver nanoparticles (specks of silver billionths of a meter across) can be made without harsh reagents by reducing silver ions with a mild acid. Your question: does L-ascorbic acid (ordinary vitamin C) reduce silver ions to nanoparticles at room temperature, and does more of it make the particles smaller?
You ask an AI for the key studies. It returns a confident citation: a plausible author pair, a 2019 date, a journal called Journal of Green Chemistry. You go to read it, and the exact title returns nothing in your library portal, nothing on Google Scholar, nothing on the publisher’s site. The journal does not exist; the real Royal Society of Chemistry title is simply Green Chemistry. The citation was fabricated.
So you run the loop properly. You retrieve one real review of green nanoparticle synthesis, read it, and snowball. Its references point backward to the reduction chemistry it built on; its “Cited by” list points forward to recent ascorbic-acid studies. Within an hour your map holds a half-dozen real sources and a contradiction: one paper reports that more reductant yields smaller particles, another the opposite. That disagreement is the live edge of the field, and it tells you the question is unsettled.
Now the decision that is yours. The broad question “can vitamin C make silver nanoparticles” is no gap: the field is enormous. But your narrow question, how particle size behaves across a specific concentration range where two real papers already disagree, may be genuine. You can defend it only because you retrieved the sources around it and they still leave it open.

title: 7. Research Builds on Research
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb02 — Research builds on research: verified evidence + real gaps. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. In the lab you watch a pile of fifteen AI citations collapse to the few that survive retrieval, build an evidence map in code, and run the retrieval-verification loop on your own claim.

title: 7. Research Builds on Research
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt earns a better draft; none earns your trust.
Locate. Ask for leads to chase, not a finished bibliography.
Act as a literature scout in [your field]. My question is: [paste it].
List the five published studies most relevant to it, each with authors,
year, exact title, and venue, so I can retrieve and verify each myself.
Only include work you are confident exists; mark anything uncertain.
After running, verify: retrieve every citation by searching its exact title in quotes before you believe it exists. Counters confident fabrication (a real-sounding citation for a paper that was never written).
List-to-verify (snowball). Start from one source you have already read.
Here is one source I have verified: [authors, year, exact title, venue].
Name three older works from its reference list it likely built on, and
three newer works that likely cite it, each with authors, year, title,
and venue, plus one line on why it belongs on the chain.
After running, verify: cross-check the “backward” names against the seed’s real reference list and retrieve the rest. A tidy chain can quietly omit the one contradicting study. Counters illusion of completeness.
Red-team. Make the tool attack your gap, not flatter it.
Here is my question and the gap I placed: [one or two sentences].
Argue that this gap is NOT real. Name up to three published studies that
may already answer it, with authors, year, title, and venue, and say what
each would have to show to close it. Do not reassure me.
After running, verify: if it just agrees the gap is open and names nothing, push back and make it try to close the gap; then retrieve every study it names. Counters sycophantic agreement.






ImportantDo not delegate



Two decisions never leave your hands. Whether a source counts as verified: only reading it yourself settles that. And whether your gap is real: only retrieval that comes up empty earns the word. What your project may claim follows from those two. An AI can point; it cannot certify.

title: 7. Research Builds on Research
text:
An AI failure case
The most common failure here is citation hallucination, the taxonomy’s confident fabrication: the model invents a source and presents it with full confidence. In the worked example it gave you a plausible author pair, a 2019 date, and Journal of Green Chemistry. You caught it in a minute: you searched the exact title in quotes across your library portal and Google Scholar, found nothing, and noticed the journal does not exist. Fluent confidence is not evidence. If you cannot locate a source where it should live, treat it as fabricated until you prove otherwise.

title: 7. Research Builds on Research
text:
Verification laboratory
The key result of this chapter is a verdict: this source is verified, and this gap is (or is not) real. Verify it with primary-source reading (verification guide, method 4): find the actual source, open it, and read the line that supports the claim before you cite it. Do this for every source on your map, not a sample. For a gap you will defend out loud, add a second, independent check such as peer reasoning: walk your gap past a classmate, whose one question often exposes a source you skipped. Two independent checks that agree are far stronger than one.

title: 7. Research Builds on Research
text:
Project transfer
This chapter builds your M2 verified evidence and contribution map, the milestone you present as a gallery walk at Friday’s studio. It puts the question and guessed gap from the previous chapter to the test of retrieval. Leave with four pieces drafted: a verified-source log (author, year, where you retrieved it, its claim in your words), an evidence map naming at least one contradiction, your gap placed honestly with its honesty-test sentence, and a one-sentence contribution line that stops where your retrieval stops. That map is the ground your later research charter stands on.

title: 7. Research Builds on Research
text:
Defend your decision
In one short paragraph, state your gap in a single sentence, then defend it. Name its compass position (description or generalization, the kinds of claim this evidence work supports). Say plainly whether the gap is confirmed by retrieval or is still an unfinished search. Then name your uncertainty: how many sources you have actually opened, and what your map cannot see. If you cannot yet write the honest boundary sentence, “across the sources I retrieved and verified, none addresses my gap,” say what retrieval it still owes. That is a finding, not a failure.

title: 9. Model, Inquiry, Data Strategy, and Answer Strategy
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 9. Model, Inquiry, Data Strategy, and Answer Strategy
text:
Why this decision matters
Picture a data science lead reading your one-page design before you spend a semester on it. Her worry is simple: “Show me the exact number you are after, then show me that the way you collect and crunch the data can actually reach that number. If those two drift apart, every result you bring me is answering a question I never asked.” A design that skips this step is not rigorous. It is a hope with good formatting. Getting the four parts to agree, on paper, before any data exist, is the cheapest quality check you will ever run.

title: 9. Model, Inquiry, Data Strategy, and Answer Strategy
text:
The concept
Every research design has the same four parts. The course calls them MIDA, one letter each.

Model: your written picture of how the world could work, meaning which things exist and what could affect what. Example: “a listener’s chance of accepting a recommended song runs from 0 to 1, and a new recommender could nudge it up.”
Inquiry: the one exact quantity you want from that world, named before any data arrive. Example: “the average lift in accept rate the new recommender causes across listeners.”
Data strategy: your plan for how the data come to exist, meaning who gets sampled and who gets which condition. Example: “randomly send each session to the old or new recommender, then log every accept.”
Answer strategy: your plan for turning the data into an estimate of the inquiry. Example: “accept rate in the new group minus accept rate in the old group.”

The four are not a checklist you fill in any order. They must align, meaning your data and answer strategies actually reach the inquiry your model makes askable. Example of misalignment: a causal inquiry (“the lift the recommender causes”) served by a data strategy that never randomizes who sees what can only show an association, meaning two things move together without proof that one caused the other. Naming the misalignment on paper is the whole job here.

title: 9. Model, Inquiry, Data Strategy, and Answer Strategy
text:
A worked example
Take a music-streaming app testing whether a new autoplay recommender raises how often listeners keep the suggested track. Walk the four parts.
The model says each listener has two possible accept rates, one under the old recommender and one under the new, and the new one could be higher, lower, or the same. The inquiry is the average difference between those two rates across all listeners, one number, causal in kind. The data strategy buckets each incoming session at random into old or new, so nothing about the listener decides which version they get. The answer strategy computes the accept rate in each bucket and subtracts.
Now audit alignment. The inquiry asks for a caused difference. Random bucketing is what licenses that word, because it breaks any link between who the listener is and which version they see. So the four parts agree. Contrast a tempting shortcut: ship the new recommender to new users and compare them against existing users on the old one. That data strategy no longer aligns with a causal inquiry, because new and existing users differ in ways that also move accept rate. The honest claim collapses from “causes” to “is associated with.” Same question, broken alignment.

title: 9. Model, Inquiry, Data Strategy, and Answer Strategy
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb03 — The anatomy of a research design: MIDA + declare → diagnose → redesign. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. There you build a small simulated world, write its four MIDA parts, and watch what happens to your answer when a data strategy quietly stops matching the inquiry.

title: 9. Model, Inquiry, Data Strategy, and Answer Strategy
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Write your own draft of each part first, then ask. Committing first gives the AI output something to disagree with.
Act as a research-design tutor. Here is my research question: [paste it].
Draft the four MIDA parts as a labeled list — Model, Inquiry, Data strategy,
Answer strategy. State the Inquiry as one exact quantity. Mark each part
"confident" or "guessing." Do not smooth over gaps.
After running, verify: read the drafted Inquiry’s words next to your question’s words. Do the kind and reach match? This counters silent scope change, where the tool answers an easier, adjacent question than the one you asked.
Act as a hostile methodologist. Here are my four MIDA parts: [paste them].
Name the single worst misalignment — a place where my data or answer strategy
cannot reach my stated inquiry. Give one concrete way my design fails, not
praise.
After running, verify: check the objection against your own alignment note. This counters sycophantic agreement, where the tool calls a design sound because you proposed it.
Find one real, published study that used a design like mine to answer a similar
question. Give title, authors, year, and venue. If you are not confident it
exists, say so instead of inventing one.
After running, verify: open a library database and locate the exact study before citing it. This counters confident fabrication, where the tool returns a plausible citation for a paper that does not exist.






ImportantDo not delegate



Three choices stay yours. Which world your question assumes (the model, and so what could confound it). Which single quantity you want (the inquiry, named in your words, not the AI’s paraphrase). Whether your four parts align, and therefore whether your claim boundary is causes or only is associated with. An AI can draft candidate parts. Deciding they agree, and owning the claim that follows, is research judgment you cannot hand off.

title: 9. Model, Inquiry, Data Strategy, and Answer Strategy
text:
An AI failure case
You paste your streaming question and ask for MIDA. The tool returns four confident, well-formatted parts and calls the design “rigorous.” Read closely: it wrote the data strategy as “compare new users on the new recommender to existing users on the old one,” and it kept the inquiry worded as a caused lift. That is two failures at once. The data strategy is plausible-but-wrong, because user tenure moves accept rate on its own, and the inquiry wording is a silent scope change from association to cause. You catch it two ways: set the inquiry’s words beside the data strategy and ask whether randomization actually happened, then sketch the model as a diagram and look for an arrow from “user tenure” into both the version seen and the accept rate. That open arrow is the confounder the fluent draft never mentioned.

title: 9. Model, Inquiry, Data Strategy, and Answer Strategy
text:
Verification laboratory
The key result to verify is the alignment claim itself: that your four parts point at one quantity. Use peer reasoning from the verification guide. Explain your model, inquiry, data strategy, and answer strategy to a partner and ask them to name the part that does not reach the others. A second person’s first question usually finds the drift you stopped seeing. For any design whose inquiry uses the word causes, add a second, independent check: draw the causal diagram and confirm no open backdoor path (a common cause of both treatment and outcome) is left standing. Two independent checks that agree are far stronger than one.

title: 9. Model, Inquiry, Data Strategy, and Answer Strategy
text:
Project transfer
This chapter is the spine of milestone M3 — your research charter and MIDA declaration. Bring your own project’s question and write its four parts, then add one sentence naming which part you were least sure aligned and why. That alignment note becomes the claim boundary you defend in your charter, and it is the exact input chapter 10 builds on when you declare and diagnose the same design.

title: 9. Model, Inquiry, Data Strategy, and Answer Strategy
text:
Defend your decision
In three or four sentences, state your project’s four MIDA parts, name the single quantity your inquiry targets, and declare your claim boundary: does your data strategy earn the word causes, or does it stop at is associated with? Then name the one part you are least confident aligns, and the uncertainty that would change your answer if it turned out otherwise. A declaration with no stated boundary and no stated uncertainty is incomplete.

title: 11. Observational Descriptive Research
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 11. Observational Descriptive Research
text:
Why this decision matters
Picture a policy stakeholder reading your report before deciding whether it applies to their city. Their first question is never about your math. It is: “Which group did your procedure actually reach?” A result that quietly describes one group while claiming to speak for another is the most common failure in observational work: an estimate that is precise, well-formatted, and about the wrong group. This chapter equips you to name the group your data reach, and to refuse the sentence that swaps it for a group your data never touched.

title: 11. Observational Descriptive Research
text:
The concept
Observational descriptive research means you watch and record what is there without changing anyone’s experience, then summarize it. Example: you place air sensors in a set of rooms and report how often they read above a health threshold. You never assign a treatment, so this pathway can describe, and with care generalize, but it cannot by itself say one thing caused another.
Four nested groups keep such a description honest. Picture four boxes, each smaller than the last.

Target population: everyone your question is about. Example: every part your factory will ever cast from a new alloy. This is the outermost box, the group you wish you could study.
Accessible population: the part of the target you could reach in principle, given time and cost. Example: the parts you can actually machine into test pieces this quarter.
Sampling frame: the concrete list you draw from. Example: the batch of test coupons sitting on the lab bench. Whatever is not on that list cannot be sampled.
Sample: the pieces you actually measure. The innermost box.

A claim is honest only when the box it describes matches the box it names. Two processes decide how the boxes differ. Selection is any process that decides who lands in your data instead of chance alone. Example: a survey taken outside one dining hall selects for people who eat there at lunch. Nonresponse is the units you drew but who never gave you data. Example: of 500 people contacted, 300 reply, and the 200 silent ones are rarely a random slice. When either process relates to the very thing you are measuring, your summary drifts from the truth, and no amount of extra data pulls it back. Size shrinks the sample-to-sample wobble; it does nothing to a systematic tilt in who you reached.

title: 11. Observational Descriptive Research
text:
A worked example
A materials lab tests a new aluminum alloy for its defect rate, meaning the fraction of parts with internal cracks or voids. Example: 2 cracked parts in 100 is a 2% defect rate. The lab casts a batch, cuts each casting into small test coupons (standard sample pieces sized for the scanner), polishes them, and scans each surviving coupon. The scan reports 2%, and the headline writes itself: “the alloy has a 2% defect rate.”
Now walk the boxes. The target population is every part that will be cast from this alloy in production. The sampling frame is the coupons that survived cutting and polishing intact, because only those reach the scanner. Here is the trap: the most defective coupons, the ones riddled with internal voids, are also the ones most likely to shatter during cutting. They removed themselves from the frame before anyone measured them. This is survivorship selection, a filter that keeps exactly the pieces least likely to show the problem you are counting. The surviving coupons are the sturdy end of the alloy, not a fair slice of it.
So the 2% is honest about one box and dishonest about another. The licensed claim is: “among coupons that survived preparation intact, the defect rate is 2%.” The silent upgrade, the sentence to refuse, is: “the alloy’s defect rate is 2%.” The true production rate is almost certainly higher, because the worst pieces never reached the scan. That gap between the box you measured and the box you named is the subject of this chapter.

title: 11. Observational Descriptive Research
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb04 — Observational descriptive research. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. In the lab you treat a real voter file as a known world, draw random samples and one convenience channel from it, and watch the random draws cluster on the truth while the convenience channel misses the true mean age by nearly twelve years. Then you run a nonresponse stress test that shows a clean estimate drifting off the truth.

title: 11. Observational Descriptive Research
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt below leaves the judgment with you and hands the AI only the legwork.
Locate. Find real methodological backing instead of trusting a paragraph.
Act as a research-methods assistant. List real, retrievable references on
survivorship and nonresponse bias in observational studies. For each, give
title, authors, year, and venue, and mark any you are not confident exist.
After running, verify by opening each source in a library database before you cite it. Counters confident fabrication: a fluent citation for a paper that does not exist looks identical to a real one until you try to retrieve it.
List-to-verify. Turn a vague worry into a checkable list of who drops out.
I am drawing a selection diagram for an observational study. My target
population is [X], my frame is [Y], my sample is [Z]. List each group that
drops out between target and sample, in a table, with whether their absence
pushes my estimate up or down.
After running, verify by mapping each named group to one real step in your own diagram, and checking the one group you feared most is on the list. Counters illusion of completeness: a long, tidy list that quietly omits the group that matters most.
Red-team. Make the AI attack your boundary sentence instead of praising it.
Here is a boundary sentence I wrote: "[your sentence]". Act as a hostile peer
reviewer. Name every place it over-reaches, under-states the bias, or hides the
direction of the drift. Do not rewrite it for me.
After running, verify by checking each objection against your own numbers and pushing back if every objection is mild. Counters sycophantic agreement: an AI that calls your caveat “well-balanced” has reviewed your ego, not your evidence.






ImportantDo not delegate



Three decisions stay yours alone. First, which population your project is really about: the AI does not know your question’s intent. Second, which frame you can honestly reach, and therefore the coverage gap you must disclose. Third, the boundary line itself: the exact sentence where your description stops and the population claim you refuse to make. You may ask an AI to attack that line, never to draw it for you.

title: 11. Observational Descriptive Research
text:
An AI failure case
