You ask a chatbot: “My scan of surviving coupons shows a 2% defect rate on a large batch. Can I report the alloy’s defect rate as 2%?” It answers with full confidence: “Yes. With a sample that large, your estimate is precise and reliable.” This is wrong in two named ways at once. It commits a silent scope change, quietly upgrading “the surviving coupons” to “the alloy,” and it leans on the fallacy that a big sample cures a tilted one.
You catch it because you wrote your own answer first, and yours named the frame: the coupons that survived preparation, not the alloy, so the swap is visible on sight. Then you check the mechanism against the data. The most defective coupons shattered during cutting, so missingness relates directly to the outcome you count. A larger batch of survivors only estimates the survivors’ defect rate more precisely. It never reaches the parts that broke.

title: 11. Observational Descriptive Research
text:
Verification laboratory
The chapter’s key result is a claim about how a procedure behaves: random draws from a frame cluster on its truth, while a convenience channel can miss it no matter how large it grows. The right check for a claim about a procedure is simulation (verification_guide method 2): build a world where you already know the answer, then see whether each sampling method recovers it. The lab does exactly this. You take a file whose true mean age you can read directly, draw ten random samples of 100, and confirm the ten means huddle in a tight band around the truth. Then you keep one convenience group and watch its mean land far outside that band. Because you set the true value yourself, any method that fails to recover it is exposed at once, with no AI sentence to trust.

title: 11. Observational Descriptive Research
text:
Project transfer
This chapter is the spine of your M4 observational descriptive design audit. For your own project you will write four pieces: the population, accessible population, frame, and sample stack, one line each; a selection diagram naming the one filter you most fear bends both who you reach and what you measure; a measurement note flagging the variable you most worry is missing not-at-random; and the two sentences M4 grades. The first is the population claim your frame licenses, stated with its uncertainty. The second is the silent upgrade your design forbids, written out so you can refuse it on sight. Draft all four yourself, then defend them at Friday’s studio.

title: 11. Observational Descriptive Research
text:
Defend your decision
In a few sentences of your own: name the exact group your evidence speaks for, state the one bounded claim you would put your name on, and write the boundary where your description stops. Then name the crossing a population claim would have to pay for in your design, whether you paid it, and the single largest source of uncertainty or coverage gap that remains. If you cannot yet write the population sentence honestly, say so plainly. That is a finding about your design, not a failure, and it names the crossing you still owe.

title: 13. Experimental Descriptive Research
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 13. Experimental Descriptive Research
text:
Why this decision matters
Picture your research advisor stopping at one line of your methods page. “You randomized,” they say, “so you wrote causes. Show me the question first.” Random assignment feels causal by reflex, and that reflex is the trap this chapter defuses. The word that fixes the kind of a question is the question, not the procedure. Point a coin flip at “what would change if we deployed this?” and you have a causal study; point the same flip at “how much of this property sits in the world right now?” and you have a measurement. Mislabel the second as the first and you overclaim: you promise a lever your evidence never tested, and a careful reader stops trusting the rest of your work.

title: 13. Experimental Descriptive Research
text:
The concept
Three terms carry the idea. Meet them one at a time.

Latent characteristic: a real property you cannot read off directly, so a design has to reveal it. Example: how much a search interface’s position pulls clicks, apart from how good each result is.
Controlled stimulus: a version of a prompt, item, or setting that you fix on purpose and assign by chance, so any difference in response pins to the version rather than to who received it. Example: the same result shown in slot 1 for some sessions and slot 5 for others, chosen at random.
Experiment as a measurement instrument: using randomly assigned controlled stimuli to expose a latent characteristic and report it as a plain description. The number you report is the estimand, the exact quantity the design is built to state, and here it describes the world as it stands.

A measurement can be confident and still wrong, because the setup itself is one of the ingredients. Two artifacts do most of the damage. A demand effect is when the people being measured guess what you are looking for and drift toward it; example: users who notice they are in a study click more carefully than they would at home. An instrument effect is when the measuring device or wording moves the reading on its own; example: slot 1 renders a larger thumbnail, so the logger credits position with attention the picture earned. Both threaten construct validity, the degree to which your number estimates the concept you meant rather than something adjacent. Randomizing who sees what does nothing about a bias that hits every unit alike, which is why you design these out separately.

title: 13. Experimental Descriptive Research
text:
A worked example
A music-streaming team wants a latent property of its “Recommended for you” shelf: how much of a track’s play count comes from where it sits versus how well it matches the listener. Nobody prints that number anywhere, so they build an instrument. For a random slice of sessions they insert one fixed probe track, identical audio and identical artwork, and assign its shelf position at random across six slots, then record the play rate by slot. Because the track never changes and the slot is dealt by chance, the spread in play rate across slots isolates the pull of position alone. The estimand is the shelf’s attention curve: how much each rank commands, described for these sessions.
Here is where the decision bites. Inside the study there truly is a causal effect of slot on clicks, but the team’s question was descriptive, and the honest report says so: “for these sessions, slot 1 drew about three times the plays of slot 5, at equal quality.” The forbidden sentence is “promoting tracks to slot 1 will triple their plays,” because that is a causal, deployable claim the design never tested. And before trusting even the descriptive number, the team rules out two artifacts: a demand effect if the probe looks like an ad, and an instrument effect if slot 1’s larger art or a slot-keyed logger inflates the count. The redesign renders every slot identically and logs impressions server-side.

text:
The Colab laboratory

In it you read a real survey experiment as a measurement of a settled attitude, recover a hidden prevalence with a list experiment that a direct question cannot reach, and then declare, diagnose, and redesign a measurement experiment so a number built mostly of demand and instrument artifacts falls back onto the construct it was supposed to report.

title: 13. Experimental Descriptive Research
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt below ends with a check you run yourself.
Act as a literature scout. List three real, published experiments used to MEASURE
a latent characteristic (position bias in a ranking interface, a hidden prevalence,
a preference weight) rather than to test a deployable intervention. For each give
authors, year, venue, and the single quantity measured, in a table. Only include
work you are confident exists; mark any row you are unsure about.
After running, verify: open a database and retrieve each study yourself; any row you cannot find in a minute is a confident fabrication, so cut it.
Here is code that estimates an interface's attention curve as the difference in
play rate across randomly assigned slots: [paste]. Name every assumption this
estimator needs to be valid, and for each, one concrete way it could fail in my
logs.
After running, verify: check each assumption against your actual pipeline; an estimator that sounds standard but assumes something your data break is a plausible-but-wrong method.
Act as a hostile measurement reviewer. Here is my construct and how I measure it:
[plan]. List every demand effect and instrument effect that could produce my number
even if the true value were different, and the redesign that rules each one out.
After running, verify: compare the list against the one artifact you wrote down first; if yours is missing, the tidy output just failed the illusion of completeness test, so add it back.






ImportantDo not delegate



Three calls stay yours. Whether your inquiry is descriptive or causal, because the kind lives in your question’s words and no tool can read your intent. Whether the design actually measures your construct rather than something adjacent, which is a judgment about meaning, not about code. And which artifacts you are willing to stake your name on having ruled out. An AI partner can draft, locate, and attack. You declare and defend.

title: 13. Experimental Descriptive Research
text:
An AI failure case
You paste your attention-curve result into an AI tool and ask it to write the findings sentence. It returns something fluent: “Moving tracks to the top slot causes a 3x lift in plays, so the team should promote its best matches to slot 1.” It reads like a win, but it is a silent scope change: you asked how attention is distributed, and the tool quietly answered a causal, deployable question about what promoting would do. You catch it by laying its sentence beside yours, word for word. Yours asked how much position pulls; its answer asked what to do. The tells are “causes,” “should,” and the imagined “lift” from an intervention you never ran. Reject it, and rewrite the claim bounded to the units you observed.

title: 13. Experimental Descriptive Research
text:
Verification laboratory
Verify the lab’s key result by simulation, the verification-guide method for checking how a procedure behaves rather than a fixed number. Build a toy world where you set the true latent value yourself, run the estimator, and confirm the estimate lands on the value you planted. Then run a falsification test: set the true position pull to exactly zero and rerun. An honest instrument returns about zero. If your pipeline instead reports an attention curve where you planted none, that curve is an instrument effect your logging created, not a property of the interface, so it must be designed out before any real number is trusted.

title: 13. Experimental Descriptive Research
text:
Project transfer
This chapter is the spine of milestone M6, your experimental measurement or data-acquisition protocol. Name the latent characteristic your project must measure, the design that reveals it, and the exact descriptive quantity it reports for named units. Then write the artifact plan: the one demand effect and the one instrument effect you most fear, each with the redesign that guards it. Add the two-sentence boundary that states the descriptive claim you license with its uncertainty and the causal sentence you forbid yourself. Those pieces are what you walk a skeptic through at Friday’s studio, and they feed the abstract that clears its gate there.

title: 13. Experimental Descriptive Research
text:
Defend your decision
State the decision this chapter equips and defend it for your own project. Name your latent characteristic and the randomized stimulus that reveals it, then say in one sentence why the inquiry is descriptive, a property that already exists, and not causal, the effect of an intervention you would deploy. Close with the boundary and the uncertainty: the descriptive claim your design licenses, the worst artifact you have not fully ruled out, and the interval or caveat around your number. A declaration with no boundary and no uncertainty is not yet a defense.

title: 15. Experimental Causal Research
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 15. Experimental Causal Research
text:
Why this decision matters

“Everyone shows me the treated group did better. So did the patients who were healthier to begin with, or more organized, or younger. ‘Better after treatment’ is not ‘the treatment worked.’ Show me that chance, not the kind of patient, decided who got treated, and tell me exactly who your number speaks for.” — a clinical-trial reviewer, reading a claim that a new reminder improved medication adherence

This is the hardest claim you make in this course, a causal one. A tool can now clean your data, run the comparison, and hand you a confident number in seconds. That speed is the danger. If you paste back “the treatment worked,” you have signed your name to a claim you have not earned. The reviewer above does not care how fluent the output sounded. She cares how treatment was assigned and who the number describes. This chapter gives you the one move that earns the word because, and the discipline to say who it applies to.

title: 15. Experimental Causal Research
text:
The concept
Start with a causal question, a question about what would change if you intervened rather than what merely goes together. Example: “does a text reminder raise the share of patients who refill on time?”, not “do patients who got reminders refill more?”
For one unit, one patient, write two numbers. The potential outcome Y(1) is what that unit would show with the treatment. Example: whether Ms. R refills on time if she gets the texts. The potential outcome Y(0) is what the same unit would show without it. Example: whether that same Ms. R refills if she gets nothing. The causal effect for her is Y(1) minus Y(0). Now the rule that governs the whole field, the fundamental problem of causal inference: for any one unit you only ever see one of those two numbers. Ms. R was either texted or not, never both, so her individual effect is unrecoverable.
So you give up the individual and chase the average. The average treatment effect (ATE) is the average of Y(1) minus Y(0) across all your units, the typical effect rather than any one unit’s. Example: on average, the reminder raised on-time refills by a few points. One move unlocks an honest estimate of it. Random assignment lets pure chance, a coin flip, decide which units get treated. Example: a computer flips a fair coin for each patient. Chance knows nothing about health or organization, so the two groups it builds are alike on average, and the untreated group’s outcome honestly stands in for what the treated group would have done untreated. That is what random assignment defeats: a confounder, a third trait that pushes on both who gets treated and the outcome. Example: more organized patients both refill anyway and are easier to enroll, so a reminder looks effective even when it did nothing. Randomizing breaks the link between any trait and who gets treated, and that broken link is the entire reason an experiment can say because.

title: 15. Experimental Causal Research
text:
A worked example
You run a clinic and want to know whether a daily text-message reminder raises the share of hypertension patients who refill their blood-pressure prescription on time. You enroll 800 patients and let a computer flip a fair coin for each: 400 to the reminder arm (the treatment), 400 to a no-text control arm.
For Ms. R, Y(1) is whether she refills if she gets the texts, and Y(0) is whether she refills if she gets nothing. You will only ever see one. Across all 800, you estimate the ATE as the difference in on-time refill rates between the arms. Say the reminder arm refills at 71 percent and the control arm at 62 percent, a 9-percentage-point gap. Because the coin, not the patient’s organization, sorted the arms, that 9 points is an honest estimate of the reminder’s average effect.
Now the honesty test. Suppose the sickest patients in the reminder arm stopped answering follow-up and left the study. Attrition is units leaving before you measure the outcome. Example: the patients whose blood pressure was worst drop out of the arm you are studying. When who leaves is tied to the outcome, the patients who remain are no longer a fair comparison, and your 9-point gap now describes only patients healthy enough to stay, not everyone you enrolled. Attrition has two siblings the lab drills. Noncompliance is assigned units that never take the treatment, like patients who changed numbers and never got a text. Spillover is treatment reaching the control arm, like a texted patient reminding an untreated friend. Each one leaves the coin flip untouched and still changes which quantity your number describes. Naming that quantity is the decision this chapter equips.

title: 15. Experimental Causal Research
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb08 — Experimental causal research. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. There you analyze a real get-out-the-vote field experiment, estimate the effect as a difference in means, and put a randomization-inference interval around it. Then you watch attrition, noncompliance, and spillover each bend a clean estimate, so you learn to name which quantity actually survives.

title: 15. Experimental Causal Research
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt below is a checkable job, not a request for a verdict.
Locate the standard tool.
Act as a statistics assistant. I ran a two-arm randomized trial and computed a
plain difference in on-time refill rates between a reminder arm and a control arm.
Before any code, name the standard way to attach a 95% interval to a difference in
two proportions, and one resampling alternative, citing where each is documented.
Only name methods you are confident exist.
After running, verify: open the cited documentation and confirm the method and its formula exist as described. Counters confident fabrication (an invented method name arrives as confidently as a real one).
List the assumptions to verify.
Here is my analysis of a randomized reminder trial: [paste your cell]. List, as a
table, every assumption my causal reading rests on: that assignment was truly
random, that no one dropped out in a way tied to the outcome, that the control arm
got no treatment. For each, say what I would check in my own data to confirm it.
After running, verify: check each listed assumption against your actual design and retention numbers before you accept the causal reading. Counters silent scope change (an association quietly reported as a settled cause).
Red-team your effect.
My claim: "the reminder raised on-time refills by 9 points." Act as a hostile
clinical-trial reviewer. Name every way dropout, noncompliance, or spillover could
make this number describe a different group than everyone I enrolled. Do not
rewrite the claim for me.
After running, verify: if it only praises the design, push back and demand the single worst threat, then test it against your retention by arm. Counters sycophantic agreement (praise that reviews your ego, not your evidence).






ImportantDo not delegate



Three calls never leave your hands. You decide whether random assignment actually happened in your study, because a tool cannot see how you sorted the arms. You decide which quantity your number estimates and for whom, the effect for everyone enrolled or only the effect among those who stayed. And you decide whether the effect is large enough to act on, and whether it is even ethical to randomize real patients and withhold something from the control arm. You own the final sentence, its boundary, and its uncertainty.

title: 15. Experimental Causal Research
text:
An AI failure case
You paste your trial into the tool and it reports, with full confidence, “the reminder raised on-time refills by 12 points, and the effect is significant.” The code runs without a single error. Here is the trap. A third of the reminder arm, the patients whose blood pressure was worst, stopped answering follow-up and left the study, so the tool computed the gap among the patients who stayed and labeled it “the effect of the reminder.” That is a silent scope change. The number now describes only the healthier patients who remained, not everyone you enrolled, and dropping the worst cases pushed it up.
You catch it by checking retention by arm, not by re-reading the fluent paragraph. When you see the reminder arm lost far more patients than the control arm, and the ones it lost were the sickest, you know the 12 points is the effect among stayers. A green check is not a correct result. You rewrite the sentence to name the group the number actually speaks for.

title: 15. Experimental Causal Research
text:
Verification laboratory
Verify the chapter’s key result, that the gap is more than chance, with two methods from the verification guide. First, simulation: re-randomize. Shuffle the treatment labels thousands of times as if you re-ran the coin flips under the assumption that the reminder does nothing, recompute the gap each time, and see how rarely pure chance produces a gap as large as yours. This is randomization inference, asking how often chance alone would hand you a gap this big if the treatment truly did nothing, and it earns you an interval rather than assuming one from a formula. Second, alternative code: recompute the same difference a second way, filtering each arm by hand and subtracting the two refill rates, and confirm you land on the same number. Two independent roads to one answer is a verified result. Two roads to two answers means your pipeline, not the reminder, is what you measured.

title: 15. Experimental Causal Research
text:
Project transfer
This chapter becomes your M8 minimum viable analysis, the first real estimate on your own question, which you pitch at this week’s Friday studio. Draft its spine: the potential outcomes Y(1) and Y(0) for one unit and which one your data will observe; the number M8 reports (a difference in means, or your pathway’s equivalent) and the interval you attach; whether your design is realistically powered for the effect you expect; the single threat nearest your design (attrition, noncompliance, spillover, or confounding if you are not randomizing); and one sentence naming exactly what your estimate means and for whom, plus the overclaim you forbid. Those five pieces are what you pitch in 60 seconds to a room of skeptics.

title: 15. Experimental Causal Research
text:
Defend your decision
In four sentences, defend the causal reading of one estimate from your own project. Name your treatment and your outcome, and state whether random assignment licenses reading your difference as a causal effect. Name the exact quantity your number estimates and for whom, for example “the effect among patients who complied,” not “the effect for everyone.” State the boundary, what a clean randomization does not rescue if attrition, noncompliance, or spillover struck. Close with your uncertainty: the width of your interval or the threat you most fear, and the one check you would run next to close it.

title: 17. Data Provenance and Data Quality
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 17. Data Provenance and Data Quality
text:
Why this decision matters
Picture a thesis advisor, the researcher who signs off on your project, reading the first draft of your background section:

“You wrote that this solvent dissolves 13 grams per liter at room temperature. Where did that number come from? Not which website copied it. Who actually put the compound in water, weighed it, and at what temperature? Bring me the paper that measured it, or take the number out.”

A value with no traceable origin is a rumor with a decimal point. Precision is not the same as trust. This chapter trains you to stop treating a number or a citation as true because it arrived looking tidy, and to ask two questions of everything you borrow: where did it come from, and is it any good.

title: 17. Data Provenance and Data Quality
text:
The concept
Data provenance is the documented origin of a value and every hand it passed through before it reached you. Example: a solubility figure printed in your textbook, traced back through a 2010 review that quoted it to the lab paper that first measured it in 1968.
That chain has two kinds of links. A primary source is the original report where a value was first measured. Example: the paper whose authors actually ran the experiment. A secondary source re-reports a value it did not measure. Example: a review or textbook that copies the primary paper’s number. A value is only as trustworthy as the primary source at the end of its chain.
Data quality is whether a value is fit for your specific use: real, measured rather than guessed, and obtained under the conditions your project needs. Example: a solubility measured at 25°C is high quality for a room-temperature question and the wrong value for a reaction you run at 60°C. A mischaracterized value is a real number attached to the wrong conditions or method. Example: a figure a model computed reported as if it were measured, or a 40°C reading cited as the 25°C value.
You settle both questions with one habit, the retrieval-verification loop: Ask a tool to surface a value and its source, Retrieve the primary source yourself, Verify that it exists and reports that value under those conditions, and Document where you found it. A citation you have not opened is a lead, not a source, however confident the tool that handed it to you.

title: 17. Data Provenance and Data Quality
text:
A worked example
You are studying greener extraction methods and you need one number: how much caffeine dissolves in water at 25°C. You ask an AI assistant, and it answers instantly: “21.7 g/L at 25°C,” with an author, a year, and a real-looking journal.
You run the loop instead of copying the number. First, provenance. You search the exact title of that citation. If it resolves nowhere, the value is orphaned and you drop it. If the paper is real, you check whether it measured the solubility or merely quoted it from an older study, which sends you one link further back toward the primary paper that put caffeine in water and weighed what dissolved.
Then, quality. You open the primary source and read the conditions line. Was it 25°C, your temperature, or 40°C? Was the value measured on a balance or predicted by software? You cross-check the figure against an independent reference, the free NIST Chemistry WebBook (webbook.nist.gov) or PubChem, both of which cite their own sources. When your citation, the primary paper, and the reference database agree on the value and the temperature, the number has earned a place in your project. When they disagree, the disagreement is the finding: the literature is not settled, so you report a range rather than a false point.

title: 17. Data Provenance and Data Quality
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb02 — Research builds on research: verified evidence + real gaps. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. In the lab you run the retrieval-verification loop on a claim from your own project, watch a tidy pile of AI-suggested citations collapse to the few that survive retrieval, and follow citation chains backward and forward into an evidence map whose one empty square is a gap you can defend, because every source around it is one you opened yourself.

title: 17. Data Provenance and Data Quality
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Each prompt is commit-first: write down the value or source you already believe, then let the tool disagree with something of yours.
Locate the primary source (delegable).
Act as a chemistry literature assistant. I have this value: [paste value, units,
conditions]. Name the primary published source that FIRST measured it, with authors,
year, exact title, journal, and the temperature and method reported. Only list work
you are confident exists; mark anything uncertain, and flag whether each is a primary
measurement or a secondary re-report.
After running, verify (counters confident fabrication): open every citation yourself before you trust it; a source you cannot retrieve is invented until it opens.
Turn borrowed numbers into a provenance table (delegable).
Here are five numbers my project borrows: [paste them]. For each, return a row with
the value, its stated conditions (temperature, pressure, solvent), the primary source,
and whether the value was measured or computed. Return it as a table so I can audit
each row on its own.
After running, verify (counters silent scope change): read each row’s conditions against the conditions your project actually needs; a value quietly measured at the wrong temperature can be handed to you as if it were yours.
Red-team the number you like (delegable).
I plan to build on this value: [paste value and source]. Act as a hostile reviewer
and argue it is unreliable for a reaction run at [your conditions]: wrong temperature,
computed not measured, or copied through a chain of reviews. Do not reassure me.
After running, verify (counters sycophantic agreement): if the reviewer only praises your number, push back and take its strongest objection to the primary source or your instructor rather than trusting easy approval.






ImportantDo not delegate



Three calls stay yours. Whether a value counts as verified is settled by a source you opened, not by the tool’s confidence. Whether its measurement conditions fit your use is a judgment about your specific reaction, and no lookup makes it. And how much uncertainty you report when your sources disagree is yours to state and defend, because your name goes on the claim the number supports.

title: 17. Data Provenance and Data Quality
text:
An AI failure case
You ask for the caffeine solubility and the tool returns “21.7 g/L at 25°C,” with a citation to a real journal. Seven details look right, so it is tempting to paste it in. You open the primary paper instead, and the conditions line reads 40°C, not 25°C. The number is real. The temperature is wrong for your question, and the tool swapped it silently. You catch it two ways. You had written your own expectation first, so a value that sits oddly high for room temperature already looked suspect. Then you read the actual conditions line rather than the tool’s summary of it, and the mismatch is plain. The value goes into your notes as a 40°C figure, and your 25°C question stays open.

title: 17. Data Provenance and Data Quality
text:
Verification laboratory
The key result here is a value with a claimed origin, so verify it with primary-source reading: find the paper that first measured the number and read the line that reports it and its conditions, not a summary of that line. Because a borrowed number reaches your poster and your defense, treat it as high-stakes and add a second, independent check, a benchmark result: compare the value against a known-good reference such as the NIST Chemistry WebBook. Two independent confirmations that agree guard against trusting a single source that quietly copied its number from somewhere else.

title: 17. Data Provenance and Data Quality
text:
Project transfer
This chapter sharpens your M2 verified evidence and contribution map, the milestone you present at Friday’s studio. Every entry in your M2 source log already needs an author, a year, and where you retrieved it. Add two columns: whether the entry is a primary source or a secondary re-report, and the conditions or scope under which its central value holds. A log built this way lets a skeptic trace any number you use back to the bench where it was made, which is what makes your gap and your contribution line believable.

title: 17. Data Provenance and Data Quality
text:
Defend your decision
In a short paragraph, take one value your project depends on and defend building on it. Name the value and its conditions, name the primary source you traced it to and where you retrieved it, and state the boundary: under what conditions the value no longer applies to your work. Then give your uncertainty in one sentence, including whether any of your sources disagree on the number. If you cannot yet name the primary source behind a value you are using, say so plainly. That admission is a finding about the borrowed number you most need to check before Friday.

title: 19. AI as Programmer
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 19. AI as Programmer
text:
Why this decision matters

“Do not read me the number. Tell me what set it was computed over. If the code only saw the buildings that already had sensors, you have not described the campus. You have described the sensors.” — a facilities-safety reviewer, asked to act on an indoor-air-quality report

An AI can write your analysis code in seconds, and it will run. That is exactly where the danger sits. The tool writes fluent code for the quantity it thinks you asked for, over whatever cases sit in the dataframe you handed it. Paste the printed number into your report and you have signed your name to a quantity you never checked, over a set you never chose. The reviewer above does not care that the code was clean. She cares what it counted, and over whom.

title: 19. AI as Programmer
text:
The concept
The tool is the programmer. You are the researcher. Two decisions never move to the programmer’s side of the desk, and this chapter is about holding them.

Descriptive summary: a number or picture that reports what is actually in the cases you can see, such as a share, an average, or a full distribution. Example: the fraction of monitored rooms whose air crosses a health line.
Quantity of interest: the one exact number your code is supposed to produce, pinned down in words before you ask for any code. Example: “the share of academic buildings whose peak-hour CO₂ exceeds 1000 ppm.”
Frame: the concrete set of cases your data actually covers, which is often smaller than the group your question is about. Example: only the buildings that already have sensors bolted to a wall.
Convenience sample: a set of cases collected because they were easy to reach, not drawn to stand in for a population. Example: those sensored buildings, which tend to be the newest and best-ventilated on campus.

Here is the trap the chapter closes. A tool can compute a flawless share over the wrong frame, and the code still finishes with a green check. A clean run is not a correct result: a cell can execute with no error and still compute a different number than your question needs. So you own two things the programmer cannot. First, you define the quantity of interest and the frame it runs over, in plain words, before you delegate. Second, you verify the returned code computed that quantity over that frame, not a plausible neighbor.

title: 19. AI as Programmer
text:
A worked example
You want to describe indoor air quality across campus for the question what share of academic buildings exceed the healthy CO₂ threshold during class hours? You have a spreadsheet of sensor readings, and you ask an AI to write the Python that returns the share above 1000 ppm.
It writes six clean lines. They run. The printout says 6% of buildings exceed the threshold, and the temptation is to write “campus air is fine.” Instead you read what the code did, and two things jump out. The dataframe holds only the eight buildings that already carry permanent sensors, the newest and best ventilated on campus. That is a convenience sample standing in silently for the whole campus. The code also calls .dropna(), and the dropped rows are the readings where cheap sensors failed, clustered in the oldest, most crowded rooms. The 6% describes the best buildings with their worst rooms deleted.
Re-anchor to your quantity of interest: the share over all academic buildings on the registrar’s list, peak-hour readings kept. Recompute over the full list, flag the failed-sensor rooms rather than dropping them, and the honest share is far higher. The tool wrote correct code for the wrong question. You did the research by deciding what the code was allowed to count.

title: 19. AI as Programmer
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb04 — Observational descriptive research. Open it in Colab with the badge above. There you hand cells to Gemini and make it earn your trust: you ask it to explain, line by line, why ten random samples huddle around a frame’s true mean while one convenience channel misses it by nearly twelve years, then confirm the range it quotes against the numbers your own cell actually printed. That gap between the tool’s fluent story and your printout is this chapter’s lesson, run live.

title: 19. AI as Programmer
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt is a checkable job, not a request for a verdict.
Locate the standard idiom.
Act as a Python data assistant. I need the share of rows above a threshold,
computed only over a subset I will name. Before any code, name the standard pandas
idiom for a threshold share over a filtered subset and the exact argument that
controls whether missing values are dropped. Only name functions you are sure exist.
After running, verify: open the pandas docs and confirm the method and that argument exist and default as described. Counters confident fabrication (an invented method or argument arrives as confidently as a real one).
Delegate, then demand the choices back as a table.
Write code that returns the share of academic buildings whose peak-hour CO2 exceeds
1000 ppm. Then return a table of every choice you made: which rows you kept, whether
you dropped any and why, the exact set the share is computed over, and whether you
reported a share or an average.
After running, verify: check the row count before and after the code, and confirm the set it computed over is the one your question names. Counters silent scope change (a share quietly computed over the sensored buildings, not the campus).
Red-team the number.
Here is my claim: "6% of academic buildings exceed the CO2 threshold." Act as a
hostile facilities reviewer. Name every way this code could have counted the wrong
quantity or this share could mislead. Do not rewrite the claim for me.
After running, verify: if it only praises the number, push back and demand the single worst flaw. Counters sycophantic agreement (praise that reviews your ego, not your evidence).






ImportantDo not delegate



You define what quantity the code computes and which set of cases it runs over, and you decide what the number is allowed to claim. The tool can write the filter, the aggregation, and the plot, but it cannot decide that the sensored buildings speak for the campus, or that a share with the worst rooms dropped is honest. You own the final sentence, its frame, and its boundary.

title: 19. AI as Programmer
text:
An AI failure case
You ask for the threshold share and the tool returns tidy code that prints, with full confidence, “6% of buildings exceed 1000 ppm — air quality is within norms.” The code has no error and reads beautifully. The trap is buried in two lines: the input dataframe was already limited to buildings with installed sensors, and a .dropna() silently removed the rooms where sensors failed. The share is real arithmetic over the wrong set.
You catch it by reading the code, not its summary. Printing the dataframe’s shape shows eight buildings, not the campus. Printing the row count before and after .dropna() shows how many readings vanished, and inspecting which reveals the worst-ventilated rooms. Recomputing over the registrar’s full building list, failures flagged instead of dropped, collapses the reassuring 6%. A green check certified that the code ran, never that it answered your question.

title: 19. AI as Programmer
text:
Verification laboratory
Verify this chapter’s key result, the threshold share, with alternative code from the verification guide: recompute the same quantity a different way and compare. If the tool used a filtered .mean() on a boolean mask, recount with a groupby over the same set and confirm the two shares match to the digit. Then catch the silent trap: print the dataframe’s shape and the row count before and after any drop, so a disappearing row cannot hide inside a clean number. Two roads to one share is a verified result. Two roads to two shares means the code, not the campus, is what you measured.

title: 19. AI as Programmer
text:
Project transfer
This is the code half of your M4 observational descriptive design audit. Before you delegate the code that produces any descriptive number for your project, write your quantity of interest and its frame in one sentence each, the way nb04 has you name the target population, accessible population, frame, and sample. Then verify the returned code computed that quantity over that frame, and record the row-count check that proved it in your AI Research Ledger: the task, the tool, the decision, the named check. That verified number is what your frame-and-sample stack rests on when you walk a skeptic through it at Friday’s studio.

title: 19. AI as Programmer
text:
Defend your decision
In four sentences, defend one descriptive number you delegated code to compute. Name the exact quantity of interest and the set of cases the code ran over. State the one verification that confirmed the code computed that quantity, not a neighbor. Give the boundary, what the number does not establish about the population beyond your frame, and close with your remaining uncertainty and the next check you would run to close it.

title: 21. Robustness and Sensitivity
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 21. Robustness and Sensitivity
text:
Why this decision matters

“Everyone shows me the version of the analysis that worked. I assume that one exists. My real question is what happened to all the reasonable versions you could have run instead, and whether you looked at them before or after you saw this answer.” — a journal reviewer, opening the first serious read your work will get

The reviewer is not accusing you of cheating. She is naming a plain fact about analysis. Almost every real estimate depends on choices you could have made differently, and any single choice can flatter the result. If you report only the version that worked, you have handed her a number with no way to tell a delicate truth from a lucky slice. This chapter gives you the habit that answers her before she has to ask.

title: 21. Robustness and Sensitivity
text:
The concept
An estimate is not one number. It is one number that depends on several choices, and a critic attacks the choices. So you attack them first. Two named attacks do this work, and people mix them up constantly.
A robustness check is re-running the same finding under a different but equally defensible choice, to see whether the answer stays. Example: you reported an average strength, so you also compute it after dropping the one specimen that failed at the grip, and confirm the story holds. A sensitivity check, which some fields call a sensitivity analysis, is deliberately changing an assumption to see how far the answer moves. Example: you assumed your load cell was calibrated, so you ask how much your result would shift if it were off by two percent.
Both attacks aim at the same target, the headline estimate: the single number that stands in for your whole finding. You attack it from four handles, and any honest plan turns one or more of them. The sample is which data you keep. The measurement is how you turn a concept into a number. The specification is the bundle of modeling choices behind the estimate. The metric is the yardstick you report.
Turning every handle at once and plotting the results gives a specification curve: a picture of one estimate under many defensible choices side by side. If the whole curve stays on one side of zero, your direction is robust. Its spread is your honest range.
The danger has a name. Specification searching, whose everyday name is p-hacking, is running many analyses and reporting only the one that gave the result you wanted, without disclosing the search. Its opposite is pre-listed checks: writing down every check you will run, and committing to report all of them, before you see any result. Same code, same table, opposite integrity. The only difference is the order of looking and deciding.

title: 21. Robustness and Sensitivity
text:
A worked example
Your lab tests whether a new heat treatment raises the yield strength of a steel alloy, the stress at which the metal begins to deform permanently. Your headline: the treated bars yield about twelve percent higher than the untreated ones. Before you defend that number, you attack it across the four handles.

Sample. All bars, or all bars except the one that fractured inside the machine’s grip, which is a legitimate mechanical exclusion, not a convenient one.
Measurement. Yield read by the standard 0.2 percent offset method, or read at the upper yield point.
Specification. Force divided by the nominal cross-section, or divided by each bar’s actual machined area.
Metric. The gain in megapascals, or the gain as a percent.

You pre-list all sixteen combinations, run them, and plot the specification curve. Every combination comes back positive: the treatment helps no matter which defensible choice you make. The magnitude ranges from about plus eight percent to plus fifteen percent. So the honest headline is not “twelve percent.” It is “higher yield in every specification we ran, by roughly eight to fifteen percent.” Then you run one more attack, a placebo test: you split bars that all got the same treatment into fake “A” and “B” groups and confirm the gap between them sits near zero. If identical bars showed a gap, your measurement, not the treatment, would be manufacturing it.

title: 21. Robustness and Sensitivity
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb09 — Share the research + attack the analysis. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. There you rerun one headline gap across a pre-listed grid and read its specification curve, watch specification searching manufacture a “significant” result from data with no real effect, and run a placebo test and a leave-one-out influence check on seeded code so you feel the difference between a robust finding and a fragile one.

title: 21. Robustness and Sensitivity
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt is a checkable job, not a request for a verdict.
Locate the standard checks.
Act as a methods assistant for a two-group strength comparison. Before any code,
name the standard families of robustness and sensitivity checks for this design and
the four things I can vary to stress the estimate. Cite a real methods reference you
are confident exists; if you are unsure a source is real, say so.
After running, verify: open the reference and confirm the checks and the source exist. Counters confident fabrication (an invented method or citation arrives as confidently as a real one).
Delegate, with a list to verify.
Here is my pre-listed robustness grid for one estimate: I vary the sample (all bars
/ drop the grip failure), the measurement (0.2% offset / upper yield point), the
specification (nominal / actual area), and the metric (MPa / percent). Name up to
three additional, equally defensible specifications I did NOT list, and for each say
which direction you expect it to push the estimate.
After running, verify: check each suggestion against the four handles and add only the ones you can actually run on your bars. Counters illusion of completeness (a tidy grid that quietly omits the one choice that would break the claim).
Red-team your range.
Here is my claim: "higher yield in every specification we ran, by roughly 8 to 15
percent." Act as a hostile reviewer of a materials study. Name the single worst way
this could be specification searching rather than robustness, and the one check that
would expose it. Do not rewrite the claim for me.
After running, verify: run the check it names against your own data; the flaw is real only if your numbers confirm it. Counters sycophantic agreement (praise that reviews your ego, not your evidence).






ImportantDo not delegate



Three calls stay yours. You decide which checks you commit to before you look, which flagged issues the data actually confirms, and the final claim, with its range and its boundary, that you defend. A reviewer, human or AI, proposes. You verify against your bars, and the evidence decides.

title: 21. Robustness and Sensitivity
text:
An AI failure case
You paste your four-handle grid and ask an AI to write your robustness section. Back comes a long, well-organized passage that runs the sample handle, the specification handle, and the metric handle, and closes with “the twelve percent result is robust across all standard specifications.” It reads as thorough and complete. The trap is that it never listed the measurement handle, the 0.2 percent offset versus upper yield point, and that is precisely the choice that moves your estimate the most. The section looks exhaustive while omitting the one check a reviewer would reach for first.
You catch it because you pre-listed all four handles yourself before opening the tool. Reading its section against your own list, you notice measurement is missing, run that comparison, and watch the estimate slide toward the low end of your range. A passage that looks complete is not the same as one that is complete. You check its list against yours, not against how finished it sounds.

title: 21. Robustness and Sensitivity
text:
Verification laboratory
Verify this chapter’s key result, the specification curve, with alternative code from the verification guide: recompute one point of the curve a second, simpler way and compare. Take a single specification, filter the two groups by hand, subtract their mean strengths, and confirm the number matches what the automated grid printed. Two roads to one answer is a verified point. Then use a counterexample hunt on the whole curve: try to build one defensible specification that flips the sign. If you cannot find one after an honest search, your “direction is robust” claim has earned its place. If you can, that specification belongs in your report, not your recycling bin.

title: 21. Robustness and Sensitivity
text:
Project transfer
This chapter turns into your M9 research audit and poster draft. Take the estimate you produced in M8 and write a short, pre-listed robustness plan for it: three checks, in order, chosen for the choices a hostile reviewer is most likely to attack, plus one placebo or leave-one-out check. Run all of them, report the whole picture, and record each as a row in your AI Research Ledger with the verification method that earned your trust. That pre-listed plan, and the range it produces, is the spine of what you defend at Friday’s studio.

title: 21. Robustness and Sensitivity
text:
Defend your decision
In four sentences, defend your robustness plan. State your headline as a direction plus a range, not a single number, and name the one check that would most likely have flipped it. State the boundary: name one thing a passed robustness plan does not establish, such as turning a correlation into a cause. Close with your uncertainty: the one specification you still are not sure about, and the check you would run next to settle it.

title: 23. AI as Adversarial Reviewer
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 23. AI as Adversarial Reviewer
text:
Why this decision matters
Picture the review of a pull request that claims a speedup. A staff engineer is a senior developer whose sign-off lets your change ship to real users, and who answers for it when production tells a different story. Example: the engineer who approved your caching change is the one paged when latency climbs at 2 a.m. Their concern is blunt.

“Do not tell me the numbers got better. Tell me which check you ran that would have caught it if they hadn’t. I trust the check, not the graph.”

A confident benchmark, a fluent AI critique, and a clean-looking table can all be wrong in the same convincing way. The engineer is asking for what sounds least impressive and matters most: the check you committed to before you looked, and the flag you actually verified against the data.

title: 23. AI as Adversarial Reviewer
text:
The concept
An adversarial reviewer is a reader whose job is to attack your result and find where it breaks, not to praise it. Example: a peer who tries to show your benchmark was luck rather than a real improvement. The reviewer can be a classmate, one AI tool, or a panel of models, and each fails in its own way.
You defend a result by trying to break it first. A robustness check re-runs the same finding under a different but equally defensible choice and sees whether the answer holds. Example: you measured a speedup on one workload, so you also measure it on two other realistic workloads. A placebo test runs your exact procedure where the effect cannot exist and confirms it comes back near zero. Example: you benchmark an unchanged build against an identical copy of itself, and any “speedup” between them is your harness lying.
The habit that keeps an attack honest is order. Specification searching means trying many versions of an analysis and reporting only the one that gave the answer you wanted, without disclosing the search. Its everyday name is p-hacking. Example: you benchmark ten input sizes and slide only the size where your change wins. The cure is a set of pre-listed checks, the full list you will run, committed to before you see any result. Example: you write down three workloads and one placebo, then run all four and report every number, including the ones that did not help.
One more failure earns a name because AI reviewers make it constantly. Correlated error is two reviewers wrong in the same way, so their agreement is an echo, not a confirmation. Example: two AI models share a blind spot and both flag the same non-issue with equal confidence. Across all of this the rule is one sentence: a reviewer proposes a flaw, and the data decides whether it is real. Confidence, human or machine, tells you nothing about whether the flaw exists.

title: 23. AI as Adversarial Reviewer
text:
A worked example
You added an in-memory cache to a web service, and your benchmark reports responses got faster. Before you defend that, you attack it. First, one term. p95 latency is the response time that 95 out of 100 requests come in under, a fairer summary than the average because it captures the slow tail your users actually feel. Example: a p95 of 210 milliseconds means only the slowest 5 percent took longer than that. Your headline is a p95 drop from 210 milliseconds to 140.
You hand the result to three adversarial reviewers, and each names a different fatal flaw. The first says the win is driven by a single warm-up run where the cache was already full. The second says you cherry-picked the one request mix where caching helps. The third says you compared a fresh deploy against a tired one, so you measured the restart, not the cache. All three are confident, and all three disagree.
You do not trust the loudest voice. You turn each flaw into a check. Leave-one-out drops the warm-up run and recomputes the p95: it barely moves, so the first flaw is refuted. Your pre-listed specification grid reruns the drop across three request mixes, and it holds in all three, so the second is refuted too. The placebo benchmarks the unchanged build against an identical copy of itself. A speedup between two identical builds would prove the third reviewer right, but it comes back near zero, so the machinery is clean.
The two loudest flags dissolved the moment a check touched them. The flaw that survives is one no benchmark can remove and none of the three raised: this ran on your laptop, not on production hardware. So the honest claim is bounded. You measured a p95 improvement on these three workloads on this machine, not a guarantee for every endpoint your users hit.

title: 23. AI as Adversarial Reviewer
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb09, Share the Research and Attack the Analysis. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. You rerun one estimate across a pre-listed grid of eight specifications, watch specification searching manufacture a significant result from pure noise, run a placebo and a leave-one-out influence check, then hand your analysis to a human, one AI, and a panel of AI models and adjudicate every flag they raise against the data.

title: 23. AI as Adversarial Reviewer
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own read of the result first, then let a reviewer attack it. Each prompt below hands out a task you can check, with a verify note naming the failure it defends against.
Red-team the result (you run the check it names).
Act as a hostile performance reviewer, not a cheerleader. Here is a one-paragraph
summary of my benchmark and its headline claim: [paste your summary]. Name the
single most serious flaw you can find, and state the exact measurement I could run
that would confirm or refute it. Do not rewrite my benchmark and do not list more
than one flaw.
After running, verify (counters sycophantic agreement): if it calls your setup “sound” or offers only mild praise, push back and ask for the worst problem assuming the result is fake. A flaw is real only once you run the named measurement and your own numbers confirm it.
List the specifications you left off (you audit the list).
Here is my pre-listed robustness grid for one benchmark result: I vary the workload
mix, the input size, and whether the cache is pre-warmed. Name up to three
additional, equally defensible specifications I did NOT list that could move the
result, and for each say which direction you expect it to push.
After running, verify (counters illusion of completeness): a long, tidy grid can still omit the one setting that breaks the claim. Add and re-run the suggestions you can actually operationalize, and discard the rest.
Collapse the panel’s flags (you keep the verdict).
Three reviewers each named one "most serious flaw" in my analysis: [A], [B], [C].
For each, give the single data check that would confirm or refute it, in one line.
Then tell me which two of the three are most likely the same underlying concern in
different words, and which one is genuinely separate.
After running, verify (counters correlated errors): three reviewers echoing one blind spot can feel like three confirmations. Run each check against your own output before believing any flag, and treat unanimous agreement as a candidate to check, never as the check itself.






ImportantDo not delegate



These stay yours, no matter how fluent the reviewer sounds. Which robustness checks you commit to before you look, because a check chosen after you see the result is not a check, it is a search. Which flagged flaws your data actually confirm, decided by the measurement and not by the confidence. Whether a flaw is a claim-boundary problem that no benchmark can fix and only a narrower claim can answer. And the one bounded result you will defend, with its uncertainty stated. A reviewer proposes; you verify, and the evidence decides.

title: 23. AI as Adversarial Reviewer
text:
An AI failure case
You paste your benchmark summary into an AI reviewer, and it answers with total certainty: your speedup is an artifact of one warm-up run, drop that run and it vanishes. The claim is specific, mechanistic, and stated without a hedge. It reads exactly like a reviewer who has seen this mistake a hundred times.
Here is how you catch it. You do not act on the verdict. You run the leave-one-out yourself, dropping the warm-up run and recomputing the p95. The improvement barely moves. The confident flaw was fabricated, a real-sounding mechanism bolted onto a problem your data do not have. Trust the certainty and you throw away a genuine result on a hunch. Pointing the check at your own numbers is the only thing that settled it.

title: 23. AI as Adversarial Reviewer
text:
Verification laboratory
The chapter’s key result is that confidence never certifies truth: a winning number can be manufactured by searching, and a confident flag can be pure invention. Confirm it yourself with simulation (from the verification guide). Benchmark an unchanged build against an identical copy of itself, so the true difference is zero by construction, then slice the runs by input size and let a searcher report the best-looking slice. Watch a “speedup” appear from nothing. For the specific number, add alternative code: recompute the same p95 a second way, sorting the raw samples and reading the 95th-percentile index directly, and confirm the two roads give one answer.

title: 23. AI as Adversarial Reviewer
text:
Project transfer
This chapter feeds milestone M9, your poster draft and research audit, presented at Friday’s studio. A research audit is the graded record of how hard you tried to break your own claim and what survived. Three moves carry over. Pre-list the three robustness or placebo checks you commit to for your own estimate, each tied to the reviewer attack it answers. Build a verified AI-review trail: every flaw a human or a model panel raised, marked confirmed or refuted by a data check, plus the most confident wrong flag you caught. Then state the one limitation no check could fix. Write it so a reader who has never seen your project could tell how far you pushed on your own number.

title: 23. AI as Adversarial Reviewer
text:
Defend your decision
In a short paragraph, state the one bounded claim your analysis supports after you attacked it. Name the check you pre-listed, the flag you verified against the data, and whether you accepted, changed, or rejected what a reviewer returned. Then name its boundary and your uncertainty. Which reviewer flag did the data refute, and where is your claim a boundary problem that no further check can rescue? A result you have not tried to break, and a flaw you accepted without a check, are both undefended.

title: 25. From Results to Claims
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 25. From Results to Claims
text:
Why this decision matters

“At the health-sciences poster hall I point at one number and ask where it came from and what it lets you say. If the headline reads ‘lowers blood pressure’ but the study only watched who chose to walk, I stop reading. A claim that outruns its evidence tells me to distrust the whole board.” — a research-integrity judge working a poster session

A result is not yet a claim. Your analysis prints a number; your poster asserts a sentence. The gap between them is where most posters break, because the sentence almost always wants to say more than the number earned. The judge above is not testing your statistics. She is testing whether the words on your board match the evidence underneath them. This chapter is the habit that closes that gap before a stranger finds it open.

title: 25. From Results to Claims
text:
The concept
Start with the two words this chapter lives between. A result is a number your analysis produced, such as a difference of 6 points between two group averages. A claim is a sentence you assert and will defend, such as “the walking group had lower blood pressure.” Turning the first into the second is a decision, not a formatting step, because you choose how much the sentence is allowed to say.
Three ideas govern that choice.
Your compass position is the kind and reach of the question your project answered: did you describe the cases you observed, or establish that one thing caused another? Example: if you watched who already chose to walk, you ran a descriptive comparison, not a causal test. Your claim boundary is the line between what your evidence licenses and what it does not, and it lives in the headline verb. Example: an observational project may write “was associated with lower,” never “lowered.” The verb is where a claim quietly crosses the line.
The last idea is where the claim’s honesty becomes visible. Uncertainty in the same eye-span means the interval or caveat sits right beside the claim, not in a footnote. Example: “about 6 points lower (95% interval: 1 to 11)” printed under the headline, so a reader meets the doubt at the same moment as the number. A bound a reader has to hunt for is a bound doing hidden work.
One more habit backs all of this. Traceability means every number on the poster walks back to the exact notebook cell that produced it. Example: the 6-point gap on your board points to the one line of code that computed it, and you can show that line when a judge asks. A number you cannot trace cannot go up.

title: 25. From Results to Claims
text:
A worked example
An honors student in nursing runs a small community pilot. Forty volunteers wear a step tracker for a month. Twenty walk at least 8,000 steps a day; twenty do not. Their average resting systolic blood pressure comes out at 128 in the active group and 134 in the less-active group, a 6-point gap.
The result is that 6-point gap. The claim is the decision. The draft headline reads “Walking LOWERS blood pressure,” drawn as two bars on an axis that starts at 125, which makes the small gap look like a cliff, with the two groups told apart by red and green alone.
Walk the decision. The compass position is descriptive: nobody was assigned to walk, so the active group may differ in age, diet, or baseline health. That means the verb “lowers” claims a crossing the design never paid for, and the honest verb is “was associated with lower.” The truncated axis has to drop to the full scale, so the 6-point gap reads as the modest difference it is. The 95% interval runs roughly 1 to 11 points, wide enough that the honest headline must carry it in the same eye-span. And each bar gets a direct value label, so a color-blind or screen-reader reader receives the number, not just the hue.
The locked claim: “In this sample, more-active volunteers averaged about 6 points lower resting systolic blood pressure (95% interval: 1 to 11); this is an observed association, not evidence that walking caused the drop.” Same result, a claim that survives the judge.

title: 25. From Results to Claims
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb10 — Poster criticism + final poster lock. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. There you run a gallery-walk audit on one headline figure drawn two ways from the same numbers, rehearse a three-lens oral defense, and lock every number to its cell and every citation to a real source before the poster becomes final.

title: 25. From Results to Claims
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt is a checkable job, never a request for a verdict.
Locate the real source behind a health claim.
Act as a literature assistant in public health. My poster claims regular walking
is linked to lower blood pressure. Name up to three peer-reviewed sources or
clinical guidelines that support this, with title, authors, year, and venue.
Only list work you are confident exists; mark anything uncertain.
After running, verify: open each source in a library database and confirm it exists and says what you claim. Counters confident fabrication (an invented citation arrives as confidently as a real one).
Turn your result into a checklist of hard questions.
Here is my headline claim: "[paste it]" and my compass position: "descriptive,
observational." Generate the eight hardest questions a skeptic would ask, and mark
which single audit each one belongs to: claim boundary, figure honesty,
uncertainty, or accessibility.
After running, verify: map every question to an audit and check it against your printed numbers; a missing audit is a gap the tidy list hid. Counters illusion of completeness (a long, organized list that skips the one flaw that matters most).
Red-team the verb.
Here is my written headline: "[paste it]." Act as a hostile reviewer and find every
place the verb claims more than an observational design licenses. Do not rewrite it
for me; list the exact words so I can fix them myself.
After running, verify: read each flagged word against your compass position and keep only the objections your design actually forces. Counters silent scope change (a claim quietly upgraded from association to cause).






ImportantDo not delegate



Three calls stay yours. You choose the verb in your headline, the one word that fixes your claim boundary. You make the defend-or-concede call when a question pushes on that boundary out loud. And you make the lock decision: the judgment that every number is traced, every citation resolves, and the poster is ready to be final. A tool can draft questions and hunt sources, but it cannot decide what your evidence licenses or when your claim is earned.

title: 25. From Results to Claims
text:
An AI failure case
You paste your honest headline into a tool and ask it to make it “punchier for a poster.” It returns, with total confidence, “Walking Reduces Blood Pressure by 6 Points.” It reads beautifully and it is wrong. The tool upgraded your verb from an association to a cause and dropped your interval, a silent scope change dressed up as a copy edit.
You catch it by comparing the new verb to your compass position. Your study never assigned anyone to walk, so “reduces” claims a crossing you did not pay for. You also notice the “6 points” now stands alone with no interval beside it. You revert to “was associated with about 6 points lower (95% interval: 1 to 11).” The fluent rewrite reviewed your poster’s style; only you can review its claim.

title: 25. From Results to Claims
text:
Verification laboratory
Verify this chapter’s key result, the number behind the claim, with alternative code from the verification guide: recompute the group means and the gap a second way and confirm you get the same 6 points. Filter each group by hand and subtract, rather than trusting a single grouped call, then recompute the interval and check it still runs about 1 to 11. Two roads to one answer verifies the number. Then verify the verb with a causal diagram: sketch what could cause both walking and lower blood pressure, such as age or baseline fitness, and if that common cause is open, the honest verb stays “associated with,” not “lowered.”

title: 25. From Results to Claims
text:
Project transfer
This chapter is the engine of your M10 final poster lock. Take the one headline sentence your project’s results produced and run it through the decision here: fix the verb to your compass position, print the interval in the same eye-span, and trace every number on the board back to its cell. Cut any citation you cannot open yourself, and write the one-line AI-use disclosure that records what a tool did and how you checked it. The claim that survives this pass is the one you carry into M11 and say out loud at the Expo.

title: 25. From Results to Claims
text:
Defend your decision
In four sentences, defend your locked headline. State the claim exactly as it will appear on the poster, and name the compass position whose boundary its verb respects. Give the uncertainty that rides beside it, the interval or caveat a reader meets at the same moment as the number. Close with the one crossing your design did not license, the sentence a stranger might try to push you into that you will decline to say.

title: 27. Research Posters
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 27. Research Posters
text:
Why this decision matters

“From four feet away I read one thing: does your tallest bar mean what it looks like it means? If your axis starts halfway up the scale, I have caught you before I read a word of your methods, and now I distrust the whole board.” — a skeptical peer walking a poster session

At a poster session a stranger gives you ninety seconds and one glance. The figure does most of the talking, and a figure can lie with true numbers. An axis that starts just below your data turns a rounding-error gap into a landslide. A reader who cannot tell your red bar from your green one receives no message at all. This chapter is the decision that keeps your strongest visual honest before it ever reaches the wall.

title: 27. Research Posters
text:
The concept
The decision is one judgment made four ways, and each way has a name.

Headline claim. The one sentence you want a reader to leave with. Its verb carries the whole meaning. Example: “Catalyst B raised our yield” promises far more than “Catalyst B yielded slightly higher.”
Claim boundary. The line between what your evidence licenses and what it does not. Example: an observational comparison may say “is associated with,” never “causes.”
Compass position. The kind and reach of the question your project answered, which fixes what the headline is allowed to say. Example: a descriptive question about the batches you measured, not a causal claim about why.
Figure honesty. The rule that a figure’s picture cannot say more than its data. Example: if the real gap is three points, the bars should look about three points apart, not twice as tall.
Truncated axis. An axis that starts above the bottom of its scale instead of at the floor. Example: a yield axis running 70 to 76 instead of 0 to 100 magnifies a small difference into a cliff.
Uncertainty on the page. The interval or caveat printed in the same glance as the claim, not buried in a footnote. Example: a “±4 points” error bar drawn right on the headline figure.
Accessibility. Whether your message reaches readers who perceive differently. Example: a red-green bar pair carries nothing for a red-green color-blind reader, roughly 1 in 12 men.
Redundant encoding. Carrying the same distinction in more than one channel, so no single channel is load-bearing. Example: label each bar with its value and give each a pattern, so color becomes a bonus, not the only signal.

A poster that fails any one of these overclaims, even when its numbers are exactly right.

title: 27. Research Posters
text:
A worked example
You are presenting an organic-chemistry project comparing two catalysts for the same reaction. Across replicate runs, Catalyst A gives a mean percent yield of 71 and Catalyst B a mean of 74. The 95% interval on each mean is about ±4 percentage points, so the two intervals overlap. Your first draft draws a bar chart with the y-axis running 70 to 76, titles it “Catalyst B BOOSTS yield,” and colors the bars red and green.
Now run the decision. Figure honesty: the real gap is 3 points, but on an axis floored at 70 the taller bar is drawn four times the height of the shorter one, so the picture claims a landslide the data never delivered. Uncertainty on the page: the intervals overlap, so a difference the figure sells as decisive might be noise, and nothing on the board shows it. Claim boundary: “BOOSTS” is a decisive, causal verb for a three-point, overlapping gap. Accessibility: told apart by red and green alone, the bars carry no information for a color-blind reader, and a screen reader reads no value off the image.
The fixed figure keeps every number and changes only the honesty. You run the axis 0 to 100, print each mean as a label on its bar, draw the ±4-point interval on top, separate the bars with a color-blind-safe pair plus a hatch pattern, and soften the headline to “Catalyst B yielded about 3 points higher, within overlapping intervals.” Same data, a picture that now tells the truth.

title: 27. Research Posters
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb10 — Poster criticism + final poster lock. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. There you draw one headline figure two ways from the same numbers, watch a truncated axis inflate a small gap several times over, and run the four audits plus accessibility until each one names a located, fixable hit on the board.

title: 27. Research Posters
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own audit first, then delegate. Each prompt below is a checkable job, not a request for a verdict.
Locate the color-only channels.
Here is the code that draws my poster's headline figure: [paste the cell].
List every place this figure encodes a meaningful distinction using color.
For each, tell me exactly what a red-green color-blind reader and a
screen-reader user would fail to receive. Do not rewrite the figure.
After running, verify: render your figure and check each named channel against the picture you can see. Counters plausible-but-wrong-method (generic accessibility advice that does not match how your figure is actually drawn).
List the skeptic’s questions, then map them to audits.
Act as a hostile poster reviewer. My headline figure is a bar chart of mean
percent yield for two catalysts, y-axis 70 to 76, red versus green bars, no
interval shown, titled "Catalyst B BOOSTS yield." Generate the six hardest
questions you would ask, then name the one this figure cannot answer at all.
After running, verify: map each question to one audit (claim boundary, figure honesty, uncertainty, accessibility); an audit with no question is the gap, and the truncated axis had better appear. Counters illusion of completeness (a long, tidy list that quietly skips the flaw that matters most).
Red-team the headline verb.
Here is my headline: "[paste it]" and my compass position: "[descriptive /
associational / causal]". Act as a skeptical reviewer and find every word that
claims more than that position licenses. List the exact words. Do not soften them.
After running, verify: if it praises the headline or finds nothing, push back and demand the single worst word, then check each flagged word against your interval. Counters sycophantic agreement (praise that reviews your nerve, not your evidence).






ImportantDo not delegate



The words of your headline and the verb that carries its boundary are yours. So is the judgment that the figure’s impression matches its number, the call that the uncertainty is visible in the same glance, and the decision that the poster is honest enough to lock. AI can generate the skeptic’s questions and scan your code for color-only channels. It cannot see your board, and it cannot decide the claim was earned. Those stay with you.

title: 27. Research Posters
text:
An AI failure case
You paste the described figure and ask Gemini for the hardest reviewer questions. It returns a confident, well-organized list of six: sample size, replicate count, generalization to other substrates, temperature control, and two more. Every question is reasonable, and not one mentions the truncated axis, the single worst flaw on the board. The list looks complete, so it is tempting to trust it and move on.
You catch it by mapping each returned question to an audit. Claim boundary, sampling, and methods all draw questions; figure honesty draws none. That empty row is the finding. You check the number your own cell printed, the exaggeration the floored axis creates, and you add the figure-honesty hit the tidy list skipped. Fluency is not completeness. The check lives in your own audit list, the one you wrote before you asked.

title: 27. Research Posters
text:
Verification laboratory
