Verify the chapter’s key result, that the truncated axis inflates the gap, with direct calculation from the verification guide: recompute the numbers by hand and compare them to the picture. The honest ratio of the two bars is 74 to 71, about 1.04, a four-percent difference. On an axis floored at 70, the drawn heights are (74−70) to (71−70), which is 4 to 1. The figure makes Catalyst B’s bar look four times Catalyst A’s when the yields really differ by four percent, and the axis alone is doing that work. For a second road, redraw the same two means on a 0-to-100 axis (alternative code) and confirm the bars now look about as close as the numbers are.

title: 27. Research Posters
text:
Project transfer
This chapter turns into the headline figure of your poster milestone. Your M9 poster draft carries a first version of that figure. Run the four audits plus accessibility on it now, log each located hit, and rebuild the figure honestly before the M10 final poster lock, where a number the picture overstates becomes a permanent, public claim you cannot patch after printing. The one figure you fix here is the one a stranger cannot break at the expo.

title: 27. Research Posters
text:
Defend your decision
In four sentences, defend your headline figure. State the bounded claim it makes and the verb that keeps it inside your compass position. Name what the figure does not establish: the overlap in the intervals, the difference that might be noise, the reach your design never bought. Say how the uncertainty rides in the same glance as the claim, and name the one reader your design still works to reach. Close with the single check that decided the figure was honest enough to lock: the number you recomputed, or the audit you could not make fail.

title: 29. Research Pitches
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 29. Research Pitches
text:
Why this decision matters

“I stop at maybe forty posters in an afternoon. What I remember is not the one with the most data. It is the one whose presenter told me, in one clean breath, exactly what they found and exactly where it stops. The ones who oversold lost me on the second sentence.” — a skeptical peer working the room at a research expo

A finished poster is silent. The moment a visitor stops, your voice takes over, and speaking is faster than reading, so you compress on the fly. That is where claims quietly grow. Under a little pressure, “was associated with” slides into “causes,” and “on data it never saw” drops out. The peer above is not testing your data. She is testing whether the sentence in your mouth still matches the sentence on your board. This chapter gives you a pitch you can say cold, under pressure, that never widens the claim your evidence earned.

title: 29. Research Pitches
text:
The concept
A pitch is a short, prepared spoken version of your project, built to fit a fixed slice of time. Example: the thirty seconds you give a visitor who pauses, and the guided tour you give the one who stays, are two pitches of different lengths.
Every pitch shares one skeleton, the pitch architecture: the fixed set of beats each version moves through, which is hook, claim, evidence, boundary, invitation.

Hook: one sentence a stranger outside your field cares about. Example: “a help desk drowns in tickets every morning and cannot tell which will drag on.”
Claim: your headline, said plainly and inside its limits.
Evidence: the finding that earns the claim, one spoken sentence at a time.
Boundary sentence: one sentence naming what the claim does not show. This is the beat pressure will try to drop, so you rehearse it until it is reflex.
Invitation: a reason to look closer.

The three layers are that one skeleton at three lengths: a 30-second hook (hook, claim, boundary, invitation, with no evidence yet), a 90-second walk (the hook plus two or three evidence beats), and a full pitch of three to five minutes. Deciding what goes in each length is the choice this chapter equips.
One rule governs all three. Compression is a shorter version of a claim that keeps its boundary. Example: “on data the model had never seen, it flagged four of every five slow tickets” compresses to “on new tickets, it caught most of the slow ones.” Inflation is a version that quietly makes the claim bigger. Example: the same finding becomes “my model predicts which tickets will be slow.” One lost a detail; the other lost the truth. The failure to hunt is the excited-mouth upgrade: the moment your spoken verb outruns your written one and the boundary moves while nobody watches the words. Example: you wrote “is associated with” and your mouth says “causes.”

title: 29. Research Pitches
text:
A worked example
You built a small machine-learning model for a campus help desk. A held-out set is a slice of your data the model never learned from, kept aside so its score is honest. Example: you trained on 4,000 past tickets and tested on 1,000 the model had never seen. On that held-out set, when the model flagged a ticket “slow,” it was right about 78% of the time. That right-when-it-flags rate has a name: precision is the share of a model’s flags that turn out correct. Your poster headline reads: “On held-out tickets, the model flagged slow ones with 78% precision.”
Now you build the pitch on the architecture.

Hook: “A help desk drowns in tickets every morning and cannot tell which ones will drag on for days.”
Claim, compressed for speech: “On tickets it had never seen, my model flagged the slow ones, and when it raised a flag it was right about three times in four.”
Boundary sentence, the beat you rehearse to reflex: “That number is precision on a held-out slice, not overall accuracy, and the model still misses some slow tickets.”
Invitation: “The surprising part is which tickets fooled it. Want the walk?”

Watch the two ways this goes wrong when you speak it. Drop the words “held-out” and you have claimed the model works on any ticket, which your test never showed. Swap “precision” for “accuracy” and you have quoted a number your poster does not report. Both are the excited-mouth upgrade, and both feel natural because the shorter sentence sounds cleaner. The fix is mechanical: mark the boundary sentence and say it at every length, even the 30-second one.

title: 29. Research Pitches
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb11 — Poster delivery: pitches, uncertainty, and difficult questions. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. There you run a pacing calculator that turns a script’s word count into seconds of speech, so you can size each layer honestly, then draft and stress-test your own three pitch layers against the one rule.

title: 29. Research Pitches
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own version first, then delegate. Each prompt is a checkable job, not a request for a verdict.
Locate the jargon and translate it.
Here is one sentence from my poster: "[paste it]". My headline claim and its
boundary are: "[paste them]". List every word a curious stranger with no training
in my field could not decode. Then give two plain rewrites a first-year in any
major would follow, each keeping my claim exactly as narrow as it is now.
After running, verify: read each rewrite next to your boundary and reject any that dropped a qualifier like “held-out” or turned “flagged” into “predicts.” Counters silent scope change (a “plain” rewrite that quietly widens the claim while it simplifies).
List the words that inflate my claim.
Here is my 30-second hook: "[paste it]". Here is my poster's boundary sentence:
"[paste it]". Play a hurried visitor. List every word in the hook that says
something bigger than the boundary allows. Rewrite nothing; just name the words.
After running, verify: if it flags nothing and calls the hook “strong,” push back and demand the single worst offender. Counters sycophantic agreement (praise reviews your ego, not your words).
Red-team the pitch as a hurried stranger.
Play a skeptical conference visitor with thirty seconds of patience, from outside
my field. Here is my full pitch: "[paste it]". Name the exact sentence where you
lose interest or stop believing me, and the single place I promise more than I show.
After running, verify: check its answer against a real practice run with a friend, and ask it “what is the one thing you left out?” Counters illusion of completeness (a polished “it’s clear” that skips the moment a stranger actually drifts).






ImportantDo not delegate



Three calls stay yours. You decide which claim your evidence actually supports, where the claim must stop (the exact boundary sentence), and whether a compressed sentence still says what the poster says. A tool can trim words and flag jargon. It cannot decide the shorter sentence is still true, and it will not be standing at the board when the stranger asks. You say the sentence. You own it.

title: 29. Research Pitches
text:
An AI failure case
You paste your 90-second walk and ask a tool to compress it to a 30-second hook. Back comes a crisp, confident line: “My model predicts slow help-desk tickets 78% of the time.” It reads beautifully, and it is wrong twice. It dropped “held-out,” so it now claims the model works on any ticket, and it turned precision into a general “78% of the time,” a number your poster never reports. The sentence is shorter and smoother than yours, which is exactly the trap.
You catch it by putting the compressed line next to your poster’s boundary sentence and reading them word for word. “Predicts … 78% of the time” does not match “precision on a held-out slice.” Then you say the line to a friend, who asks, “so it’s right 78% of the time on any ticket?” That question is the widened claim coming back at you. You keep your own boundary sentence and drop the tool’s version.

title: 29. Research Pitches
text:
Verification laboratory
Verify this chapter’s key result, that your compressed pitch still says what your poster says, with peer reasoning from the verification guide: say your 30-second hook once to a partner from outside your field, then have them repeat back, in their own words, your claim and its boundary. If what they repeat is bigger than your poster allows, the compression inflated, and the words they added tell you where. Add a second, independent check from a different family: run your script through the pacing cell in nb11 (direct calculation on the word count) to confirm it fits the clock without rushing. One check guards the claim, the other guards the clock.

title: 29. Research Pitches
text:
Project transfer
This chapter builds the first component of milestone M11, your presentation package: the three pitch layers plus the poster-walk map that says what your hand points at, beat by beat. Draft all three for the poster you already locked, mark the boundary sentence in each, and deliver the 30-second hook twice to a partner who flags every spoken upgrade. Those flags are your revision list. You drill the whole package Friday at the mock symposium, a full rehearsal in a safe room, and M11 is due that night.

title: 29. Research Pitches
text:
Defend your decision
In four sentences, defend one pitch layer you built. Name the layer and the single boundary sentence you refuse to drop at any length. State the claim you can make aloud, compressed for speech, and show that it still sits inside your poster’s boundary. Close with your uncertainty: the one word your excited mouth is most likely to upgrade under pressure, and the check you will run to catch it.

title: 31. AI Disclosure and Research Integrity
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 31. AI Disclosure and Research Integrity
text:
Why this decision matters

“I am not going to penalize you for using an AI tool. I am going to penalize you for not being able to tell me where you used it and how you checked it. A result I cannot trace back to a source and a verification is a result I cannot trust.” — a conference reviewer, standing at your poster and asking the AI-use question

At the Expo, one of the five kinds of question a stranger can ask you is the AI-use question. Many journals and conferences now ask authors to state where and how AI tools were used, so this is not a classroom formality. If your only answer is “yes, a bit, and it seemed fine,” you have turned good work into an untrustable result on the spot. The disclosure is what keeps an honest use of AI honest under questioning.

title: 31. AI Disclosure and Research Integrity
text:
The concept
AI disclosure is a truthful statement of where and how you used an AI tool in your work. Example: “I used Gemini to draft the code that computed my safety factor, and I recomputed that number by hand before reporting it.” Research integrity is the practice of keeping your work honest and traceable, so a reader can see where each result came from and trust it. Example: a claim that names its source and its check has integrity; a claim that names neither does not.
The tool that makes disclosure easy is the one you have kept all semester: the AI Research Ledger, a running table with one row per AI use, recording the task, the tool, your decision, and the verification method. A complete disclosure is not written from scratch at the end. It is read straight off your ledger.
Three parts make a disclosure complete, and each maps to a ledger column: which tool you used, for which task, and how you verified the output. Drop any one and it fails. “I used AI” with no task is meaningless; a task with no verification is exactly the gap a reviewer is trained to find.

title: 31. AI Disclosure and Research Integrity
text:
A worked example
You are presenting a senior-design poster in mechanical engineering. Your bracket must hold a static design load, and your headline number is its safety factor, the ratio of the material’s strength to the stress your load actually puts on the part. A safety factor of 2.0 means the part can take twice the load before it yields.
You used an AI tool twice: once to find the yield strength of 6061-T6 aluminum, once to write the short calculation that turns that strength and your load into a safety factor. Both are honest uses, and neither is safe until it is disclosed and verified.
Your ledger already holds two rows. For the material property, the verification method is primary-source reading: you opened the alloy’s published datasheet and confirmed the yield strength the tool gave you. For the calculation, the method is alternative code, recomputing the same number a second way:
# The number the AI code reported, recomputed by hand.
yield_strength = 276      # MPa, confirmed against the 6061-T6 datasheet
applied_stress = 131      # MPa, from my load and cross-section
safety_factor  = yield_strength / applied_stress
print(round(safety_factor, 2))   # 2.11 — matches the AI-generated cell
When the reviewer asks the AI-use question, you do not improvise. You read your two rows aloud: the tools, the tasks, and the two checks. The claim you defend is bounded. The safety factor is about 2.1 under this static load, with the strength confirmed against the datasheet and the arithmetic reproduced by hand. Its boundary is honest too: you modeled a single idealized load case, not fatigue and not stress concentration at the holes.

title: 31. AI Disclosure and Research Integrity
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb12 — Final conference preparation, presentation + reflection. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. There you code the AI-use questions you were asked as data and fill the AI Research Ledger that becomes the disclosure you defend. The final Exit Defense asks you to name what you delegated to Gemini and the specific check that decided whether you accepted, changed, or rejected it.

title: 31. AI Disclosure and Research Integrity
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt is a checkable job, not a request for a verdict.
Locate the standard.
Act as a research-integrity assistant. Name the elements a complete AI-use
disclosure should contain for a student research poster, and cite one real,
retrievable source such as a university research-integrity office or a major
publisher's AI policy. Only name a source you are confident exists.
After running, verify: open the cited policy and confirm each named element is actually in it. Counters confident fabrication (an invented policy element arrives as confidently as a real one).
Turn notes into a disclosure table to verify.
Here are my raw notes on every time I used an AI tool on this project: [paste].
Build a table with columns tool, task, and verification method, one row per use.
Do not invent a verification I did not mention. If a use has no check, leave that
cell blank and flag the row.
After running, verify: match each row against your ledger; a verification filled in where your notes had none is fabricated, so cut it. Counters illusion of completeness (a tidy table that quietly reports checks you never ran).
Red-team your disclosure.
Here is my drafted AI-use disclosure: [paste]. Act as a hostile reviewer who
suspects I am hiding a use or overstating my checks. Name every place I claim a
verification I have not shown, or omit a use a reviewer would expect. Do not
rewrite it for me.
After running, verify: for each flagged line, produce the ledger row that backs it or delete the claim. Counters sycophantic agreement (a reviewer that praises your honesty instead of testing it).






ImportantDo not delegate



The honesty of the disclosure is yours alone. You decide which uses to disclose (including the one you would rather not mention), whether a verification you claim actually happened, and whether the final statement is true. A tool can format your ledger into a clean paragraph. It cannot know what you really did or certify that you checked it. Signing your name to a disclosure you cannot back is the integrity failure this chapter exists to prevent.

title: 31. AI Disclosure and Research Integrity
text:
An AI failure case
You ask a tool to “write my AI-use disclosure from these notes,” and it hands back a polished paragraph: “All AI-generated outputs were independently verified against primary sources.” It reads beautifully, and it is false. You verified the yield strength against the datasheet, but you never independently checked the load figure the same tool helped you estimate. The sentence upgraded one real check into a blanket claim of “all.”
You catch it by reading the disclosure line by line against your ledger. Every sentence claiming a verification must point to a row that names that check. The word “all” has no such row, so it is fabricated and comes out. Your true disclosure is narrower and stronger: you name the two checks you ran and the one input you did not independently confirm. A confident paragraph is not a truthful one. You verify the disclosure against the record, not against how responsible it sounds.

title: 31. AI Disclosure and Research Integrity
text:
Verification laboratory
Verify this chapter’s key result, your finished disclosure, with peer reasoning from the verification guide. Hand a partner your disclosure and your actual project, and ask one question: can you find an AI use I did not disclose, or a verification I claimed but cannot show? If they find one, your disclosure was incomplete, which is a finding, not a failure. For the engineering number itself, recompute the safety factor a second way, so the value you disclose survives two independent roads to the same answer.

title: 31. AI Disclosure and Research Integrity
text:
Project transfer
This chapter turns your AI Research Ledger into the disclosure you submit and defend with M12, your conference reflection and criticism portfolio. The ledger is a required part of that package: a missing ledger scores Craft 0 and the submission is returned unread. So read your finished ledger into a short, truthful AI-use statement, attach it to M12, and rehearse answering the Expo’s AI-use question straight from it. When a stranger asks how you checked your work, your answer is already written and already verified.

title: 31. AI Disclosure and Research Integrity
text:
Defend your decision
In four sentences, defend your disclosure. Name one AI use on your project and the exact verification you ran on it. State the bounded claim your disclosure lets you make, with its boundary: what your check does not establish. Close with your uncertainty, the one AI-assisted input you have not yet independently confirmed and the check you would run next to close the gap.

title: 33. From Poster to Research Note
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 33. From Poster to Research Note
text:
Why this decision matters

“Show me your results in the order you trust them. If your flashiest number comes from one lucky run and it leads the paper, I already know how this review ends. I do not want your most surprising result first. I want your most defensible one.” — a conference reviewer, on the first page of a systems paper they actually weigh

On the poster you stood next to your work and filled the silences with your voice. The note has no you standing next to it. Every leap the poster let you imply, the prose now has to earn, in order, for a reader who will never hear you explain it. The reviewer above is not impressed by a big number. She is asking which finding you would still defend after the room empties, and whether she could rerun your work and watch the same number return. This chapter is how you answer both.

title: 33. From Poster to Research Note
text:
The concept
A poster and a note make the same argument, but they carry it differently. Poster logic is communication built for a reader standing next to you, where a few boxes and your live voice share the work. Example: a Gap box that reads only “few benchmarks on cold-start latency” while you say the rest out loud. Paper logic is communication that must stand alone on the page, where every claim is argued in prose because you are not there to answer. Example: that same gap written as a paragraph naming what prior benchmarks covered, what they skipped, and why the missing piece matters. Turning one into the other is not adding words. It is making the reasoning visible.
A note carries that reasoning on a backbone. The problem–gap–question spine is three linked pieces: the real trouble your work responds to, the specific thing prior retrievable work has not established, and the answerable question that closes exactly that gap. Example: services stall on the first request (problem); published latency studies time only warmed-up requests (gap); how much does an in-memory cache cut first-request time on this workload (question). The spine holds only when the gap is real, and a gap is real only when you show what is already known with evidence density, the share of a paragraph resting on sources a reader could open rather than bare assertion. Example: “as everyone knows” carries no density; a sentence resting on a benchmark a reader can rerun carries some.
The back half of the note needs one more term. A results hierarchy is the order you present findings in, best-supported first and fragile last. Example: a median measured over a thousand runs leads; a single fast request waits at the back, labeled exploratory. Your limitations follow the same discipline, stated in proportion to what actually threatens the claim: “measured on a synthetic load, not production traffic,” not a generic “more research is needed.”
Finally, a note a reader cannot rerun is half a claim. The other half is a reproducibility capsule, everything a stranger needs to rebuild your number and nothing they must guess: a notebook that passes restart-and-run-all (clear everything, run every cell top to bottom, and the headline number returns), pinned data and tool versions, a fixed seed, a decision log, and your AI-use ledger.

title: 33. From Poster to Research Note
text:
A worked example
You benchmarked an in-memory cache for a small web service. Your poster had a box: “38% faster.” The note has to turn that box into a claim you can defend.
Rank the findings. Across a thousand requests from a seeded load generator, the median latency dropped about 38%, with a tight interval around it. You also noticed one cached request that returned almost instantly, a 95% drop. The surprising number is the single request; the defensible one is the median over a thousand runs. So the median leads, and the lone request goes to the back, labeled as one observation, not a result.
Size the claim. The honest headline reads: “on this synthetic workload, adding the cache cut median first-request latency by about 38% (interval attached); this is a measurement on generated load, not a guarantee for production traffic.” The inflated version you refuse: “the cache makes the service 38% faster.” That sentence drops the workload, drops the word median, and promises every user a speedup your bench never tested.
Package it. Your capsule pins the load generator’s seed, records the machine and library versions, logs that you excluded the warmup request and why, and passes restart-and-run-all so the 38% returns on a clean kernel. The note is now readable and runnable.

title: 33. From Poster to Research Note
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb14 — From poster to research note. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. There you score the evidence density of two paragraphs, build a results hierarchy by watching a tight interval earn the top while a forty-row interval waits at the back, and run an auditor that hunts the five ways a reproducibility capsule breaks.

title: 33. From Poster to Research Note
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt is a checkable job, not a request for a verdict.
Locate real sources for the gap.
Act as a literature assistant in systems performance. List peer-reviewed or
official sources that benchmark cache or cold-start latency, in a table with
title, authors, venue, year, and one-line finding. Only include work you are
confident exists; mark anything uncertain. Do not write my gap paragraph.
After running, verify: retrieve each source yourself before it enters the note; a source you cannot open in seconds is fabricated until proven otherwise. Counters confident fabrication (an invented citation arrives as confidently as a real one).
List every over-reach to verify.
Here is my discussion paragraph and the cell that printed my median latency drop
with its interval: [paste both]. List every sentence that claims more than the
printout supports: a bigger number, a wider workload, or "faster for everyone"
where I measured one synthetic load. List them; do not rewrite them.
After running, verify: check each flagged sentence against your printout and keep only edits that shrink a claim to fit the evidence. Counters silent scope change (a sharper sentence that upgrades “on this workload” to “for everyone” has quietly answered a different question).
Red-team the capsule.
Here are the key lines of my reproducibility capsule: [paste them]. Playing a cold
replicator who has only these lines, list every input, version, or by-hand step you
would need to rerun my number and might not find here. Then name one problem this
line-scan can never catch that only a person running it cold would hit.
After running, verify: match each gap against what you can actually see missing, and confirm the “cannot catch” problem is real by rereading your own lines. Counters illusion of completeness (a capsule can look complete and still omit the one input a stranger needs).






ImportantDo not delegate



Three calls stay yours no matter how fluent the tool is. Which finding is your headline, how big that claim is allowed to be, and which sources are real are never delegated. An AI can sharpen a sentence, but a sharper sentence is not a bigger finding, and clean prose is exactly how an overclaim sneaks in. You own the words you put your name on, their boundary, and their uncertainty.

title: 33. From Poster to Research Note
text:
An AI failure case
You paste your discussion into Gemini and ask it to make the writing sharper. It hands back cleaner prose, and it runs through your spell-check without a flag. Buried in the polish, one sentence has changed size. Your original said “median first-request latency dropped about 38% on this synthetic workload.” The rewrite says “the cache makes the service roughly 38% faster.” The word median is gone, the workload boundary is gone, and a measurement on generated load now promises every user a speedup. The sentence reads better and claims more.
You catch it by diffing the rewrite against your original sentence and against your printed benchmark. The number came from a median over a seeded load generator, not a mean, and not production traffic, so the general claim is not licensed. You keep the edits that clarify and reject the one that inflated. Fluent prose is not evidence. You verify the claim, not the polish.

title: 33. From Poster to Research Note
text:
Verification laboratory
Verify this chapter’s key result, the headline speedup, with alternative code from the verification guide: recompute the median latency drop a second way, from the raw timings rather than the summarized table, and confirm the number and its interval match. Then verify the capsule with peer reasoning: hand it to a partner who reruns it cold with restart-and-run-all. If your number comes back on their machine, the capsule is runnable. If it does not, you measured your own memory, not a reproducible result.

title: 33. From Poster to Research Note
text:
Project transfer
This chapter becomes your M14 research note and reproducibility capsule. Draft the problem–gap–question spine in prose with every claim sourced, order your findings into a results hierarchy with the defensible one leading, size your limitations to your evidence, and assemble the capsule so restart-and-run-all returns your headline. A partner table-reads the note and reruns the capsule at Friday’s studio, where M14 is due and M15, your final research chapter, begins. The note makes your work readable; the capsule makes it runnable.

title: 33. From Poster to Research Note
text:
Defend your decision
In four sentences, defend your research note. Name the finding you chose as the headline and why it, not a more surprising result, earned the top. State the bounded claim in one sentence, with its boundary (the workload, sample, or population it does not cover). Then state your uncertainty: your interval or the caveat that most threatens the number, and the one check a partner running your capsule cold would run next to test it.

title: 35. Managing Multiple AI Agents
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 35. Managing Multiple AI Agents
text:
Why this decision matters

“Bring me a chapter, not a pile of AI output. I want to know which part each tool touched, what you told it to do, and how you checked it. If you cannot tell me that, you did not write the chapter.” — your thesis advisor, reading the draft you will defend this week

By now one AI assistant can draft, cite, critique, and polish in a single breath. The temptation is to open four of them at once and feel four times as safe. You are not. Four roles reading the same draft with the same instructions tend to miss the same thing and agree loudly about it. The advisor above does not want a louder chorus. She wants to see that you broke the job into pieces, put those pieces in a sane order, and stayed the one person who decided what the chapter claims. This chapter gives you that management skill.

title: 35. Managing Multiple AI Agents
text:
The concept
The move that turns a crowd of AI roles into a workflow you can defend is task decomposition: breaking one large job into smaller subtasks, each with a single clear owner. Example: instead of asking a tool to “improve my chapter,” you split the work into check the design, check the citations, and tighten the prose. Each piece now has a pass-or-fail you can judge.
Each piece becomes a scoped role: an AI job narrow enough to state in one sentence and check in one pass. Example: “flag every sentence in my results section that claims more than my sample can support.” A scoped role has a clear finish line. “Make it better” does not.
The backbone that keeps roles honest is the worker-critic pair: one role produces something and a second, separate role attacks what the first produced. Example: a worker drafts your limitations paragraph, then a critic hunts for the limitation the worker left out. A worker alone is confident about its own quality, which is exactly the blind spot it cannot see from the inside.
Wiring the roles means deciding what waits on what. A dependency is a required order between two subtasks, where one needs the other’s output before it can start. Example: a critic cannot attack a draft the worker has not written yet. Roles with no dependency between them run at the same time. Roles joined by one run in order.
The chain of dependencies you cannot shorten is the critical path: the longest run of must-follow-must steps in your workflow. Example: worker drafts, then critic attacks, then you integrate is three steps that cannot collapse into fewer. That chain sets the minimum number of rounds your workflow takes. Reading the critical path off your own design, instead of guessing it, is the skill this chapter drills.

title: 35. Managing Multiple AI Agents
text:
A worked example
You are finishing a chapter on a small health study: whether a ten-minute post-lunch walk is associated with a lower afternoon resting heart rate among the residents on your dorm floor. You have a draft and four AI roles waiting.
Split. You break the review into scoped roles. A clarity reviewer checks whether each sentence reads plainly. A methodologist checks whether “associated with” is the honest word for what your data can show. A citation-checker confirms the two heart-rate studies you cite are real and say what you claim. An editor tightens the prose. Each role owns one sentence-long job.
Wire. The worker drafts your limitations paragraph. The three critics can all read that draft at the same moment, because none needs another’s output. That is three roles running in parallel. Then you integrate their notes, and the editor tightens what survives. Trace the arrows: worker to a critic is one, critic to you is two, you to editor is three. Your critical path is three arrows deep, and it runs three critics at its widest.
Keep one step human. The integrate step is yours. When the methodologist says your walk-and-heart-rate result is only an association and the clarity reviewer loved the sentence that called it an effect, no role settles that clash. You do. The decision about what the chapter claims never enters the workflow as a delegated task.
The tools drafted, flagged, and polished. You split the job, set the order, and owned the claim that carries your name.

title: 35. Managing Multiple AI Agents
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb15 — Managing multiple AI agents + the final defense. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. There you build the worker-critic workflow as a directed graph, read its critical-path depth and parallel width straight off the code, and watch the path grow by one round the moment you make a critic wait on another critic. Then you decompose your own chapter into scoped roles and defend the wiring you chose.

title: 35. Managing Multiple AI Agents
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt is a checkable job, not a request for a verdict.
Decompose, then hand back the human step.
I am splitting the review of my health chapter into scoped AI roles. Here are my
section headings and one line on each: [paste them]. Propose a table with one row
per role: the role's one-sentence scoped task, the draft or input it needs, whether
it can run in parallel or must wait on another role, and the single decision it must
hand back to me.
After running, verify: check that every “hand back to me” cell names a real never-delegate decision, not one invented to look cautious. Counters illusion of completeness (a tidy role table that reads thorough while omitting the check that matters).
Confirm the wiring has no early error slipping past.
Here is my proposed workflow as a list of "role A feeds role B" arrows: [paste them].
For each role, tell me exactly what input it depends on, and name any role I have
scheduled to start before the input it needs actually exists.
After running, verify: draw the arrows yourself and trace whether each role’s input exists when it starts. Counters plausible-but-wrong-method (a confident wiring that lets a role run before its input is ready).
Red-team the decomposition.
Act as a hostile reviewer of my AI workflow, not of my chapter. Name every place an
early mistake in one role could flow downstream unchecked, and every role whose job
overlaps another so much that it adds no independent check. Do not rewrite my plan.
After running, verify: if it only praises your plan, push back and demand the single worst wiring flaw. Counters sycophantic agreement (praise that reviews your ego, not your workflow).






ImportantDo not delegate



The split and the order can be informed by a tool, but three calls stay yours alone. You decide which jobs are worth a separate role and which just multiply output you cannot supervise. You decide the order, so that no role settles a question a role upstream of it should have answered first. And you keep the integrate step human: when two roles disagree about what your chapter claims, you make that call in your own words. A workflow that hands the claim to a tool is not a workflow you can defend.

title: 35. Managing Multiple AI Agents
text:
An AI failure case
You ask a tool to design your multi-agent workflow, and it returns a confident, well-formatted plan: run the worker, the methodologist, the skeptic, and the editor all in parallel to “save rounds.” The plan runs in your head without a hitch. Here is the trap. The editor and the skeptic have nothing to read yet, because the worker has not drafted anything. The tool scheduled three roles to start before the input they depend on exists, and dressed it up as efficiency.
You catch it by drawing the arrows and asking one question at each role: does this role’s input exist at the moment it starts? The editor’s input is the integrated draft, which does not exist until the end, so “editor in parallel with worker” is impossible. Redrawn honestly, the plan is a critical path three arrows deep, not one. A green, tidy plan is not a valid one. You check the dependencies, not the confidence.

title: 35. Managing Multiple AI Agents
text:
Verification laboratory
Verify this chapter’s key number, the critical-path depth, with alternative code from the verification guide: recompute the same quantity a different way and compare. The notebook computes the depth with a graph function. You confirm it by hand: write out every role, draw each “feeds” arrow, and trace the longest unbroken chain from start to finish, counting as you go. If your hand count matches the code, the depth is verified. If it does not, the wiring you drew and the wiring the code built are two different workflows, and that disagreement is the finding worth chasing.

title: 35. Managing Multiple AI Agents
text:
Project transfer
This chapter builds the first piece of M15, your final research chapter and AI-management portfolio. Take one real section of your own project, your methods, your results, or your limitations, and write down its decomposition: the scoped roles you will run, which run in parallel and which must wait, and the one node you keep human. Bring that decomposition to Friday’s studio as the opening move of your portfolio. It is the evidence that you ran the AI team rather than letting the team run you.

title: 35. Managing Multiple AI Agents
text:
Defend your decision
In four sentences, defend the workflow you designed for one section of your chapter. Name the scoped roles you chose and the order you wired them in. State the one early error your ordering stops from flowing downstream, and name the step you kept human and why no role can take it. Close with the boundary: one place your wiring could still let a shared mistake through, and the independent check you would run before you trust the roles’ agreement.

title: 37. Final Research and AI-Management Portfolio
text: Open the lab in Colab

title: 37. Final Research and AI-Management Portfolio
text:
Why this decision matters

“By the time you stand up to defend, I do not count your tools. I ask which claims you are willing to be wrong about in public, and whether you can show me you checked them yourself.” — a defense examiner, on the day the portfolio is due

You reach this chapter with a full project behind you and one thing left to do: gather it into something you can defend out loud. A semester of AI-assisted work leaves a trail of drafts, role outputs, and half-finished checks. Left in a pile, none of it is defensible. The examiner will not read the pile. She will ask which claims you own, where each stops, and how you know. This chapter is where you decide those answers on purpose, before the room decides them for you.

title: 37. Final Research and AI-Management Portfolio
text:
The concept
Your final submission has two halves, and this chapter builds both.
A research portfolio is the assembled body of your finished work: your claims, the evidence behind them, and the verification that makes each one defensible. Example: for a lab project it holds your headline result, the measurements behind it, and the check you ran to confirm the number is right.
Alongside it sits an AI-management portfolio, the honest record of how you ran your AI team all semester: which tasks you delegated, where you took the decision back, and which calls you never handed to a tool. Example: it shows a reviewer role drafted your limitations, that you overrode it when it softened a claim you could not support, and that you alone chose the claim boundary.
The decision that closes both is your stopping rule, a written statement of what “done” means, phrased around your own verified confidence rather than the tools’ agreement. Example: “I stop when I can defend each surviving claim to a hostile reviewer with a check I ran myself.” A stopping rule of “when the roles all agree” is a trap, because a team of look-alike tools can agree loudly and be wrong together.
That trap has a name you will guard against: false consensus, agreement that exists only because the tools shared a blind spot or you asked a leading question. Example: you ask three reviewers to “confirm my chapter is ready,” and all three oblige, so the chorus tells you nothing. The cure is an independence check, a step that makes agreement worth more than a head count: a check you run yourself, outside the tools.
The whole thing ends in the Evidence Defense, a short public defense of your claims at their boundaries, followed by questions you cannot rehearse for. Example: you state what you found, name exactly what you do not claim, and answer each question from your own record.

title: 37. Final Research and AI-Management Portfolio
text:
A worked example
Picture an honors project in analytical chemistry. You measured how much acetic acid a bottle of vinegar contains, using titration, a lab method where you add a solution of known strength drop by drop until a reaction just finishes, revealing the unknown amount. Three runs gave an average of 4.9% acetic acid by mass. Now you choose the claim you will defend.
You run three AI reviewer roles on your chapter draft. A reviewer role praises your headline sentence, “Vinegar contains 4.9% acetic acid.” A diagnostician role flags the same sentence for reaching past your evidence: you measured one bottle three times, not vinegar in general. That disagreement is a gift, because it points straight at the boundary decision. You take the pen back and rewrite the claim to what your data hold: “This bottle, measured three times, averaged 4.9% acetic acid, with the runs spanning 4.7% to 5.1%.” The narrower sentence is the one you will defend.
Then you run an independence check the tools cannot fake. The bottle’s own label states 5% acidity, so you compare your 4.9% against that value by hand. The two numbers land close, which is real reassurance because the label was never part of your AI workflow. Your stopping rule is now satisfied, so you stop. Into the AI-management portfolio go four things: the roles you ran, the reviewer-versus-diagnostician conflict and your override, this stopping rule, and the label comparison that made your reviewers’ agreement worth more than a head count.

title: 37. Final Research and AI-Management Portfolio
text:
The Colab laboratory
This chapter’s lab is notebook nb15 — Managing multiple AI agents + the final defense. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. There you wire a worker-critic team, run a seeded simulation showing four look-alike reviewers miss a real flaw far more often than four independent ones, then draft the four parts of your own M15 portfolio and stage your Evidence Defense.

title: 37. Final Research and AI-Management Portfolio
text:
Recommended AI prompts
Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt hands the tool reach, not the decision.
Locate the real benchmark.
Act as a research assistant in analytical chemistry. I measured the acetic acid
content of one vinegar sample. I want a published or label-stated reference value to
compare against. Give the value, the exact source it comes from, and a link. Only
state a figure you are confident is real; if you are unsure, say so and tell me where
to look.
After running, verify: open the source or read the label yourself before you trust the number (primary-source reading). Counters confident fabrication, a reference value quoted with an invented source.
List every claim to verify.
Here is my draft chapter: [paste]. List every empirical claim in it as a table: the
claim, the number or measurement it rests on, and whether that claim stays inside my
single sample or reaches past it. Do not add any new claims.
After running, verify: check the table against your own list of what you measured, and confirm nothing reaches past your data unmarked. Counters illusion of completeness, a tidy list that omits the one claim quietly overreaching.
Red-team the defense.
Act as a hostile but fair cross-examiner at a research defense. Here is my headline
claim, its boundary, and my two weakest verification steps: [paste]. Ask me the five
hardest questions you can, each tied to a specific claim or check. Do not soften them,
and do not answer them for me.
After running, verify: if every question is gentle or your claim is called “well-supported,” push back and demand the weakest link. Counters sycophantic agreement, a partner that rehearses your ego instead of your defense.






ImportantDo not delegate



The tools may draft, review, and surface hard questions. They may never decide which claims you put your name on, where a claim like “4.9% acetic acid” must stop, whether your reviewers were ever independent, what your stopping rule is, or a single word of the public defense. When agreeing roles bless a claim, deciding whether that agreement is real or just correlated is yours. So is the answer you give when the room asks how you know.

title: 37. Final Research and AI-Management Portfolio
text:
An AI failure case
You send your finished chapter to three reviewer roles and ask each to “confirm it is ready to submit.” All three return the same verdict: “Looks solid, no major problems.” It feels like three confirmations. It is one. The three roles run on the same base model, you gave them the same leading prompt, and they share a blind spot: none flags that your headline number carries no uncertainty, no range across your titration runs. That is false consensus, and trusting it would send an overclaimed result into your defense.
You catch it with a reframe and a check the tools cannot see. First you drop the leading ask and run a neutral one: “List the three weakest claims in this chapter and why.” Now a role names the missing range. Then you leave the tools entirely and recompute the spread of your runs yourself, which hands you the 4.7% to 5.1% boundary the reviewers all skipped. The lesson is blunt: agreement is worth only the independence behind it. Count the checks you ran yourself, never the roles that nodded.

title: 37. Final Research and AI-Management Portfolio
text:
Verification laboratory
Verify this chapter’s key result, that a claim in your portfolio is one you can defend, with benchmark result, the method of comparing your output against a known-good reference value (see ai_resources/verification_guide.md). Set your measured 4.9% against the label’s stated 5% acidity and confirm they agree within your run-to-run spread. Because the label was never part of your AI workflow, this is a genuine independence check, not one tool grading another. Pair it with peer reasoning: walk your claim boundary past a classmate and see whether your reason for stopping at “this bottle” survives their first question. Two independent checks beat one, and each leaves a ledger line.

title: 37. Final Research and AI-Management Portfolio
text:
Project transfer
This last chapter delivers milestone M15: your final research chapter, your AI-management portfolio, and the Evidence Defense you perform this week. Bring four assembled parts and one staged defense. The four parts are the decomposition of AI roles you ran on your chapter, one real conflict-and-override with its non-AI check, your stopping rule and never-automate list, and one independence check that made an agreement worth more than a head count. The defense is three movements in order: your claim and its boundary, your central choice and the road you did not take, and the verification you lead with. Draft every piece yourself. A tool may critique it after, but the chapter and the defense you deliver have to be reasoned and owned by you.

title: 37. Final Research and AI-Management Portfolio
text:
Defend your decision
In five or six sentences, close the course. State one claim from your own project that you will put your name on in the room. Give its boundary: name what your evidence does not establish, and if the claim rests on one sample, say so. Name one thing still uncertain and why. Then show your AI-management portfolio doing its job in one honest line: the place your roles agreed, the independent check that made the agreement real, and the one decision you refused to hand to any tool. That last line is what the examiner is listening for.
